definition: An entity is a substance if and only if (i) it is a particular, (ii) it is capable of independent existence, and (iii) it persists through change in its properties.
conditions: is a particular
capable of independent existence
persists through change in its properties
counterexample: A shadow cast by a tree upon a wall is a particular — it has a determinate shape and location — and it persists through changes in its properties, growing longer as the sun descends and shorter as the sun ascends. Yet a shadow is not capable of independent existence in any robust sense; it depends entirely upon the tree, the light source, and the surface upon which it falls. The second condition fails, and rightly so, for a shadow is no substance. But consider now a wave upon the ocean. It is a particular, it persists through changes in its properties — varying in height, speed, and shape as it traverses the sea — and it appears to enjoy independent existence, moving freely across the water. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet a wave is not a substance; it is a disturbance propagated through a medium, having no matter of its own.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the entity possess its own constituent matter or intrinsic principle of unity; a wave satisfies all three conditions yet is merely a pattern of motion in a medium, not a substance in any philosophically serious sense.
passage: It is tempting to define substance as follows: an entity is a substance if and only if it is a particular, it is capable of independent existence, and it persists through change in its properties. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases — a horse, a stone, a man — each of which is a particular thing, exists independently of other particulars, and endures through alteration of its accidental properties. Yet the definition admits cases that one should hesitate to call substances. Consider a wave upon the open ocean. It is a particular: one may point to it, track its progress, and distinguish it from neighbouring waves. It persists through changes in its properties, for as it moves across the sea its height increases and diminishes, its speed varies with the depth of the water, and its shape alters as it encounters currents and obstacles. And it appears to enjoy independent existence, travelling freely for hundreds of miles without dependence upon any other particular entity. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet a wave is not a substance. It is a disturbance propagated through a medium; the water through which it passes does not travel with it, and the wave possesses no matter of its own. The definition has failed to require that the entity possess its own constituent matter or intrinsic principle of unity, and so it admits what is merely a pattern of motion as though it were a genuine substance.

definition: An entity is a substance if and only if (i) it is the bearer of properties, (ii) it is not itself a property of anything, and (iii) it is spatiotemporally located.
conditions: bearer of properties
not itself a property of anything
spatiotemporally located
counterexample: Consider the number seven as conceived by the Platonist. It is the bearer of properties — it is odd, it is prime, it is greater than five. It is not itself a property of anything; it is an object, not an attribute. Yet it is not spatiotemporally located; it exists, if it exists at all, outside space and time. The third condition fails. If one holds, as many philosophers have held, that numbers are substances in the sense of being self-subsistent objects, then the definition is too narrow.
missing_condition: The definition restricts substance to the spatiotemporal, but if abstract objects such as numbers are admitted as genuine entities — as Platonists maintain — then the requirement of spatiotemporal location excludes a class of objects that have as strong a claim to substantiality as any concrete particular.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a substance if and only if it is the bearer of properties, it is not itself a property of anything, and it is spatiotemporally located. The definition is attractive in its economy: it distinguishes substances from their properties, from relations, and from abstract entities at a single stroke. A horse bears properties — colour, shape, mass — is not a property of anything else, and occupies a definite region of space at each moment of its existence. The definition captures such cases admirably. Yet it encounters a difficulty when one considers the objects of mathematics. Take the number seven. It is the bearer of properties: it is odd, it is prime, it is the sum of three and four. It is not itself a property of anything; the Platonist regards it as an object in its own right, not as an attribute predicated of collections. But it is not spatiotemporally located. The number seven does not exist in London or in the year 1927; it exists, if it exists at all, outside space and time altogether. The third condition fails. If one holds, as a great many philosophers from Plato onwards have held, that numbers are self-subsistent objects — substances in the fullest sense — then the definition is too narrow. It has mistaken a feature of concrete substances for an essential feature of substance as such.

definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C is a sufficient condition for E, (ii) C temporally precedes E, and (iii) there is no further event D such that D is sufficient for E and D is temporally between C and E.
conditions: C is sufficient condition for E
C temporally precedes E
no intervening event D sufficient for E between C and E
counterexample: A man strikes a match in a room filled with oxygen. The striking is sufficient for the lighting, it precedes the lighting, and there is no intervening sufficient condition. But now suppose a second man simultaneously holds a lit taper beside the match head. The striking is still sufficient, the taper is also sufficient, and neither is temporally between the other and the effect. The definition cannot adjudicate which is the cause, yet both are; we have a case of causal overdetermination that the definition cannot accommodate.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that causation is a relation between a single sufficient condition and its effect, but cases of overdetermination show that multiple independently sufficient conditions may jointly or severally produce the same effect, and the definition has no resources to handle such multiplicity.
passage: One might define causation as follows: an event C is the cause of an event E if and only if C is a sufficient condition for E, C temporally precedes E, and there is no further event D such that D is sufficient for E and temporally falls between C and E. The third clause is intended to screen off remote causes in favour of proximate ones. This definition works tolerably well for simple causal chains: the spark causes the explosion, the virus causes the illness, the frost causes the pipe to burst. Yet it founders upon cases of causal overdetermination. Suppose a man strikes a match in a room filled with oxygen, and at the very same instant a second man holds a lit taper to the match head. Either event alone would have been sufficient to light the match. The striking temporally precedes the lighting, and so does the application of the taper; neither is temporally between the other and the effect. The definition cannot say which event is the cause, for both satisfy all three conditions equally. Nor can it say that both are causes, for it is framed in terms of a single sufficient condition. Cases of this sort — where two independent causes each suffice for the effect — reveal that the definition has built into itself an assumption of causal uniqueness that the world does not always respect.

definition: A duration is a period of time if and only if (i) it is measurable by a clock, (ii) it is occupied by events or processes, and (iii) it is part of a single linear temporal order.
conditions: measurable by a clock
occupied by events or processes
part of a single linear temporal order
counterexample: Consider a period of dreamless sleep. From the standpoint of the sleeper, the duration is not occupied by any events or processes of which she is aware; it is, experientially, a blank. Yet it is plainly a period of time — eight hours pass, the clock advances, and the world ages. The second condition, if construed subjectively, fails; if construed objectively, it is satisfied only by appeal to events external to the sleeper. The definition equivocates on what it means for a duration to be 'occupied' by events.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the passage of time with the occurrence of experienced events; a period of time may elapse without any events of which a given subject is aware, and the definition must either relativise 'occupation' to a perspective or abandon the requirement altogether.
passage: One might propose that a duration is a period of time if and only if it is measurable by a clock, it is occupied by events or processes, and it is part of a single linear temporal order. This definition captures the ordinary understanding of temporal duration: the hour of a lecture, the day of labour, the year of study — each is measurable, each is filled with events, and each takes its place in the continuous succession of moments that constitutes the temporal order. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with dreamless sleep. A woman retires at eleven o'clock and wakes at seven; eight hours have elapsed. The clock has measured the interval, and the interval is part of the linear temporal order. But from the standpoint of the sleeper, the duration is not occupied by any events or processes. There are no experiences, no thoughts, no dreams — nothing whatever fills the interval. It is, from her perspective, as though the eight hours did not exist. If the second condition is construed subjectively, it fails. If it is construed objectively — the heart beats, the blood circulates, the earth rotates — then it is satisfied, but only by shifting the meaning of 'occupied' from the events of the subject's experience to events external to or beneath her awareness. The definition, it appears, equivocates on a crucial term.

definition: An instant is a moment of time if and only if (i) it has no temporal duration, (ii) it divides the temporal continuum into a past and a future, and (iii) some event occurs at it.
conditions: has no temporal duration
divides temporal continuum into past and future
some event occurs at it
counterexample: Consider a moment at which nothing whatever happens — no event occurs, no state changes, no process is initiated or completed. Such a moment has no temporal duration and divides the continuum into past and future, but the third condition fails. Yet it is a moment of time for all that; the mere absence of events at an instant does not annihilate the instant itself.
missing_condition: The definition makes the existence of a temporal instant contingent upon the occurrence of an event at that instant, but time may pass without events, and the instant exists in virtue of its position in the temporal order, not in virtue of its being occupied.
passage: One might define a moment of time as an instant that has no temporal duration, divides the temporal continuum into a past and a future, and is such that some event occurs at it. The first two conditions are structural: they characterise the moment as a durationless point that separates what has been from what is to come. The third condition ties the moment to the world of events — it requires that something happen at the instant in question. This seems reasonable enough, for we ordinarily identify moments by the events that occur at them: the moment of the explosion, the moment of the eclipse, the moment at which the train departed. Yet the definition proves too restrictive. Consider a moment at which nothing whatever happens. No event occurs, no state changes, no process begins or ends. The universe is, at that instant, entirely quiescent. Such a moment has no temporal duration and divides the continuum into past and future; the first two conditions are satisfied. But the third condition fails, for no event occurs at the instant. The definition would deny that it is a moment of time at all. Yet this is plainly wrong. The instant exists; it occupies its position in the temporal order between two neighbouring instants. Time does not cease to flow merely because nothing happens. The definition has confused the existence of an instant with the occurrence of an event at that instant.

definition: A region is a portion of space if and only if (i) it is three-dimensional, (ii) it is capable of being occupied by a material body, and (iii) it stands in distance relations to other regions.
conditions: three-dimensional
capable of being occupied by a material body
stands in distance relations to other regions
counterexample: The interior of a proton is, by the best physical theories, a region of space; it is three-dimensional and stands in distance relations to other regions. Yet it is not capable of being occupied by a material body in any ordinary sense, for no macroscopic body could be placed there, and even subatomic particles within it are governed by quantum confinement. The second condition fails if 'material body' is understood in its familiar sense, yet the region is unquestionably a portion of space.
missing_condition: The definition ties the concept of space to the possibility of occupation by material bodies of a familiar sort, but there are regions of space — at subatomic scales or within the interiors of fundamental particles — where no such occupation is possible, yet which are regions of space nonetheless.
passage: It is natural to define a portion of space as a region that is three-dimensional, that is capable of being occupied by a material body, and that stands in distance relations to other regions. This definition captures the space of ordinary experience: the room in which one sits, the field through which one walks, the volume of a container into which one pours water. Each is three-dimensional, each can be occupied by a body, and each stands at determinate distances from other regions. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty at very small scales. Consider the interior of a proton. It is, by the best physical theories, a region of space; it has three spatial dimensions, and it stands in determinate distance relations to the regions surrounding it. But it is not capable of being occupied by a material body in any ordinary sense of the phrase. No macroscopic body could be placed within a proton; the very notion is incoherent. Even the quarks and gluons within the proton are subject to quantum confinement and cannot be said to 'occupy' the region in the way that a stone occupies a hole. If the second condition is interpreted literally, it fails. The definition has taken a feature of space at the macroscopic scale — its capacity to be filled by material bodies — and treated it as an essential feature of space as such, when in truth space extends to scales at which no such filling is possible.

definition: A void is an empty space if and only if (i) it contains no material body, (ii) it is bounded by material bodies, and (iii) it is of finite extent.
conditions: contains no material body
bounded by material bodies
of finite extent
counterexample: The intergalactic void between galaxy clusters contains no material body — or, at most, a scattering of stray atoms per cubic metre — yet it is not bounded by material bodies in any meaningful sense: the galaxy clusters at its margins are themselves separated by immense further voids. Moreover, such a void may, for all we know, extend without finite bound. The second and third conditions fail, yet the intergalactic void is as genuine an instance of empty space as any vacuum flask.
missing_condition: The definition models empty space on enclosed terrestrial cavities — the evacuated flask, the sealed chamber — and fails to accommodate the unbounded, open voids of cosmological space, where emptiness is not enclosed by matter but is the prevailing condition of the universe.
passage: One might define an empty space as a void that contains no material body, is bounded by material bodies, and is of finite extent. This captures the laboratory vacuum and the sealed chamber: each is devoid of matter, each is enclosed by material walls, and each has a definite finite volume. Yet the definition proves far too narrow when one considers the voids of cosmic space. The regions between galaxy clusters are, to a very close approximation, devoid of matter — a handful of stray hydrogen atoms per cubic metre, nothing more. They are empty spaces in the most dramatic sense imaginable. But they are not bounded by material bodies. The galaxy clusters that lie at their margins are themselves separated by further voids, and the boundaries, if any can be discerned, are diffuse and arbitrary. Nor are these voids of finite extent in any clearly determinable way; some may stretch for hundreds of millions of light-years. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has modelled empty space upon enclosed terrestrial cavities and has thereby excluded the greatest empty spaces of all — the cosmological voids in which most of the volume of the universe consists. What is essential to empty space is the absence of matter, not the presence of material boundaries or the finitude of extent.

definition: Entity A is identical with entity B if and only if (i) A and B share all their properties, (ii) A and B occupy the same spatiotemporal region, and (iii) A and B are of the same kind.
conditions: share all properties
occupy same spatiotemporal region
of the same kind
counterexample: A statue and the lump of bronze from which it is made occupy the same spatiotemporal region and share all their intrinsic physical properties — mass, temperature, chemical composition. Yet they are not of the same kind: the statue is a work of art with aesthetic properties and a history of creation, whilst the lump of bronze is merely a quantity of metal. The third condition suggests they are not identical. But if they are not identical, we have two distinct objects occupying the very same region at the very same time, which offends against the principle that no two objects of the same sort can be co-located.
missing_condition: The definition adds a same-kind requirement that generates a dilemma: if the statue and the bronze are of different kinds, we must accept co-location of distinct objects; if we insist on identity, we must abandon the kind requirement. The definition cannot accommodate the constitutive relation between an object and its matter.
passage: One might propose that entity A is identical with entity B if and only if A and B share all their properties, A and B occupy the same spatiotemporal region, and A and B are of the same kind. The definition is intended to strengthen Leibniz's law with two further conditions: spatial coincidence and sortal agreement. It handles the straightforward cases well enough — the Morning Star and the Evening Star share all properties, occupy the same region, and are of the same kind, namely celestial bodies, and so are identical. Yet the definition encounters a celebrated difficulty with the statue and its matter. Consider a statue of Athena cast in bronze. The statue and the lump of bronze occupy precisely the same spatiotemporal region; every point in space occupied by the one is occupied by the other. They share all their intrinsic physical properties: mass, density, temperature, chemical composition. But they are not of the same kind. The statue is a work of art; it has aesthetic properties, a title, and a history of artistic creation. The lump of bronze is merely a quantity of metal; it has none of these features. The third condition fails, and the definition pronounces them non-identical. But then we appear to have two distinct entities occupying the very same region of space at the very same time, which many philosophers find deeply objectionable. The definition has generated a puzzle rather than resolved one.

definition: An object at time t1 is identical with an object at time t2 if and only if (i) the two objects are composed of the same matter, (ii) they are spatiotemporally continuous, and (iii) they are of the same general form.
conditions: composed of the same matter
spatiotemporally continuous
of the same general form
counterexample: A human being at the age of seven and the same human being at the age of seventy are, by common consent, one and the same person. They are spatiotemporally continuous and of the same general form. But they are not composed of the same matter; the matter of the human body is replaced many times over in the course of a lifetime through metabolism. The first condition fails, yet identity plainly holds.
missing_condition: The definition makes material identity a necessary condition for diachronic identity, but living organisms replace their matter continuously while preserving their identity; what is essential is the continuity of the organising principle or life process, not the persistence of particular material constituents.
passage: One might hold that an object at one time is identical with an object at a later time if and only if the two are composed of the same matter, they are spatiotemporally continuous, and they are of the same general form. This definition works well for inert objects: the stone in the garden today is the same stone that was there a year ago, for it is composed of the same minerals, it has occupied a continuous path through space and time, and it retains the same general form. Yet the definition fails conspicuously for living things. Consider a man at the age of seven and the very same man at the age of seventy. They are, by common consent, one and the same person — the same individual, with a continuous biography, a continuous consciousness, and a continuous bodily existence. They are spatiotemporally continuous, and they are of the same general form — both are human bodies, bipedal and upright. But they are not composed of the same matter. The matter of a human body is replaced many times over during a lifetime through the processes of metabolism; the atoms that composed the child are long since dispersed. The first condition fails entirely. The definition has taken a feature of inanimate persistence — material constancy — and elevated it to a necessary condition of identity, when in fact living beings preserve their identity through a continuity of organising form and vital process, not through retention of particular material constituents.

definition: A universal is an entity that (i) is predicable of many particulars, (ii) is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) is mind-independent.
conditions: predicable of many particulars
wholly present in each instance
mind-independent
counterexample: Consider the property of being amusing. It is predicable of many particulars — this joke, that anecdote, a certain situation — and one might suppose it is wholly present in each amusing thing. But it is not mind-independent; whether something is amusing depends constitutively on the responses of minded creatures. Without minds to be amused, nothing would be amusing. The third condition fails, yet 'amusing' functions grammatically and logically as a universal — it is predicated of many things in just the way that 'red' or 'square' is.
missing_condition: The definition requires mind-independence, but many properties that function as universals — the amusing, the boring, the frightening — are constitutively dependent on the existence of minded subjects, and to exclude them is to restrict the class of universals in a way that the logical role of predication does not warrant.
passage: One might define a universal as an entity that is predicable of many particulars, is wholly present in each of its instances, and is mind-independent. The first condition captures the classical idea that a universal is a one-over-many; the second ensures that the universal is not divided or distributed among its instances but is fully present in each; the third secures the objectivity of the universal, its independence from any mind that might contemplate it. This definition captures the properties beloved of the realist: redness, squareness, mass, charge. Each is predicable of many things, each is wholly present wherever it is instantiated, and each exists independently of whether anyone thinks about it. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with response-dependent properties. Consider the property of being amusing. It is predicable of many particulars: this joke is amusing, that anecdote is amusing, a certain concatenation of circumstances is amusing. One might grant, for the sake of argument, that it is wholly present in each amusing thing. But it is not mind-independent. Whether something is amusing depends constitutively on the existence of minds capable of being amused; in a universe without such minds, nothing would be amusing. The third condition fails. Yet 'amusing' functions as a universal in every logical and grammatical respect — it is predicated of many things, it admits of degrees, it supports generalisations. The definition has confused the metaphysical status of certain paradigmatic universals with the logical role that all universals play.

definition: A universal is an entity that (i) can be instantiated, (ii) is abstract, and (iii) is such that its existence does not depend upon the existence of any particular instance.
conditions: can be instantiated
is abstract
existence does not depend on any particular instance
counterexample: Consider the universal 'being a dodo.' It can be instantiated — there were once many dodos — and it is abstract. But since the extinction of the dodo, there are no instances. If one maintains, as the definition suggests, that the universal's existence does not depend on any particular instance, then the universal 'being a dodo' still exists even though nothing instantiates it. This is coherent but controversial. More troublingly, consider the property 'being a round square.' It can be characterised and discussed, but it cannot be instantiated, for it is logically impossible for anything to be both round and square. The first condition fails, yet one might argue it is an entity of the same logical type as other universals.
missing_condition: The definition requires instantiability, which excludes impossible properties such as being a round square; yet these impossible properties behave logically like other universals — they can be discussed, negated, and combined — and their exclusion is a substantive metaphysical commitment disguised as a definitional requirement.
passage: One might define a universal as an entity that can be instantiated, is abstract, and is such that its existence does not depend upon the existence of any particular instance. The first condition requires that the entity be the sort of thing that could have instances; the second locates it in the abstract realm; the third secures its independence from the contingent existence of any given particular. This definition captures the standard cases: redness can be instantiated by red things, triangularity by triangles, humanity by human beings. Each is abstract, and each would exist even if its instances were destroyed. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with impossible properties. Consider the property of being a round square. It is abstract, and its existence presumably does not depend upon any particular instance, for it has no instances and never could have. But it cannot be instantiated, for it is logically impossible for anything to be both round and square simultaneously. The first condition fails. Yet the property 'being a round square' is discussed in philosophy, it can be negated, combined with other properties, and subjected to logical operations. It behaves, in all formal respects, as a universal. The definition has built into itself a substantive metaphysical commitment — that universals must be possibly instantiated — which excludes an entire class of entities that one might reasonably wish to count among the universals.

definition: A state of affairs is possible if and only if (i) it does not involve a logical contradiction, (ii) it is compatible with the laws of nature, and (iii) there exist or have existed conditions from which it could arise.
conditions: does not involve logical contradiction
compatible with laws of nature
conditions exist or have existed from which it could arise
counterexample: It is logically consistent and compatible with the laws of nature for there to exist a planet composed entirely of gold. Yet there do not exist and have never existed conditions from which such a planet could arise, given the actual distribution of elements in the universe. The third condition fails, yet we should not hesitate to say that a golden planet is possible — it is a genuine physical possibility, merely one that the contingent initial conditions of our universe happen not to have realised.
missing_condition: The definition conflates possibility with historical realisability; a state of affairs may be possible in virtue of its consistency with logic and natural law without there ever having been conditions favourable to its occurrence. Possibility outruns what the actual history of the universe can produce.
passage: It is tempting to define possibility as follows: a state of affairs is possible if and only if it does not involve a logical contradiction, it is compatible with the laws of nature, and there exist or have existed conditions from which it could arise. The definition captures a reasonable range of cases. It is possible that a particular atom of radium will decay within the next hour, for this involves no contradiction, is compatible with natural law, and the conditions for its occurrence are already in place. It is possible that a volcano will erupt in Iceland next year, for the same reasons. Yet the definition proves too restrictive. Consider a planet composed entirely of gold. Such a planet involves no logical contradiction — there is no absurdity in the idea. It is compatible with the laws of nature — nothing in physics forbids a sphere of gold. But there do not exist, and there have never existed, conditions from which a golden planet could actually arise. The processes of stellar nucleosynthesis and planetary accretion do not permit the concentration of gold into a planetary body. The third condition fails. Yet we should hesitate to deny that a golden planet is possible; it is a genuine physical possibility that the contingent initial conditions of our universe have simply failed to realise. The definition has confused what is possible with what is historically available, and these are not the same.

definition: A proposition is necessary if and only if (i) its negation involves a contradiction, (ii) it is knowable a priori, and (iii) it is analytic.
conditions: negation involves a contradiction
knowable a priori
analytic
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H2O. It appears to be necessary — water could not have been some other substance — yet it is neither knowable a priori nor analytic. It was discovered through empirical chemistry, and it cannot be derived from the meanings of 'water' and 'H2O' alone. The second and third conditions fail, yet the proposition is, by widespread philosophical agreement, necessary.
missing_condition: The definition identifies necessity with analyticity and a priori knowability, but there are necessary truths — such as theoretical identities discovered by empirical science — that are synthetic and knowable only a posteriori. Necessity outruns the analytic-a priori.
passage: One might define a necessary proposition as one whose negation involves a contradiction, that is knowable a priori, and that is analytic. This definition captures the paradigmatic necessary truths: 'All bachelors are unmarried,' 'No triangle has four sides,' 'If p and q, then p.' Each is such that its denial leads to contradiction, each is knowable without recourse to experience, and each is true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms. Yet the definition encounters a celebrated difficulty with certain propositions that appear to be necessary but are neither a priori nor analytic. Consider the proposition that water is H2O. If water is indeed H2O — if the chemical kind water is identical with the compound H2O — then this identity is necessary: water could not have been some other substance. Its negation, 'water is not H2O,' describes a metaphysical impossibility. Yet the proposition is not knowable a priori; it was discovered through painstaking empirical investigation by Cavendish and others. And it is not analytic; 'water' and 'H2O' are not synonymous, and the proposition cannot be derived from the meanings of the terms alone. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has assumed that all necessary truths are analytic and a priori, but if there are necessary a posteriori truths — as a great many philosophers now maintain — then the definition is too narrow.

definition: An entity undergoes change if and only if (i) it possesses a property at one time that it lacks at another, (ii) it remains the same entity throughout, and (iii) the alteration is caused by some external force or influence.
conditions: possesses property at one time that it lacks at another
remains same entity throughout
alteration caused by external force or influence
counterexample: A radioactive atom decays spontaneously, transmuting from one element into another. It possesses a property at one time — being an atom of radium, say — that it lacks at a later time, when it has become an atom of radon. One may grant, with some strain, that it remains the same entity. But the decay is not caused by any external force or influence; it is a spontaneous, stochastic event arising from the internal instability of the nucleus. The third condition fails, yet the atom has unquestionably changed.
missing_condition: The definition requires an external cause for every change, but some changes — radioactive decay, the spontaneous emission of a photon — arise from the intrinsic nature of the entity itself, without any external stimulus. Change does not require an external mover.
passage: One might propose that an entity undergoes change if and only if it possesses a property at one time that it lacks at another, it remains the same entity throughout the process, and the alteration is caused by some external force or influence. The definition captures a wide range of familiar changes: the leaf changes colour because of the seasonal reduction in sunlight, the iron rusts because of exposure to moisture, the ball moves because it is struck. In each case the change is produced by an identifiable external cause. Yet the definition proves too restrictive when one considers spontaneous physical processes. A radioactive atom — an atom of radium, say — decays spontaneously, emitting a particle and transmuting into an atom of radon. It possesses a property at one time (being radium) that it lacks at a later time (when it has become radon). One may grant that it remains, in some attenuated sense, the same entity. But the decay is not caused by any external force or influence. It is a spontaneous event, governed by quantum-mechanical probabilities, arising from the internal instability of the nucleus. No external agent acts upon the atom; nothing pushes it or compels it to decay. The third condition fails. Yet the atom has changed — it has undergone a profound alteration, transforming from one element into another. The definition has generalised from cases of externally caused change and has thereby excluded a fundamental class of natural changes that proceed from the intrinsic nature of the thing itself.

definition: An object persists through an interval of time if and only if (i) it exists at the beginning of the interval, (ii) it exists at the end of the interval, and (iii) it exists at every moment within the interval.
conditions: exists at beginning of interval
exists at end of interval
exists at every moment within interval
counterexample: A watch is disassembled by a watchmaker for cleaning. At time t1 it exists as a watch; at time t2 it is in pieces upon the bench; at time t3 it is reassembled and exists again as a watch. If the watch ceases to exist as a watch during the interval of disassembly — and it is natural to say that a scattered heap of gears and springs is not a watch — then the third condition fails: the watch does not exist at every moment within the interval. Yet we say, quite naturally, that it is the same watch throughout, and that it has persisted.
missing_condition: The definition requires continuous existence at every moment, but objects may persist through intervals during which they temporarily cease to exist in their characteristic form, provided they are later reconstituted from the same parts according to the same plan. Persistence, it appears, may tolerate gaps.
passage: A natural definition of persistence holds that an object persists through an interval of time if and only if it exists at the beginning of the interval, it exists at the end of the interval, and it exists at every moment within the interval. This captures the ordinary cases: a stone persists from morning to evening, existing continuously throughout the day; a tree persists from spring to autumn, present at every moment of its growth. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with objects that are temporarily disassembled. Consider a watch brought to a watchmaker for cleaning. At time t1, before the watchmaker begins, the watch exists: it is a functioning timepiece composed of gears, springs, hands, and a case, all assembled in the proper arrangement. The watchmaker then takes the watch apart, laying each component upon the bench. At time t2, the watch — considered as an assembled, functioning object — does not exist. What exists is a collection of separated parts. At time t3, the watchmaker reassembles the parts, and the watch exists again. The first and second conditions are satisfied: the watch exists at the beginning and at the end. But the third condition fails, for the watch does not exist at every moment within the interval — at t2 it is merely a heap of components. Yet we say, quite naturally and without any sense of paradox, that it is the same watch, that it has persisted through the interval. The definition cannot accommodate this ordinary judgement.

definition: An entity is a part of a whole if and only if (i) the destruction of the entity would diminish the whole, (ii) the entity is spatially contained within the whole, and (iii) the entity contributes to the function of the whole.
conditions: destruction would diminish the whole
spatially contained within the whole
contributes to function of the whole
counterexample: The human appendix is spatially contained within the body, and its destruction would, in a trivial sense, diminish the whole. But it does not contribute to the function of the whole; it is, so far as current medicine can determine, a vestigial organ with no significant function. The third condition fails, yet the appendix is unquestionably a part of the human body.
missing_condition: The definition requires functional contribution, but many parts — vestigial organs, decorative elements, residual components — contribute nothing to the function of the whole and are parts nonetheless. Parthood is a mereological relation, not a functional one.
passage: One might define parthood as follows: an entity is a part of a whole if and only if the destruction of the entity would diminish the whole, the entity is spatially contained within the whole, and the entity contributes to the function of the whole. This definition captures the paradigm cases of parthood in artifacts and organisms: the engine is a part of the motor-car, for its removal would diminish the car, it is contained within the car, and it contributes to the car's function of locomotion. The heart is a part of the body, for the same three reasons. Yet the definition proves too restrictive when one considers vestigial components. The human appendix is spatially contained within the body, and its removal would, in a minimal sense, diminish the body — some tissue would be lost. But it does not contribute to the function of the body. The appendix is, so far as current medical science can determine, a vestigial organ; it performs no significant physiological function, and its removal leaves the body entirely unimpaired. The third condition fails. Yet the appendix is plainly a part of the human body: it is made of the body's tissue, it is connected to the body's organs, and it develops as part of the body in embryogenesis. The definition has confused the mereological relation of parthood with the distinct notion of functional contribution, and these are not the same.

definition: A collection of objects forms a whole if and only if (i) the objects are in spatial contact with one another, (ii) the objects are causally integrated, and (iii) the objects are unified by a common purpose or function.
conditions: objects in spatial contact with one another
objects causally integrated
objects unified by common purpose or function
counterexample: Consider the solar system. The sun, planets, moons, and asteroids form a whole — the solar system — that is recognised by astronomers and laymen alike. The objects are causally integrated through gravitational interactions, and one might ascribe to them a common character, if not a common purpose. But they are not in spatial contact with one another; they are separated by millions of miles of empty space. The first condition fails, yet the solar system is a whole.
missing_condition: The definition requires spatial contact, but some wholes — the solar system, a flock of migrating birds, an army deployed across a continent — are composed of spatially separated parts held together by causal or organisational relations rather than by physical contact.
passage: It is tempting to suppose that a collection of objects forms a whole if and only if the objects are in spatial contact with one another, the objects are causally integrated, and the objects are unified by a common purpose or function. This definition captures a good many ordinary wholes: the bricks of a wall are in contact, causally integrated through mortar and gravity, and serve the common purpose of enclosure. The organs of a body are in contact, causally integrated through physiological processes, and serve the common purpose of sustaining the life of the organism. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider the solar system. It is, by common acknowledgement, a whole — a system of bodies bound together and recognised as a single entity by astronomers and laymen alike. The sun, planets, moons, and asteroids are causally integrated through gravitational interactions of extraordinary precision. One might even attribute to them a common character, though 'purpose' is perhaps too strong a word for a natural system. But the objects are not in spatial contact with one another. The earth is ninety-three million miles from the sun; Jupiter is yet farther. There is no physical touching, no contiguity of surfaces. The first condition fails entirely. The definition has taken a feature of compact terrestrial wholes — the mutual contact of their parts — and required it of all wholes, when in fact some of the most important wholes in nature are composed of spatially separated parts held together by forces that act across distances.

definition: A proposition has modal status if and only if (i) it can be classified as necessary or contingent, (ii) its modal classification is determinable by rational reflection alone, and (iii) its modal status is invariant across all contexts of utterance.
conditions: classifiable as necessary or contingent
modal classification determinable by rational reflection alone
modal status invariant across all contexts
counterexample: The proposition 'there are eight planets in the solar system' can be classified as contingent — there might have been nine, or seven. But its modal classification is not determinable by rational reflection alone; one must know the empirical facts about the solar system to determine that the number eight is contingent rather than, say, somehow necessitated by the laws of gravitation. The second condition fails, yet the proposition plainly has modal status — it is contingent.
missing_condition: The definition requires that modal classification be achievable by pure reflection, but for many propositions — especially those concerning the natural world — modal status can be determined only with the aid of empirical knowledge. The definition conflates the metaphysical question of what is necessary or contingent with the methodological question of how we come to know it.
passage: One might define modal status as follows: a proposition has modal status if and only if it can be classified as necessary or contingent, its modal classification is determinable by rational reflection alone, and its modal status is invariant across all contexts of utterance. The first condition is straightforward: to have modal status is to be the sort of proposition that admits of being necessary or contingent. The second restricts the determination of modality to the a priori. The third ensures objectivity. The definition captures the familiar cases of logical and mathematical necessity: that two and two are four, that no bachelor is married, that every triangle has three sides. In each case the modal status is determinable by reflection alone, and it does not vary with context. Yet the definition proves too restrictive for a great many propositions whose modal status is uncontroversial. Consider the proposition that there are eight planets in the solar system. It is contingent — the number of planets might have been different. But its contingency is not determinable by rational reflection alone. One must know the empirical facts about the formation of the solar system, the nature of planetary accretion, and the contingency of initial conditions to see that eight is not a necessary number. The second condition fails. Yet no one would deny that the proposition has modal status. The definition has confined modal knowledge to the a priori and thereby excluded a vast domain of modal truths that are accessible only through the cooperation of reason and experience.

definition: A state of affairs is contingent if and only if (i) it obtains but might not have obtained, (ii) its obtaining depends upon particular causes, and (iii) its non-obtaining would be compatible with the same laws of nature.
conditions: obtains but might not have obtained
obtaining depends upon particular causes
non-obtaining compatible with same laws of nature
counterexample: Consider the fact that the mass of the electron is approximately 9.109 x 10^-31 kilograms. It obtains, and many physicists believe it might not have obtained — the fundamental constants might have been different. Its non-obtaining would be compatible with the laws of nature if those laws are construed as structural equations that could take different parameter values. But its obtaining does not depend upon particular causes; the electron mass is not the effect of any causal process but is, so far as we know, a brute fact about the universe. The second condition fails, yet the electron mass is, if anything is, contingent.
missing_condition: The definition requires that contingent states of affairs be causally produced, but some contingencies — the values of fundamental constants, the initial conditions of the universe — are brute contingencies that do not arise from any causal process. The definition has conflated contingency with causal dependence.
passage: One might define contingency thus: a state of affairs is contingent if and only if it obtains but might not have obtained, its obtaining depends upon particular causes, and its non-obtaining would be compatible with the same laws of nature. This captures the ordinary cases of contingency admirably. It is contingent that the cat is on the mat — the cat's position depends upon particular causes (its decisions, its movements), and the cat might equally well have been elsewhere without any violation of natural law. It is contingent that the battle of Waterloo was fought on the eighteenth of June — the date depended upon particular military decisions, and a different date would have been compatible with the laws of nature. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty with fundamental physical constants. Consider the mass of the electron, approximately 9.109 times ten to the minus thirty-one kilograms. Many physicists believe this value is contingent — the fundamental constants might have been different, and a universe with a different electron mass is at least conceivable. Its non-obtaining would be compatible with the laws of nature, if those laws are understood as structural equations that could accept different parameter values. But the electron mass does not depend upon particular causes. It is not the effect of any causal process; no chain of events produced the electron mass. It is, so far as we know, a brute fact about our universe. The second condition fails, yet the electron mass is contingent if anything is.

definition: A truth is metaphysically necessary if and only if (i) it holds in every conceivable circumstance, (ii) it is not dependent upon the existence of any particular object, and (iii) it is expressible as a tautology of some formal language.
conditions: holds in every conceivable circumstance
not dependent on existence of any particular object
expressible as tautology of some formal language
counterexample: The proposition 'Hesperus is Phosphorus,' if true, is held by many to be metaphysically necessary: if the Morning Star is identical with the Evening Star, then it could not have failed to be. It holds in every conceivable circumstance (given the identity). But it is dependent upon the existence of a particular object — the planet Venus — and it is not expressible as a tautology in any standard formal language, since 'Hesperus' and 'Phosphorus' are not logically equivalent terms. The second and third conditions fail, yet the proposition is a paradigmatic necessary truth.
missing_condition: The definition restricts necessity to purely general, logically tautologous truths, but necessary truths may involve particular objects (as in identity statements) and may not be tautological in form. De re necessities — necessities about particular things — are excluded by a definition calibrated only to de dicto logical necessities.
passage: One might propose that a truth is metaphysically necessary if and only if it holds in every conceivable circumstance, it is not dependent upon the existence of any particular object, and it is expressible as a tautology of some formal language. The definition captures the truths of logic and mathematics: 'p or not-p' holds in every conceivable circumstance, depends upon no particular object, and is a tautology of propositional logic. Similarly for the truths of arithmetic and set theory, which can be formalised and shown to be logically necessary. Yet the definition excludes a class of truths that many philosophers hold to be metaphysically necessary. Consider the proposition that Hesperus is Phosphorus — that the Morning Star is identical with the Evening Star. If this identity holds, it appears to hold necessarily: the planet Venus could not have failed to be identical with itself. It holds in every conceivable circumstance, granted the identity. But it depends upon the existence of a particular object, namely the planet Venus; if Venus did not exist, the proposition would lack a subject. And it is not expressible as a tautology, for 'Hesperus' and 'Phosphorus' are distinct names, and no formal manipulation renders the proposition a logical truth. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has restricted necessity to truths that are both general and tautologous, and has thereby excluded de re necessities — necessities about particular objects — which are, on many accounts, perfectly genuine.

definition: A thing changes if and only if (i) it loses a property, (ii) it gains a different property, and (iii) the loss and the gain occur simultaneously.
conditions: loses a property
gains a different property
loss and gain occur simultaneously
counterexample: Consider a lake that gradually warms over the course of a summer. It loses the property of being cold and gains the property of being warm, but the loss and the gain do not occur simultaneously — the warming is a continuous process spread over weeks or months. At no single moment does the lake both lose coldness and gain warmth; the transition is gradual and admits of no precise instant of exchange. The third condition fails, yet the lake has plainly changed.
missing_condition: The definition models change on the instantaneous replacement of one property by another, but much change — indeed, most natural change — is gradual, and the loss and gain of properties are spread over extended intervals. The definition cannot accommodate continuous change.
passage: One might define change as follows: a thing changes if and only if it loses a property, gains a different property, and the loss and the gain occur simultaneously. The definition is meant to capture the moment of transition — the precise instant at which the old property departs and the new one arrives. It handles certain abrupt changes tolerably well: a light is switched on, and at the instant of switching the filament loses the property of being dark and gains the property of being luminous. But the definition founders upon gradual change. Consider a lake that warms over the course of a summer. In April the water is cold; by August it is warm. The lake has lost the property of being cold and gained the property of being warm. But the loss and the gain do not occur simultaneously. There is no single instant at which the lake is both losing coldness and gaining warmth; the transition is continuous, spread over many weeks, and at any given moment the lake is merely slightly warmer than it was the moment before. The third condition fails. The definition has modelled change upon sudden, discontinuous transitions and has thereby excluded the vast majority of natural changes, which are gradual, continuous, and resistant to the identification of a single moment of transformation. Change, it appears, need not involve a simultaneous exchange of properties.

definition: An object persists if and only if (i) it is wholly present at each moment of its existence, (ii) it retains its essential properties throughout, and (iii) there is a continuous causal chain linking its earlier states to its later states.
conditions: wholly present at each moment
retains essential properties throughout
continuous causal chain linking earlier to later states
counterexample: A caterpillar enters a chrysalis and undergoes metamorphosis, emerging as a butterfly. The butterfly persists as the same organism as the caterpillar — it has the same DNA, the same continuous life history. There is a continuous causal chain linking the caterpillar to the butterfly. But the butterfly does not retain the essential properties of the caterpillar: it has wings rather than many legs, it flies rather than crawls, its body plan is wholly different. The second condition fails, yet biological continuity secures the identity of the organism.
missing_condition: The definition requires retention of essential properties, but organisms may undergo radical metamorphosis — transforming their body plan entirely — while persisting as the same individual. What is essential to persistence is the continuity of the underlying biological process, not the retention of any particular set of manifest properties.
passage: One might hold that an object persists if and only if it is wholly present at each moment of its existence, it retains its essential properties throughout, and there is a continuous causal chain linking its earlier states to its later states. The definition is meant to capture the intuition that a persisting thing remains fundamentally the same even as it changes in various respects. A man persists from youth to age: he is wholly present at each moment, he retains his essential properties — his humanity, his rationality, his bodily form — and there is a continuous causal chain linking the boy to the old man. Yet the definition encounters a striking difficulty with metamorphosis. A caterpillar enters a chrysalis and, over a period of weeks, is transformed into a butterfly. The butterfly is the same organism as the caterpillar — it possesses the same genetic material, the same continuous developmental history, and the same unbroken chain of biological causation. The third condition is satisfied. But the butterfly does not retain the essential properties of the caterpillar. It has wings where the caterpillar had none; it flies where the caterpillar crawled; its body plan, its feeding apparatus, its mode of locomotion are wholly different. The second condition fails. The definition would deny that the butterfly is the same entity as the caterpillar, yet biology assures us that it is. The definition has set the bar of essential-property retention too high for a world in which organisms routinely undergo radical transformation.

definition: A thing is possible if and only if (i) it can be coherently conceived, (ii) it can be described without equivocation, and (iii) its existence would not violate any known principle of logic or nature.
conditions: can be coherently conceived
can be described without equivocation
existence would not violate any known principle
counterexample: Before the discovery of non-Euclidean geometry, a space in which parallel lines converge could not be coherently conceived by most mathematicians — it appeared to violate a necessary truth of geometry. The first condition would have failed. Yet such a space is possible; it is realised in the geometry of Riemann and, indeed, in the large-scale structure of the universe under general relativity. What can be coherently conceived is hostage to the state of mathematical and scientific knowledge, and so conceivability is an unreliable guide to possibility.
missing_condition: The definition ties possibility to conceivability, but conceivability is limited by the current state of knowledge and imagination. Some genuine possibilities are temporarily inconceivable, and some apparent conceivabilities prove impossible upon closer analysis. Possibility is a feature of reality, not of our powers of conception.
passage: One might propose that a thing is possible if and only if it can be coherently conceived, it can be described without equivocation, and its existence would not violate any known principle of logic or nature. The definition seems reasonable: if a thing can be clearly thought and described without contradiction, and if no principle forbids it, then surely it is possible. And the definition handles the standard cases well. A mountain of gold is possible: it can be conceived, described, and nothing in logic or nature forbids it. A round square is impossible: it cannot be coherently conceived. Yet the definition encounters a subtle difficulty when one considers the history of mathematics. Before the work of Lobachevsky, Bolyai, and Riemann in the nineteenth century, a space in which the parallel postulate fails — a space in which lines that begin parallel converge or diverge — could not be coherently conceived by most mathematicians. Euclid's fifth postulate was taken to be a necessary truth, and its denial appeared to lead to absurdity. The first condition would have failed: non-Euclidean space was inconceivable. Yet non-Euclidean space is possible; indeed, it is actual, for the large-scale geometry of the universe under general relativity is non-Euclidean. Conceivability proved to be a poor guide to possibility, limited as it was by the mathematical knowledge of the day. The definition has made possibility dependent upon our capacity to conceive, and this capacity is a variable and fallible thing.

definition: Two objects are numerically identical if and only if (i) they share all qualitative properties, (ii) they share all relational properties, and (iii) they have the same origin.
conditions: share all qualitative properties
share all relational properties
have the same origin
counterexample: Consider a pair of iron spheres produced simultaneously, by the same process, in the same foundry, from the same batch of molten iron. They share all qualitative properties — same mass, same composition, same temperature — and all relational properties relative to their context of manufacture. They have the same origin. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet they are two objects, not one. The definition fails to distinguish numerical identity from the closest possible qualitative resemblance combined with shared origin.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the objects be one and the same spatiotemporal particular. Two qualitatively indiscernible objects with the same origin are still two objects if they occupy different regions of space. Numerical identity requires not merely agreement in properties and origin but actual singularity of the particular.
passage: It is natural to suppose that two objects are numerically identical if and only if they share all qualitative properties, they share all relational properties, and they have the same origin. The first two conditions amount to Leibniz's law — the identity of indiscernibles — and the third adds a genetic requirement intended to exclude accidental duplicates. The definition handles many cases correctly: the statue in the gallery today is identical with the statue that was placed there last year, for the two share all properties and have the same origin. Yet the definition encounters a decisive difficulty with perfect duplicates. Imagine a foundry in which two iron spheres are cast simultaneously from the same batch of molten iron, by the same process, in moulds of exactly the same dimensions. The spheres are qualitatively indiscernible: same mass, same composition, same temperature, same surface texture. They share all relational properties relative to their context of manufacture — each was cast at the same time, in the same foundry, by the same craftsman. And they have the same origin: the same batch of iron, the same moment of casting. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet there are plainly two spheres, not one. They sit side by side upon the bench, occupying different regions of space. The definition cannot distinguish numerical identity from the most perfect qualitative agreement, for it has no resources to require that the objects be one and the same spatiotemporal particular rather than two particulars that happen to be indiscernible.

definition: A thing is a substance if and only if (i) it is ontologically independent, requiring nothing else for its existence, (ii) it persists through time as a numerically identical entity, and (iii) it serves as the ultimate subject of predication, never itself being predicated of another thing.
conditions: ontologically independent, requiring nothing else for its existence
persists through time as numerically identical
serves as ultimate subject of predication, never predicated of another
counterexample: Consider a case in which one examines a living organism — say, a particular oak tree. The oak depends for its existence upon soil, water, sunlight, and the atmosphere; remove any of these and it perishes. It is not ontologically independent in the strict sense demanded by the first condition. Yet the oak is a substance if anything is: it persists through time, it is the subject of innumerable predications, and it possesses a unity and self-directedness that distinguishes it from mere aggregates. The first condition fails, yet the oak remains a paradigmatic substance.
missing_condition: The definition demands absolute ontological independence, but substances in the natural world are characteristically dependent upon external conditions for their continued existence; what distinguishes them is not independence from all else but rather a distinctive internal unity and principle of organisation.
passage: One might propose that a thing is a substance if and only if it is ontologically independent, requiring nothing else for its existence, it persists through time as a numerically identical entity, and it serves as the ultimate subject of predication, never itself being predicated of another thing. This definition captures certain intuitions admirably. A stone, one might say, exists in its own right, endures through the years, and is the subject of predication — we say of it that it is grey, that it is heavy, that it lies in the garden. Yet the definition proves too restrictive when one turns to natural substances. Consider a particular oak tree standing in a field. The oak depends for its existence upon a great many things external to itself: upon the soil in which it is rooted, the water it draws from the earth, the sunlight that nourishes its leaves, and the atmosphere from which it takes its carbon. Remove any one of these and the tree will perish. It appears, then, that the oak is not ontologically independent in the sense required by the first condition. Yet it would be exceedingly odd to deny that the oak is a substance. It persists through decades, it is the subject of countless true predications, and it possesses an internal unity and principle of organisation — a nature, one might say — that distinguishes it from a mere heap of cellulose. The definition has mistaken a feature of the Cartesian or Spinozistic notion of substance — absolute independence from all else — for a feature of substance as such. In truth, the substances of the natural world are characteristically dependent upon their environments, and what marks them out is not self-sufficiency but a distinctive manner of being organised.

definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) there is a necessary connexion between C and E such that, given C, E could not have failed to occur.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
necessary connexion such that given C, E could not fail to occur
counterexample: Suppose that a man places a letter in the post in London, and three days later a friend in Edinburgh receives it and, upon reading its contents, decides to sell his house. The posting of the letter is the cause of the decision to sell, yet the two events are separated by hundreds of miles and several days — they are not spatiotemporally contiguous in any ordinary sense. The second condition fails, yet the causal connexion between the posting and the decision is plain.
missing_condition: The definition requires spatiotemporal contiguity between cause and effect, but causation frequently operates across spatial and temporal gaps through intermediate links, and what matters is the existence of a causal chain rather than immediate adjacency.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an event C is the cause of an event E if and only if C temporally precedes E, C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and there is a necessary connexion between them such that, given C, E could not have failed to occur. This account has a distinguished pedigree and captures the paradigm cases well: the striking of a match causes the flame, the blow of the hammer causes the nail to sink, the opening of the sluice causes the water to flow. In each instance the cause and the effect are adjacent in space and time, and the one seems to necessitate the other. Yet consider a rather different case. A man in London places a letter in the post, and three days later a friend in Edinburgh, upon reading its contents, resolves to sell his house. We should not hesitate to say that the posting of the letter caused the decision to sell; the friend himself would affirm as much. But the two events are separated by a considerable distance and by several days. They are not spatiotemporally contiguous in any straightforward sense. One might attempt to rescue the definition by insisting upon a chain of contiguous intermediate events — the letter passing through the sorting office, travelling by rail, arriving at the Edinburgh address — but this merely concedes that contiguity between cause and effect is not required; what is required is a chain of intermediaries. The definition as stated demands adjacency between C and E themselves, and this condition fails. The lesson, it appears, is that causation may operate across gaps in space and time, provided there exists a suitable chain of transmission, and that contiguity is a feature of certain causal connexions rather than of causation as such.

definition: A moment of time is real if and only if (i) it is present — that is, it is occurring now, (ii) it is directly experienced by conscious beings, and (iii) it is not merely a mathematical abstraction or a point on a coordinate axis.
conditions: it is present, occurring now
directly experienced by conscious beings
not merely a mathematical abstraction
counterexample: Consider the eruption of Vesuvius in the year 79. That event is no longer present; it is not occurring now. No conscious being alive today directly experiences it. Yet it would be absurd to say that the moment at which Vesuvius erupted is not real, for it left behind physical effects — the buried city of Pompeii — that persist to this day. The first and second conditions fail, yet the moment is as real as any.
missing_condition: The definition restricts reality to the present moment as experienced, but past moments that have left indelible physical traces and that figure in true historical propositions possess a reality that is independent of present experience.
passage: One might hold that a moment of time is real if and only if it is present — that is, occurring now — it is directly experienced by conscious beings, and it is not merely a mathematical abstraction or a point on a coordinate axis. This account reflects a certain philosophical temperament: the conviction that only the vivid, experienced present is genuinely real, and that past and future are mere shadows or constructions. It captures something important, for the present moment does seem to have a peculiar vividness that the past and the future lack. Yet the definition is far too restrictive. Consider the eruption of Vesuvius in the year 79. That eruption is not present; it is not occurring now. No living person directly experiences it. By the lights of the definition, the moment at which Vesuvius erupted is not real. But this conclusion is extraordinary. The eruption buried the city of Pompeii under metres of volcanic ash, and the physical remains of that catastrophe are still visible today. Archaeologists excavate the streets, the houses, the very bodies of the victims. The eruption left effects that have persisted for nearly two thousand years. To say that the moment of the eruption is unreal is to say that the cause of all these effects never genuinely occurred, which is plainly untenable. The definition has confused the phenomenological salience of the present with the ontological status of temporal moments as such, overlooking the fact that past moments may be as real as present ones, their reality attested by the enduring traces they leave upon the world.

definition: An entity X at time t1 is identical with an entity Y at time t2 if and only if (i) X and Y share all their properties, (ii) X and Y occupy a continuous spatiotemporal path from t1 to t2, and (iii) X and Y are composed of the same material parts.
conditions: share all properties
occupy continuous spatiotemporal path
composed of same material parts
counterexample: Consider a particular ship that, over the course of many years, has every one of its planks replaced as each becomes worn. At the end of this process, none of the original material parts remain. The ship at t2 shares neither its properties in full — its planks are new, its paint is fresh — nor its material composition with the ship at t1. The third condition fails entirely, and arguably the first as well. Yet the ship has been in continuous use throughout, has never ceased to exist, and is universally regarded as the same ship.
missing_condition: The definition requires sameness of material composition, but an entity may retain its identity through the gradual replacement of all its parts, provided the replacement is piecemeal and the entity's form, function, and continuity of existence are preserved.
passage: A natural account of identity through time holds that an entity X at time t1 is identical with an entity Y at time t2 if and only if X and Y share all their properties, they occupy a continuous spatiotemporal path from t1 to t2, and they are composed of the same material parts. This definition seems to capture the ordinary case: my desk today is the same desk as yesterday because it has the same properties, it has remained in the same place, and it is made of the same wood. Yet the definition encounters a well-known difficulty. Consider a ship that is maintained over many years by the gradual replacement of its planks. Each year, a few worn planks are removed and new ones fitted in their place. After a sufficient number of years, every original plank has been replaced. The ship at the end of this process is composed of entirely different material from the ship at the beginning. The third condition — sameness of material parts — fails utterly. And since the properties of the ship have also changed in various respects — it has new planks, new paint, perhaps a new mast — it is doubtful whether the first condition is satisfied either. Yet the ship has been in continuous service throughout, it has never ceased to exist, and its owners, its crew, and the port authorities have never for a moment doubted that it is the same ship. The definition has treated material composition as essential to identity, when in truth an entity may preserve its identity through the complete replacement of its matter, provided the replacement is gradual and the entity's form and function are maintained.

definition: A universal is a property that exists if and only if (i) it is instantiated by at least one particular, (ii) it is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) it can be apprehended by the mind through abstraction from particular cases.
conditions: instantiated by at least one particular
wholly present in each instance
apprehended by mind through abstraction from particulars
counterexample: Suppose that a certain shade of blue — let us call it cerulean-prime — has never been instantiated by any particular object in the history of the universe, yet it occupies a determinate position in the colour spectrum between two shades that are instantiated. It appears that cerulean-prime is a perfectly genuine property: it stands in determinate relations to other colours, it could be instantiated, and it can be defined with precision. Yet the first condition fails, for no particular has ever possessed it. The definition excludes uninstantiated universals, yet there seem to be properties that no actual thing has ever had.
missing_condition: The definition requires instantiation, but some universals appear to exist as determinate positions in a structured space of properties — such as the colour spectrum — regardless of whether any particular happens to instantiate them.
passage: One might define a universal as a property that exists if and only if it is instantiated by at least one particular, it is wholly present in each of its instances, and it can be apprehended by the mind through abstraction from particular cases. This account has a certain Aristotelian flavour and captures many of our ordinary intuitions. The property of redness exists because there are red things; the property of squareness exists because there are square things; and we apprehend these properties by observing their instances and abstracting what is common. Yet the definition faces a difficulty with uninstantiated properties. Consider a particular shade of blue — call it cerulean-prime — that has never been possessed by any object in the history of the universe. It is a shade that falls between two instantiated shades in the colour spectrum, and it can be defined with great precision in terms of its wavelength. Cerulean-prime stands in determinate relations to other colours: it is lighter than navy, darker than sky-blue, and more saturated than powder-blue. It could be instantiated — there is no impossibility in an object's being that shade. Yet no object has ever been cerulean-prime. The first condition fails. If we accept the definition, we must deny that cerulean-prime is a genuine property, which seems wrong. It occupies a definite place in the structure of colour-space, and its existence appears to depend not upon actual instantiation but upon its position in a system of properties. The definition has tied the existence of universals too closely to their actual instances, neglecting those properties whose reality consists in their place within a structured domain.

definition: A state of affairs is possible if and only if (i) it is conceivable without contradiction, (ii) it is compatible with the laws of nature, and (iii) there exists some process by which it could be brought about.
conditions: conceivable without contradiction
compatible with laws of nature
exists some process by which it could be brought about
counterexample: Consider the state of affairs in which no material objects exist — a wholly empty universe. This is conceivable without contradiction; one can think it through without arriving at any logical absurdity. It is compatible with the laws of nature, for if there were no matter, there would be nothing for the laws to act upon and no violation would arise. Yet there exists no process by which it could be brought about, for any process of annihilation would require something — an agent, an event, a mechanism — and the existence of that something would prevent the universe from being wholly empty. The third condition fails, yet the empty universe seems possible.
missing_condition: The definition requires a realisable process, but some possible states of affairs — particularly those involving the absence of all things — cannot be brought about by any process and yet remain genuinely possible, since their possibility is a matter of logical and metaphysical coherence rather than causal accessibility.
passage: A plausible account holds that a state of affairs is possible if and only if it is conceivable without contradiction, it is compatible with the laws of nature, and there exists some process by which it could be brought about. This definition captures many intuitive judgements. It is possible for a stone to be thrown across a river, for this is conceivable, lawful, and there exists a process — a human arm — by which it can be accomplished.
