It is not possible for a body to travel faster than light, for this is incompatible with the laws of nature. The account seems to sort cases well. But consider the state of affairs in which no material objects exist at all — a universe that is wholly empty. One can conceive of this without contradiction. There is no logical absurdity in the thought of nothingness. It is compatible with the laws of nature; indeed, if there were nothing, the laws of nature would have nothing to govern. Yet there exists no process by which this state could be brought about. Any process of destruction or annihilation would require some agent or mechanism, and the existence of that agent would prevent the universe from being empty. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, it appears that the empty universe is possible; the question 'Why is there something rather than nothing?' presupposes as much. The definition has confused possibility with causal accessibility, as though a state of affairs could be possible only if some mechanism could produce it, when in truth possibility is a matter of what could be the case, not of what could be made to be the case.

definition: A proposition is necessary if and only if (i) its denial leads to a logical contradiction, (ii) it is true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms, and (iii) it can be known independently of experience.
conditions: denial leads to logical contradiction
true in virtue of meanings of constituent terms
known independently of experience
counterexample: Suppose that water is H2O. This appears to be a necessary truth — water could not have been anything other than H2O, for to be water just is to be H2O. Yet the denial of this proposition does not yield a logical contradiction in any obvious sense; one can coherently entertain the thought that water might have a different chemical composition without arriving at a formal absurdity. Moreover, the proposition is not true in virtue of the meanings of its terms — 'water' and 'H2O' are not synonymous — and it was discovered through empirical investigation, not known independently of experience. All three conditions fail, yet the proposition appears to be necessary.
missing_condition: The definition captures only logical or analytic necessity, but there appear to be necessary truths — such as the identity of natural kinds with their underlying natures — that are discovered empirically and whose necessity is metaphysical rather than logical or definitional.
passage: One might maintain that a proposition is necessary if and only if its denial leads to a logical contradiction, it is true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms, and it can be known independently of experience. This definition captures a familiar class of necessary truths: that all bachelors are unmarried, that nothing is both red and green all over, that two plus two equals four. In each case the denial produces a contradiction, the truth follows from the meanings of the words, and the proposition is knowable a priori. Yet the definition appears to exclude another class of propositions that have a strong claim to necessity. Consider the proposition that water is H2O. There is a powerful intuition that this is not a contingent truth — that water could not have turned out to be something other than H2O, for to be water just is to have that molecular composition. Yet the denial of this proposition does not yield a logical contradiction in any straightforward sense. One can coherently entertain the thought of a substance that looks and behaves like water but has a different chemical structure, without arriving at a formal absurdity. Furthermore, the proposition is not true in virtue of the meanings of its terms; the word 'water' and the expression 'H2O' are not synonymous, and the identification was a discovery of chemistry, not of lexicography. And the proposition was assuredly not known independently of experience; it required centuries of empirical inquiry. All three conditions fail. The definition has identified necessity with analyticity, when there appear to be necessary truths whose necessity is metaphysical rather than logical — truths about what things are, not about what words mean.

definition: A thing undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) it possesses a property at one time that it lacks at another, (ii) the thing itself persists as the same entity through the change, and (iii) the change is intrinsic — that is, it involves the alteration of the thing's own properties rather than its relational properties.
conditions: possesses a property at one time that it lacks at another
persists as same entity through the change
change is intrinsic, involving alteration of own properties
counterexample: Suppose that a person who has been the tallest person in his village ceases to be so because a younger man has grown taller than he. The first man has undergone a change — he was the tallest and now he is not — and he has persisted as the same entity throughout. But the change is entirely relational: no intrinsic property of the man has altered. He has not shrunk; his height is precisely what it was. The third condition fails, yet it seems wrong to say that no genuine change has occurred, for the man's social standing, his practical circumstances, and the truths that can be stated about him have all shifted.
missing_condition: The definition excludes relational changes, but a thing may undergo genuine change when its relational properties alter, even if its intrinsic properties remain the same, particularly when the relational change has real consequences for the thing's situation.
passage: It might be proposed that a thing undergoes genuine change if and only if it possesses a property at one time that it lacks at another, the thing itself persists as the same entity through the change, and the change is intrinsic — that is, it involves the alteration of the thing's own properties rather than its merely relational properties. This account is designed to distinguish real change from what some philosophers have called 'mere Cambridge change,' and it captures many paradigm cases well. When a leaf turns from green to brown, the leaf has changed intrinsically. When iron rusts, its intrinsic chemical properties have altered. But consider a person who has, for many years, been the tallest person in his village. One day a younger man grows taller than he, and our subject ceases to be the tallest. He has undergone a change: a proposition that was once true of him is now false. He has persisted through the change as the same individual. Yet no intrinsic property of his has altered in the slightest. His height, his weight, his health — all remain exactly as they were. The change is entirely relational, brought about by the growth of another person. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that this relational change has real consequences: the man may lose a certain standing in the community, he may be passed over for tasks that require great height, and the truths that can be asserted of him have genuinely shifted. The definition, in its eagerness to exclude trivial relational changes, has excluded relational changes that carry real significance for the entity in question.

definition: A thing persists through time if and only if (i) it is wholly present at each moment of its existence, (ii) it maintains a continuous spatiotemporal trajectory, and (iii) it retains its essential properties throughout.
conditions: wholly present at each moment of existence
maintains continuous spatiotemporal trajectory
retains essential properties throughout
counterexample: Consider a glacier that, over the course of centuries, moves from one valley to another, losing ice at its terminus and gaining ice from snowfall at its source. At no two moments is it composed of the same ice; its spatiotemporal trajectory involves a slow, creeping displacement over miles of terrain; and its essential properties — its size, its shape, its composition — change considerably. One might argue that the glacier at any given moment is not wholly present in the sense required, for its existence is spread over a vast and shifting expanse. The third condition fails, and the first is questionable, yet the glacier persists — it is the same glacier that was mapped a century ago.
missing_condition: The definition requires retention of essential properties, but many persisting entities — particularly geological and biological ones — undergo continuous transformation of their properties while preserving a characteristic pattern of dynamic activity that constitutes their persistence.
passage: One might hold that a thing persists through time if and only if it is wholly present at each moment of its existence, it maintains a continuous spatiotemporal trajectory, and it retains its essential properties throughout. This definition captures the persistence of certain simple objects well enough: a diamond in a vault, one might say, is wholly present at each moment, occupies a continuous path through spacetime, and retains its hardness, its crystalline structure, and its chemical composition. Yet the definition is less convincing when applied to entities of a more dynamic character. Consider a glacier. Over the course of centuries, a glacier moves from one valley to another, advancing and retreating in response to variations in climate. It loses ice at its terminus through melting and calving, and gains ice from fresh snowfall at its source. At no two moments is it composed of the same ice. Its shape changes as it flows around obstacles, its size varies from decade to decade, and its position shifts by miles over the centuries. The essential properties it possessed at the time it was first mapped — its particular size, shape, position, and composition — have all altered. The third condition fails. And it is questionable whether the glacier is wholly present at each moment in the way that a diamond is, for its existence seems to be constituted by a continuous process of accumulation and loss rather than by the presence of a fixed quantity of matter. Yet the glacier persists. It is the same glacier that was described by earlier observers, and it has a continuous history. The definition has modelled persistence on the endurance of static objects, neglecting the persistence of dynamic entities whose identity consists not in the retention of fixed properties but in the continuation of a characteristic pattern of activity.

definition: An object X is a proper part of an object Y if and only if (i) X is spatially contained within Y, (ii) X is of the same ontological kind as Y, and (iii) the removal of X would diminish Y without destroying it.
conditions: spatially contained within Y
of same ontological kind as Y
removal would diminish Y without destroying it
counterexample: Consider the heart of a living animal. The heart is spatially contained within the animal, so the first condition is met. But the heart is an organ and the animal is an organism — they are not of the same ontological kind, so the second condition fails. Moreover, the removal of the heart would not merely diminish the animal but would destroy it — the animal would die — so the third condition fails as well. Yet the heart is a proper part of the animal if anything is.
missing_condition: The definition requires sameness of ontological kind and survivable removal, but parts and wholes frequently belong to different ontological categories, and some parts are so vital that their removal destroys the whole, without this diminishing their status as genuine parts.
passage: A natural mereological principle holds that an object X is a proper part of an object Y if and only if X is spatially contained within Y, X is of the same ontological kind as Y, and the removal of X would diminish Y without destroying it. The definition works well for certain simple cases. A brick is a proper part of a wall: it is spatially contained within the wall, it is of the same general kind — a material object — and its removal would diminish the wall without destroying it. A chapter is a proper part of a book: it is contained within the book, it is of the same kind — a piece of writing — and its removal would diminish the book without reducing it to nothing. Yet consider the heart of a living animal. The heart is spatially contained within the animal; the first condition is satisfied. But the heart is an organ and the animal is an organism — they belong to different ontological categories, the one a functional component, the other a living whole. The second condition fails. Furthermore, the removal of the heart would not merely diminish the animal; it would kill it. The animal cannot survive the loss of its heart. The third condition fails as well. Yet it would be very strange to deny that the heart is a proper part of the animal. It is embedded within the animal, it contributes to the animal's functioning, and ordinary language and biological science alike treat it as a part. The definition has been drawn from cases in which parts are dispensable and homogeneous with their wholes, and it fails when applied to parts that are vital and ontologically distinct from the wholes to which they belong.

definition: A proposition P is metaphysically possible if and only if (i) there is a complete and consistent description of a world in which P obtains, (ii) that world obeys the same fundamental laws as the actual world, and (iii) the entities referred to in P exist in that world.
conditions: complete consistent description of a world in which P obtains
that world obeys same fundamental laws as actual world
entities referred to in P exist in that world
counterexample: Consider the proposition that the laws of nature might have been different — that, for instance, the gravitational constant might have had a different value. This proposition concerns a world that, by hypothesis, does not obey the same fundamental laws as the actual world. The second condition fails. Moreover, in such a world the entities as we know them might not exist in the same form, so the third condition is questionable. Yet it appears genuinely possible that the laws of nature could have been otherwise; there seems to be no metaphysical necessity that the constants of physics take the values they do.
missing_condition: The definition restricts possibility to worlds sharing our laws, but metaphysical possibility extends to worlds with different fundamental laws, since the laws themselves do not appear to be metaphysically necessary.
passage: One might define metaphysical possibility as follows: a proposition P is metaphysically possible if and only if there is a complete and consistent description of a world in which P obtains, that world obeys the same fundamental laws as the actual world, and the entities referred to in P exist in that world. This account captures a range of ordinary modal judgements. It is metaphysically possible that I should have been born in a different country, for one can describe a lawful world in which this is so. It is metaphysically possible that the earth should have had two moons, for there is nothing in the laws of nature that forbids it. Yet the definition is too restrictive in a crucial respect. Consider the proposition that the gravitational constant might have had a value slightly different from its actual value. This proposition concerns a world whose fundamental laws differ from those of the actual world — for the gravitational constant partly determines what the law of gravitation is. The second condition therefore fails: the world in question does not obey the same laws. Yet it appears genuinely possible that the gravitational constant could have been different. There is nothing contradictory or incoherent in the supposition, and physicists themselves speak of the values of the constants as contingent features of our universe. The definition has conflated metaphysical possibility with what one might call nomological possibility — possibility relative to a fixed set of laws. But metaphysical possibility, it appears, is broader than this. The laws of nature themselves are among the things that might have been otherwise, and a definition that holds the laws fixed will be unable to accommodate this intuition.

definition: Space is a real entity if and only if (i) it exists independently of the material objects that occupy it, (ii) it has a determinate structure — geometry — that obtains whether or not anything fills it, and (iii) it is immutable, remaining the same regardless of the distribution or motion of matter.
conditions: exists independently of material objects
has determinate structure independent of occupants
immutable regardless of distribution or motion of matter
counterexample: Suppose that we accept the findings of general relativity, according to which the geometry of space is affected by the distribution of mass and energy. Near a massive body, space is curved; far from matter, it is approximately flat. Space is not immutable — its structure changes in response to the matter within it. The third condition fails. Yet space remains a real entity on this account; it is not reduced to a mere fiction. It has genuine physical properties, it affects and is affected by matter, and it is the arena in which physical events occur.
missing_condition: The definition requires immutability, but space may be a real entity even if its geometry is dynamic and responsive to the distribution of matter, as the theory of general relativity suggests.
passage: A robust realism about space might hold that space is a real entity if and only if it exists independently of the material objects that occupy it, it has a determinate structure that obtains whether or not anything fills it, and it is immutable, remaining the same regardless of the distribution or motion of matter. This definition captures the Newtonian conception admirably. For Newton, absolute space is a fixed, unchanging container in which bodies move; its geometry is Euclidean and eternal; and it would persist in all its structure even if every material body were annihilated. Yet consider what becomes of this definition if one takes seriously the general theory of relativity. According to that theory, the geometry of space is not fixed but is influenced by the distribution of mass and energy. Near a massive body such as a star, space is curved; far from all matter, it is approximately flat. Space, on this account, is not immutable; its structure is dynamic, bending and warping in response to the presence of matter. The third condition fails. Yet it would be rash to conclude that space is not a real entity. On the contrary, the very fact that space can be curved — that it has genuine geometrical properties that vary from place to place and can be measured — suggests that it is something real rather than a mere abstraction. The definition has tied the reality of space to its immutability, a feature of the Newtonian conception that does not appear to be essential to the reality of space as such.

definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C is a sufficient condition for E — that is, whenever C occurs, E follows, (ii) C is a necessary condition for E — that is, without C, E would not have occurred, and (iii) no other event is independently sufficient for E.
conditions: C is sufficient condition for E
C is necessary condition for E
no other event independently sufficient for E
counterexample: Suppose that two assassins, acting independently and unknown to each other, each fire a shot at a victim at the same instant, and each shot is individually lethal. The victim dies. Each shot is sufficient for the death, so the first condition is met by each. But neither shot is necessary, for if one had not been fired, the other would have produced the same result. The second condition fails for each shot. And each shot violates the third condition from the perspective of the other. Yet both shots are causes of the death — it would be wrong to say that neither assassin killed the victim.
missing_condition: The definition cannot accommodate cases of causal overdetermination, where two or more independently sufficient causes jointly produce an effect, since it demands that the cause be necessary and that no rival sufficient cause exist.
passage: One might propose that an event C causes an event E if and only if C is a sufficient condition for E, C is a necessary condition for E, and no other event is independently sufficient for E. This account captures many ordinary causal judgements. The spark caused the fire: the spark was sufficient (given the presence of fuel and oxygen), the fire would not have occurred without it, and no other event was independently sufficient for igniting the fuel. Yet the definition encounters a well-known difficulty with overdetermination. Suppose that two assassins, acting entirely independently and with no awareness of each other, each fire a lethal shot at a victim at the very same instant. Each shot, taken alone, would be sufficient to kill. The victim dies. Now consider the first assassin's shot. It is sufficient for the death — the first condition is met. But it is not necessary, for had it not been fired, the second shot would have been lethal on its own. The second condition fails. Furthermore, there is another event — the second shot — that is independently sufficient for the death, so the third condition fails as well. The same reasoning applies, mutatis mutandis, to the second assassin's shot. The definition entails that neither shot is the cause of the death, which is absurd. Both assassins killed the victim; both shots are causes. The definition has demanded that a cause be indispensable, but in cases of overdetermination, each cause is dispensable precisely because the other is also sufficient, and yet both are genuine causes.

definition: A thing is a substance if and only if (i) it has a fixed, determinate nature or essence, (ii) that essence is present in the thing from the first moment of its existence, and (iii) the thing cannot gain or lose its essence without ceasing to exist.
conditions: has fixed determinate nature or essence
essence present from first moment of existence
cannot gain or lose essence without ceasing to exist
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that undergoes metamorphosis and becomes a butterfly. The caterpillar is a crawling, leaf-eating larva; the butterfly is a winged, nectar-drinking adult. If the essence of the thing is determined at the first moment of its existence, then its essence is to be a caterpillar, and the emergence of the butterfly ought to count as the destruction of the original substance and the creation of a new one. Yet biologically the organism has not ceased to exist; it is the same individual throughout, as its continuous metabolism and unbroken life-history attest.
missing_condition: The definition ties essence to a fixed nature present from origin, but some substances possess developmental essences — their nature unfolds over time through radical transformation, and the capacity for such transformation is itself part of what the thing essentially is.
passage: One might hold that a thing is a substance if and only if it has a fixed, determinate nature or essence, that essence is present in the thing from the first moment of its existence, and the thing cannot gain or lose its essence without ceasing to exist. This account captures certain intuitions well. A lump of gold has a fixed nature — it is gold — and it has that nature from the moment of its formation; if it were transmuted into lead, it would no longer be gold, and one might say the original substance had perished. Yet the definition faces a difficulty with organisms that undergo radical transformation. Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and emerges as a butterfly. The caterpillar is a crawling, leaf-eating larva with no wings, no proboscis, and a segmented body quite unlike that of the adult. The butterfly is a winged creature that feeds on nectar and navigates by the sun. If the essence of the thing is fixed at the outset and present from the first moment, then the essence is to be a caterpillar, and the transformation into a butterfly ought to constitute the destruction of the original substance. But this is biologically untenable. The organism does not die and give rise to a new organism; it is the same individual throughout, with an unbroken metabolism and a continuous life-history. The caterpillar and the butterfly are stages in the life of a single substance. The definition has conceived of essence as static, when the essence of certain substances is developmental — their nature is to undergo transformation, and the capacity for metamorphosis is itself part of what the organism essentially is.

definition: Two events are simultaneous if and only if (i) they occur at the same moment of absolute time, (ii) an ideally situated observer could perceive both at once, and (iii) a clock co-present with each event would show the same reading.
conditions: occur at same moment of absolute time
ideally situated observer could perceive both at once
clock co-present with each would show same reading
counterexample: Consider two flashes of light, one occurring near the surface of the earth and the other occurring at a great distance in interstellar space. According to the special theory of relativity, whether these two events are simultaneous depends upon the frame of reference of the observer. An observer at rest relative to the earth may judge them simultaneous, while an observer moving at high velocity relative to the earth may judge them to occur at different times. There is no frame-independent fact of the matter about their simultaneity. The appeal to absolute time in the first condition presupposes a framework that relativity has undermined.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes absolute simultaneity and a privileged frame of reference, but simultaneity between distant events is relative to the observer's state of motion, and no definition can treat it as an absolute, frame-independent relation without ignoring the structure of spacetime.
passage: It is tempting to say that two events are simultaneous if and only if they occur at the same moment of absolute time, an ideally situated observer could perceive both at once, and a clock co-present with each event would show the same reading. This definition seems unproblematic in everyday life. Two clocks on a mantelpiece strike the hour together; two runners cross the finish line at the same instant; two church bells ring at noon. In each case, the events occur at the same time, an observer can witness both, and co-present clocks agree. Yet the definition encounters a profound difficulty when applied to events separated by great distances. Consider two flashes of light, one near the surface of the earth and the other in distant interstellar space. According to the special theory of relativity, the question of whether these two events are simultaneous does not have a single, determinate answer. An observer at rest relative to the earth may judge them simultaneous, whilst an observer moving at high velocity relative to the earth will judge them to occur at different times. Neither observer is mistaken; simultaneity between distant events is relative to the observer's state of motion. The first condition — occurrence at the same moment of absolute time — presupposes a framework that the special theory of relativity has shown to be untenable. There is no absolute time against which the simultaneity of distant events can be measured. The definition has taken a feature of our ordinary, low-velocity experience — the apparent absoluteness of simultaneity — and elevated it to a metaphysical principle, when in truth simultaneity is a relation that depends upon the frame of reference.

definition: An entity X is identical with an entity Y if and only if (i) X and Y have the same origin — they came into existence at the same time, in the same place, and from the same matter, (ii) X and Y are of the same kind, and (iii) X and Y are indiscernible — they share all their properties.
conditions: same origin in time, place, and matter
same kind
indiscernible, sharing all properties
counterexample: Consider a bronze statue and the lump of bronze from which it is made. They came into existence at the same time — when the sculptor cast the bronze into the mould — they occupy the same place, and they are composed of the same matter. The first condition is met. Yet they are not of the same kind: one is a statue, the other a lump of metal. The second condition fails. Moreover, they differ in their modal properties: the lump could survive being melted down and recast, but the statue could not. The third condition fails as well. Yet there is a strong intuition that we are dealing with one entity, not two.
missing_condition: The definition cannot resolve the puzzle of coincident entities — objects that share their matter and origin but differ in kind and in modal properties — because it requires identity of kind and indiscernibility, which fail for coincident objects that are nonetheless intimately related.
passage: A natural account of identity holds that an entity X is identical with an entity Y if and only if X and Y have the same origin — they came into existence at the same time, in the same place, and from the same matter — they are of the same kind, and they are indiscernible, sharing all their properties. This definition captures straightforward cases: the morning star is identical with the evening star because they have the same origin, are of the same kind (a planet), and share all their properties. But consider a bronze statue and the lump of bronze of which it is composed. Suppose the sculptor casts the bronze into a mould, and the statue and the lump come into existence simultaneously. They share the same origin in time, place, and matter, so the first condition is satisfied. But they are not of the same kind: one is a work of art, the other a quantity of metal. The second condition fails. And they appear to differ in their properties — specifically, their modal properties. The lump of bronze could survive being melted down and recast into a different shape, but the statue could not; melting would destroy the statue whilst preserving the lump. The third condition fails as well. The definition therefore tells us that the statue and the lump are not identical, which implies that two distinct objects occupy the same region of space at the same time. This is a deeply puzzling conclusion. The definition has required sameness of kind and complete indiscernibility as conditions of identity, but these conditions fail for coincident objects, leaving us with the uncomfortable result that material constitution is not identity.

definition: A universal exists if and only if (i) it is a feature that recurs identically in multiple distinct particulars, (ii) it is mind-independent — it would exist even if no mind ever conceived of it, and (iii) it is simple, admitting of no further analysis into more basic universals.
conditions: recurs identically in multiple distinct particulars
mind-independent
simple, admitting no further analysis
counterexample: Consider the property of being scarlet. Scarlet is a determinate shade of red, and as such it admits of further analysis: it is a species of redness, which is itself a species of being coloured. Scarlet is not simple in the required sense; it can be analysed into more basic universals — redness and a specific degree of saturation and hue. The third condition fails. Yet scarlet recurs identically in multiple objects and is mind-independent; it is a perfectly genuine universal despite its complexity.
missing_condition: The definition requires simplicity, but many genuine universals are determinates that stand in systematic relations of genus and species to other universals, and their analysability into more basic universals does not deprive them of their status as real, recurring features of the world.
passage: One might maintain that a universal exists if and only if it is a feature that recurs identically in multiple distinct particulars, it is mind-independent, and it is simple, admitting of no further analysis into more basic universals. The demand for simplicity is motivated by the thought that only the most basic features of reality deserve to be called universals, and that complex features are mere constructions from simpler ones. This account may seem to capture a certain austere conception of universals: the fundamental physical properties, perhaps — charge, spin, mass — are simple, recurring, and mind-independent. But consider the property of being scarlet. Scarlet is a determinate shade of red, and it recurs identically in many distinct objects — a scarlet rose, a scarlet flag, a scarlet sunset. It is mind-independent; the rose would be scarlet whether or not anyone perceived it. But scarlet is not simple. It is a determinate falling under the determinable redness, which in turn falls under the determinable being coloured. Scarlet can be analysed into more basic components: a specific hue, a specific saturation, a specific brightness. The third condition fails. Yet it would be very peculiar to deny that scarlet is a genuine universal. It is a real, objective, recurring feature of things, and it enters into systematic relations with other colour-properties that suggest a structured domain of universals rather than a flat collection of simples. The definition has mistaken an ideal of ontological parsimony for a condition of existence, excluding the complex universals whose reality is attested by the structured, hierarchical character of the properties we find in the world.

definition: A thing changes if and only if (i) it passes from one state to a qualitatively different state, (ii) the transition occurs within a finite interval of time, and (iii) there is a cause — an event or process external to the thing — that brings about the transition.
conditions: passes from one state to qualitatively different state
transition occurs within finite interval
cause external to the thing brings about transition
counterexample: Consider a radioactive atom that spontaneously decays, emitting an alpha particle and transmuting into an atom of a different element. The atom has passed from one state to a qualitatively different state — the first condition is met. The transition occurs within a finite interval — the second condition is met. But the decay is spontaneous; there is no external cause that triggers it. Quantum mechanics describes the decay as an intrinsically probabilistic process with no external precipitant. The third condition fails, yet the atom has unquestionably changed.
missing_condition: The definition requires an external cause for every change, but some changes — such as radioactive decay — are spontaneous, arising from the intrinsic nature of the thing itself without any external trigger, and are nonetheless genuine changes.
passage: A plausible account holds that a thing changes if and only if it passes from one state to a qualitatively different state, the transition occurs within a finite interval of time, and there is a cause external to the thing that brings about the transition. This account captures the common run of changes well. A kettle of water changes from cold to hot because a flame is applied beneath it. A leaf changes from green to yellow because the autumn cold interrupts the production of chlorophyll. In each case, an external cause initiates the transition. But consider a radioactive atom — a particular atom of radium, let us say. At a certain moment, with no external provocation, the atom spontaneously decays. It emits an alpha particle and transmutes into an atom of radon. The atom has passed from one state to a qualitatively different state: it was radium and is now radon. The transition occurs within a finite and indeed exceedingly brief interval. But there is no external cause. No force acts upon the atom, no event in its environment triggers the decay. According to the quantum-mechanical description, the decay is intrinsically probabilistic; there is no antecedent event that makes it happen at that moment rather than at another. The third condition fails. Yet the atom has unquestionably undergone a change — one of the most dramatic changes possible, in fact, the transmutation of one element into another. The definition has assumed that all change requires an external precipitant, when nature furnishes examples of spontaneous change arising from the intrinsic character of the thing itself.

definition: A thing persists if and only if (i) it is a material object with determinate spatial boundaries, (ii) it maintains its structural organisation over time, and (iii) it does not undergo division into two or more separate entities.
conditions: material object with determinate spatial boundaries
maintains structural organisation over time
does not undergo division into separate entities
counterexample: Consider an amoeba that reproduces by binary fission, dividing into two daughter cells. Before the division, there is one amoeba; after, there are two. The original amoeba has undergone division into two separate entities, violating the third condition. Yet each daughter cell is alive, active, and continuous with the original. It seems wrong to say that the original amoeba has ceased to persist; rather, it persists in the form of its two offspring, each of which inherits half of the original's cytoplasm and genetic material.
missing_condition: The definition excludes persistence through division, but some entities — particularly biological ones — persist by dividing, and their persistence consists in the continuation of their living processes in the resulting parts rather than in the maintenance of a single undivided body.
passage: One might propose that a thing persists if and only if it is a material object with determinate spatial boundaries, it maintains its structural organisation over time, and it does not undergo division into two or more separate entities. This definition captures the persistence of many familiar objects: a stone persists because it is a bounded material object that retains its structure and does not split apart. A building persists as long as it maintains its structural integrity and is not demolished or divided. Yet the definition fails when applied to certain biological entities. Consider an amoeba that reproduces by binary fission. The amoeba is a material object with determinate boundaries, and it maintains its organisation up to the moment of division. But then it divides into two daughter cells, each of which is alive, metabolically active, and genetically identical to the original. The third condition — the prohibition on division — is violated. According to the definition, the original amoeba ceases to persist at the moment of fission. But this seems wrong. The amoeba has not been destroyed; it has reproduced. Its living processes continue uninterrupted in the two daughter cells, each of which inherits half the original's substance. The case of fission suggests that persistence is not necessarily tied to the maintenance of a single, undivided body. Some entities persist by dividing, their identity propagated in their successors, and a definition that rules out division rules out one of the most fundamental modes of biological continuity.

definition: A whole exists as a genuine entity if and only if (i) its parts are in physical contact with one another, (ii) the parts are fastened or bonded together, and (iii) the whole has properties that none of its parts individually possesses.
conditions: parts in physical contact
parts fastened or bonded together
whole has properties none of its parts individually possesses
counterexample: Consider the solar system. Its parts — the sun, the planets, the moons, the asteroids — are not in physical contact with one another; they are separated by vast stretches of empty space. They are not fastened or bonded together in any mechanical or chemical sense; they are held in their orbits by gravitational attraction, which operates across a void. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the solar system has properties that none of its parts individually possesses — orbital periods, a centre of mass, a characteristic plane of revolution — and it functions as a genuine system. It is exceedingly difficult to deny that the solar system is a real whole.
missing_condition: The definition requires physical contact and bonding, but some genuine wholes — such as gravitationally bound systems — are constituted by parts separated by great distances and held together by forces that operate across a void, rather than by contact or adhesion.
passage: It might be maintained that a whole exists as a genuine entity if and only if its parts are in physical contact with one another, the parts are fastened or bonded together, and the whole has properties that none of its parts individually possesses. This definition captures many familiar wholes. A table is a genuine entity: its parts — legs, top, joints — are in physical contact and are fastened together by glue or screws, and the table has the property of being a surface for dining, which none of its parts has individually. A molecule is a genuine entity: its atoms are bonded together and the molecule has chemical properties that the individual atoms lack. Yet consider the solar system. Its parts — the sun, the planets, the moons, the asteroids — are separated by millions of miles of empty space. They are not in physical contact. They are not fastened or bonded in any mechanical or chemical sense; no glue, no rope, no weld holds them together. What holds the solar system together is gravitational attraction, a force that operates across a void without any physical medium. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the solar system is a genuine whole. It has emergent properties — a characteristic plane of revolution, a centre of mass, determinate orbital periods — that none of its parts individually possesses, and the third condition is satisfied. The definition has taken contact and mechanical bonding as necessary for wholehood, overlooking those wholes whose unity is constituted by non-contact forces operating across spatial separation.

definition: An entity E is a substance if and only if (i) E is ontologically independent — that is, E does not depend for its existence upon any other entity, (ii) E persists through time as a self-identical individual, and (iii) E is the bearer of properties without itself being a property of anything.
conditions: E is ontologically independent — it does not depend for its existence upon any other entity
E persists through time as a self-identical individual
E is the bearer of properties without itself being a property of anything
counterexample: Consider a living organism — a horse, let us say. The horse persists through time as a self-identical individual and is the bearer of properties without itself being a property of anything. Yet the horse is not ontologically independent in the requisite sense: it depends for its existence upon the continued availability of food, water, and air, and upon a particular range of environmental conditions without which it would cease to be. The first condition fails, yet the horse is a paradigmatic substance in any ordinary philosophical usage.
missing_condition: The definition requires complete ontological independence, but substances may depend upon external conditions for their continued existence whilst remaining substances. What is wanted is a notion of relative independence — independence from inhering in a subject — rather than absolute independence from all other entities.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a substance if and only if it is ontologically independent, it persists through time as a self-identical individual, and it is the bearer of properties without itself being a property of anything. The definition has considerable initial appeal. A lump of gold is ontologically independent; it does not depend for its existence upon any further entity in the manner that a colour depends upon a surface. It persists through time and bears properties — weight, lustre, malleability — without being itself a property of something else. Yet one must consider the case of a living organism, a horse, for instance. The horse persists through time as a self-identical individual and is the bearer of properties — it is brown, it is swift, it is of a certain weight — without being a property of anything. It is, by any ordinary philosophical reckoning, a substance. Yet the horse is not ontologically independent in the sense the definition requires. It depends for its continued existence upon the intake of food and water, upon the presence of breathable air, upon the maintenance of a body temperature within a narrow range, and upon a host of further environmental conditions. Remove any of these and the horse perishes. The first condition fails. The definition has confused two quite different notions of independence: the independence of a substance from inhering in a subject, which is the mark of substantiality, and the independence of a thing from all external conditions for its continued existence, which is a far stronger requirement that few if any entities in the natural world could satisfy.

definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C is temporally prior to E, (ii) C and E are spatially contiguous — that is, there is no spatial gap between them, and (iii) events of type C are invariably followed by events of type E.
conditions: C is temporally prior to E
C and E are spatially contiguous
events of type C are invariably followed by events of type E
counterexample: Consider the gravitational attraction between the sun and a distant comet. The sun's gravitational pull is the cause of the comet's altered trajectory, yet the sun and the comet are not spatially contiguous; they are separated by millions of miles of empty space. The second condition fails. Moreover, one might question whether the attraction is invariably followed by the same trajectory, since the precise path depends upon the comet's velocity and the positions of other bodies. Yet the sun's gravity is undeniably a cause of the comet's motion.
missing_condition: The definition requires spatial contiguity between cause and effect, but causation may operate across spatial distances, as in gravitational and electromagnetic interactions. What is wanted is a condition regarding the existence of a causal connection or mechanism, not the spatial adjacency of cause and effect.
passage: A familiar analysis holds that an event is a cause of another event if and only if the first is temporally prior to the second, the two are spatially contiguous, and events of the first type are invariably followed by events of the second type. This analysis captures a good many everyday cases of causation. When the billiard ball strikes a second ball and sets it in motion, the cause is prior in time, the balls are in contact at the moment of impact, and such collisions are invariably followed by the motion of the struck ball. Yet one must consider the gravitational attraction between the sun and a distant comet. The sun exerts a gravitational pull upon the comet, and this pull is the cause of the comet's altered trajectory as it sweeps through the solar system. The sun's gravitational influence is temporally prior to the alteration in the comet's path, or at least contemporaneous with it, and the regularity of the connection is beyond dispute. But the sun and the comet are not spatially contiguous; they are separated by vast reaches of empty space. There is no chain of contiguous bodies linking them; the gravitational influence operates, so far as our best physical theories inform us, across a distance. The second condition of the definition fails. The analysis has taken a feature of certain paradigmatic cases of causation — the spatial contact between colliding bodies — and elevated it to a necessary condition of all causation, when in truth some of the most fundamental causal processes in nature operate across spatial distances without any intervening contact.

definition: A temporal instant T is real if and only if (i) T is present — that is, T is the moment of direct experience and action, (ii) T is not merely a mathematical abstraction or a point in a coordinate system, and (iii) T is ontologically distinct from every other temporal instant.
conditions: T is present — the moment of direct experience and action
T is not merely a mathematical abstraction
T is ontologically distinct from every other temporal instant
counterexample: Consider the moment at which Caesar crossed the Rubicon. This moment is not present; it is irrecoverably past. Yet it would be strange to deny that it is a real temporal instant. Something genuinely occurred at that moment — a man led his army across a river, and the course of Roman history was altered. If the moment were not real, then the events located at it would have no temporal anchorage. The first condition fails, yet the moment is real in any philosophically significant sense.
missing_condition: The definition restricts reality to the present instant, but past and future instants may be equally real. What is wanted is an account of temporal reality that does not presuppose the privileged ontological status of the present.
passage: One might hold that a temporal instant is real if and only if it is present, it is not merely a mathematical abstraction, and it is ontologically distinct from every other temporal instant. The definition reflects a strong presentist intuition: only the present moment, the moment of direct experience and action, is real; the past has ceased to exist and the future does not yet exist. The present instant has a vividness and concreteness that mathematical points in a coordinate system manifestly lack; it is the locus of all that genuinely is. Yet one must consider the moment at which Caesar crossed the Rubicon. That moment is not present; it lies in the irrecoverable past, separated from the present by more than two millennia. By the lights of the definition, it is not a real temporal instant. But this conclusion is exceedingly difficult to accept. Something genuinely occurred at that moment. A man led his legions across a river in northern Italy, and the act set in motion a chain of consequences that shaped the history of the Western world. If the moment were not real, then the events located at it would have no temporal anchorage, no genuine position in the order of things. The first condition of the definition fails. The analysis has elevated a feature of one philosophical theory of time — the privileged ontological status of the present — to a necessary condition of temporal reality, when in truth the reality of past instants is attested by the reality of the events that occurred at them.

definition: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B share all the same intrinsic properties, (ii) A and B are composed of the same material constituents, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path linking A at t1 and B at t2.
conditions: A and B share all the same intrinsic properties
A and B are composed of the same material constituents
there is a continuous spatiotemporal path linking A at t1 and B at t2
counterexample: Consider an oak tree. The sapling planted fifty years ago and the great oak standing in its place today are the same tree — the same individual organism persisting through time. Yet the sapling and the mature oak share very few intrinsic properties: their height, mass, shape, and internal structure differ enormously. They are not composed of the same material constituents; the cells of the sapling have long since died and been replaced many times over. Only the third condition — spatiotemporal continuity — is satisfied. Yet the identity of the tree across time is not in doubt.
missing_condition: The definition requires sameness of intrinsic properties and material constituents, but diachronic identity may obtain even when properties and constituents change radically. What is essential may be the continuity of an organising principle or a life process, not the retention of particular properties or matter.
passage: Suppose one holds that an object at one time is identical with an object at a later time if and only if the two share all the same intrinsic properties, are composed of the same material constituents, and are linked by a continuous spatiotemporal path. The definition captures certain intuitions: we identify a golden ring found in a drawer with the ring placed there a year ago partly because it has the same weight, shape, and composition. Yet one must consider the case of the oak tree. A sapling is planted in a garden fifty years ago. Today, a great oak stands in its place — fifty feet tall, with a trunk three feet in diameter and a vast canopy of branches. We say, without hesitation, that this is the same tree that was planted half a century ago. It is the same individual, persisting through time. Yet the sapling and the mature oak share very few intrinsic properties. The sapling was two feet tall, slender, and supple; the oak is massive, rigid, and complex in structure. Their shapes, sizes, and internal configurations are wholly different. Nor are they composed of the same material constituents. The cells of the sapling have long since died and been replaced; the matter now composing the oak has been drawn from the soil, the water, and the air over decades of growth. Not a single atom of the original sapling need remain in the present tree. The first two conditions fail entirely. Only spatiotemporal continuity is preserved. The definition has required for diachronic identity a degree of constancy in properties and composition that living things — and indeed many inanimate objects — do not display, overlooking the possibility that identity through time may rest upon the continuity of a life process or an organising principle rather than upon the persistence of particular properties or particular matter.

definition: A universal U exists if and only if (i) U is instantiated — that is, there is at least one particular that exemplifies U, (ii) U is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) U is perceivable through sense experience of its instances.
conditions: U is instantiated — there is at least one particular that exemplifies U
U is wholly present in each of its instances
U is perceivable through sense experience of its instances
counterexample: Consider the universal of perfect circularity. No physical object is perfectly circular; every circle drawn or manufactured deviates, however slightly, from the mathematical ideal. The universal of perfect circularity has no instances in the physical world, and it is not perceivable through sense experience of any particular, since no particular perfectly exemplifies it. Yet geometers reason about perfect circularity, and their theorems are true. The first and third conditions fail, yet the universal appears to exist in some philosophically significant sense.
missing_condition: The definition requires instantiation and perceivability, but some universals may exist uninstantiated and may be grasped through intellectual apprehension rather than through the senses. What is wanted is an account that allows for the existence of universals that have no physical instances.
passage: One might propose that a universal exists if and only if it is instantiated by at least one particular, it is wholly present in each of its instances, and it is perceivable through sense experience of those instances. The definition has the merit of grounding universals in the concrete world. The universal redness exists because there are red things — roses, rubies, sunsets — and one perceives redness by perceiving them. The universal is not a remote abstraction but something encountered in the ordinary course of sensory experience. Yet one must consider the universal of perfect circularity. No physical object is perfectly circular. Every circle drawn with a compass, every wheel turned on a lathe, every planetary orbit traced through the heavens deviates, however minutely, from the mathematical ideal. The universal of perfect circularity is not instantiated by any physical particular. Nor is it perceivable through sense experience, for there is no instance of it to perceive. Yet perfect circularity appears to be a genuine universal. Geometers prove theorems about it; the ratio of its circumference to its diameter is a determinate and necessary truth. If perfect circularity does not exist, then it is difficult to see how geometrical truths about circles could be true. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. The analysis has taken the features of a certain class of universals — those that are exemplified in the physical world and accessible to the senses — and treated them as necessary conditions of the existence of universals as such, when in truth some universals may exist uninstantiated and may be grasped not through perception but through the intellect alone.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S is consistent with the laws of nature, (ii) S is consistent with the present state of the universe, and (iii) there exists some causal pathway by which S could be brought about from the present state.
conditions: S is consistent with the laws of nature
S is consistent with the present state of the universe
there exists some causal pathway by which S could be brought about from the present state
counterexample: Consider the possibility that the laws of nature might have been otherwise — that the gravitational constant might have had a different value, or that the speed of light might have been greater. Such possibilities are not consistent with the actual laws of nature; they require different laws. Nor is there any causal pathway from the present state of the universe to a state in which the laws are different. Yet it seems genuinely possible, in a robust metaphysical sense, that the laws of nature could have been otherwise. The first and third conditions fail.
missing_condition: The definition restricts possibility to what is physically achievable given actual natural laws, but metaphysical possibility extends beyond physical possibility. What is wanted is a broader notion that encompasses states of affairs that are logically or metaphysically coherent even if they contravene the actual laws of nature.
passage: One might hold that a state of affairs is possible if and only if it is consistent with the laws of nature, consistent with the present state of the universe, and there exists some causal pathway by which it could be brought about from the present state. This definition captures what one might call physical or nomological possibility. It is possible, in this sense, for water to freeze at a higher altitude, since such freezing is consistent with the laws of thermodynamics and could be brought about by transporting water to an elevated location. The definition has a reassuring concreteness. Yet one must consider a very different sort of possibility. Is it possible that the gravitational constant might have had a different value? Is it possible that the speed of light might have been greater or less than it is? These are not idle questions; physicists and philosophers alike have taken them seriously, and the study of possible worlds in which fundamental constants differ from their actual values is a thriving branch of philosophical cosmology. Yet such possibilities are not consistent with the actual laws of nature; they require that the laws be different from what they are. Nor is there any causal pathway from the present state of the universe to a state in which the gravitational constant has a different value. The first and third conditions fail. The definition has confined possibility to what is physically achievable under the actual laws, when in truth there appears to be a broader sense of possibility — metaphysical possibility, one might call it — in which it is possible for the laws themselves to have been otherwise, a sense that resists reduction to consistency with the laws as they happen to be.

definition: An object O persists through a change if and only if (i) O retains all of its essential properties before and after the change, (ii) the change affects only the accidental properties of O, and (iii) O is continuously observable throughout the process of change.
conditions: O retains all of its essential properties before and after the change
the change affects only the accidental properties of O
O is continuously observable throughout the process of change
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and emerges as a butterfly. During the pupal stage, the creature's body is almost entirely dissolved and reconstituted. It is not continuously observable as the same individual — inside the chrysalis, the organism bears no recognisable resemblance to either the caterpillar or the butterfly. And it is far from clear that all essential properties are retained; the caterpillar crawls, the butterfly flies, and their morphologies are radically different. Yet biologists rightly regard the butterfly as the same organism that was once a caterpillar. The first and third conditions fail.
missing_condition: The definition requires continuous observability and retention of essential properties, but an object may persist through radical transformation in which it is temporarily unrecognisable and its apparent essential properties are dramatically altered. What is wanted is a condition pertaining to biological or causal continuity rather than phenomenal constancy.
passage: Suppose one holds that an object persists through a change if and only if it retains all of its essential properties, the change affects only its accidental properties, and the object is continuously observable throughout the process of change. The definition reflects a natural conservatism: persistence requires that the thing remain fundamentally what it is, altering only in superficial respects, and that we be able to track it throughout the alteration. A bronze statue may be heated and cooled — its temperature changes, but its shape, its material, and its identity remain. Yet one must consider the metamorphosis of a caterpillar into a butterfly. The caterpillar crawls upon leaves, possesses a segmented body, and feeds voraciously. It then enters a chrysalis, within which its body is almost entirely dissolved — reduced to a formless soup of cells — before being reconstituted as a butterfly with wings, compound eyes, and a proboscis. The creature is not continuously observable as the same individual during this process; inside the chrysalis, the organism bears no recognisable resemblance to either the caterpillar that preceded it or the butterfly that will emerge. Nor is it evident that all essential properties are retained: the caterpillar's mode of locomotion, its bodily structure, and its manner of feeding are utterly transformed. Yet the butterfly is the same organism that was once the caterpillar. Biologists trace the continuity through genetic identity, through the unbroken sequence of cellular processes, and through the causal chain linking egg to larva to pupa to adult. The first and third conditions fail. The definition has demanded a degree of phenomenal constancy and essential stability that nature does not always provide, overlooking the possibility that persistence may rest upon underlying causal and biological continuity rather than upon the surface retention of recognisable properties.

definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C and E are both actual events — that is, both occur in the actual world, (ii) C is a sufficient condition for E — that is, given C, E must occur, and (iii) the occurrence of C temporally precedes the occurrence of E.
conditions: C and E are both actual events
C is a sufficient condition for E — given C, E must occur
C temporally precedes E
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man strikes a match and it lights. We say that the striking caused the lighting. But the striking of the match was not, by itself, a sufficient condition for the lighting. Had the match been damp, or had there been no oxygen in the surrounding air, or had the striking been too feeble, the match would not have lit. The striking was a cause of the lighting only in conjunction with a host of background conditions — the dryness of the match, the presence of oxygen, the firmness of the stroke — none of which is ordinarily called the cause. The second condition fails: the putative cause is not a sufficient condition for the effect.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the cause be a sufficient condition for the effect, but in ordinary cases no single event is sufficient for another; causation operates against a background of enabling conditions. What is wanted is a notion of causation that distinguishes the cause from the background conditions without requiring that the cause alone suffice for the effect.
passage: Suppose one holds that an event is a cause of another if and only if both are actual events, the first is a sufficient condition for the second, and the first temporally precedes the second. The definition appears to capture the intuitive idea of causation as a relation of necessitation: given the cause, the effect must follow. A heated iron bar expands; the heating is sufficient for the expansion, and the heating precedes the expansion. One might be tempted to regard this as the paradigm of all causation. Yet one must consider the case of a person who strikes a match and the match lights. We say without hesitation that the striking caused the lighting. The striking is an actual event, the lighting is an actual event, and the striking precedes the lighting. But was the striking a sufficient condition for the lighting? It was not. Had the match been damp, it would not have lit despite the striking. Had the surrounding atmosphere been devoid of oxygen, the match would not have lit. Had the friction surface been worn smooth, the match would not have lit. Had the stroke been too gentle, the match would not have lit. The striking of the match was not, by itself, sufficient for the lighting; it was sufficient only in conjunction with a considerable number of background conditions — the dryness of the match, the presence of oxygen, the roughness of the striking surface, the adequate force of the stroke — and these background conditions are not ordinarily called the cause. The second condition of the definition fails. No single event in the actual world is, strictly speaking, sufficient by itself for any other event. The definition has confused the relation of a cause to its effect with the relation of a logically sufficient condition to its consequent, overlooking the fact that causes operate against a background of enabling conditions and are not, in isolation, sufficient for anything.

definition: An object O persists through time if and only if (i) O exists at two or more distinct times, (ii) O retains its essential properties — that is, the properties without which O would cease to be the kind of thing it is — at every time at which it exists, and (iii) O occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region with no temporal gaps.
conditions: O exists at two or more distinct times
O retains its essential properties at every time at which it exists
O occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region with no temporal gaps
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that undergoes metamorphosis and emerges as a butterfly. The organism persists — it is the same individual animal before and after the transformation. Yet the essential properties of the caterpillar — its worm-like body plan, its crawling locomotion, its feeding upon leaves — are not retained by the butterfly. The butterfly has wings, compound eyes suited to flight, a proboscis for drinking nectar, and a wholly different mode of life. If the essential properties of the caterpillar defined the kind of thing it is, then the butterfly is not the same kind of thing. The second condition fails, yet the organism persists.
missing_condition: The definition requires the retention of essential properties, but objects may persist through radical changes that transform their essential properties, as in biological metamorphosis. What is wanted is an account of persistence that accommodates the possibility of transformation without loss of identity.
passage: One might propose that an object persists through time if and only if it exists at two or more distinct times, retains its essential properties at every time at which it exists, and occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region with no temporal gaps. The definition has a certain philosophical appeal, for it ties persistence to the preservation of what is essential — the features without which the object would cease to be the kind of thing it is. A lump of gold persists so long as it remains gold; a clock persists so long as it retains its capacity to tell the time; a house persists so long as it retains the structure that makes it habitable. Yet one must consider the caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and undergoes metamorphosis. The caterpillar is a worm-like creature, soft-bodied and many-legged, that crawls upon leaves and devours them. Within the chrysalis, its body is very largely dissolved and reorganised. What emerges is a butterfly — a winged creature with a wholly different body plan, different sense organs, different locomotion, and a different mode of feeding. The organism persists through this transformation; it is the same individual animal, the same biological particular, that was a caterpillar and is now a butterfly. Its spatiotemporal continuity is unbroken. But its essential properties — if we take essential properties to be those without which it would cease to be the kind of thing it is — have been utterly transformed. The worm-like body, the crawling locomotion, the leaf-eating habit — all are gone. If these properties were essential to the caterpillar, then the butterfly is not the same kind of thing as the caterpillar, and the second condition fails. The definition has assumed that persistence requires the conservation of a fixed set of essential properties, when in truth organisms and many other objects may persist through transformations so radical that nothing recognisable remains of the earlier stage, save the unbroken continuity of the underlying process.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in the actual world, (ii) the negation of P is logically self-contradictory, and (iii) P is knowable a priori — that is, P can be known to be true independently of experience.
conditions: P is true in the actual world
the negation of P is logically self-contradictory
P is knowable a priori
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. This proposition is, by wide philosophical consensus, necessarily true: water could not have been composed of anything other than hydrogen and oxygen and still be water. It is true in the actual world. But its negation is not logically self-contradictory; there is no formal contradiction in the sentence 'water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen,' for the connection between the term 'water' and its chemical composition is not a matter of logic but of empirical discovery. Nor is the proposition knowable a priori; it was discovered through chemical experiment. The second and third conditions fail, yet the proposition is necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition conflates necessity with logical truth and a priori knowability, but some necessary truths are discovered empirically and their negations are not logically contradictory. What is wanted is a notion of necessity that accommodates necessary a posteriori truths — truths that hold of necessity but are known through experience.
passage: Suppose one holds that a proposition is necessarily true if and only if it is true in the actual world, its negation is logically self-contradictory, and it is knowable a priori. The definition captures a prominent class of necessary truths. The proposition that all bachelors are unmarried is true, its negation involves a contradiction — for a married bachelor is a contradiction in terms — and one can know its truth independently of experience, simply by grasping the meanings of the words. The proposition that no object is both wholly red and wholly green at the same time seems similarly to satisfy the three conditions. But one must consider the proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. This proposition is, by wide philosophical agreement, necessarily true. Water is essentially H2O; a substance that was not composed of hydrogen and oxygen would not be water, however closely it might resemble water in its superficial properties. The proposition is true in the actual world. But its negation is not logically self-contradictory. There is no formal contradiction in the sentence, 'Water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen.' The terms 'water,' 'hydrogen,' and 'oxygen' are not related as 'bachelor' and 'unmarried' are related; the connection between them is not a matter of definition or logical form but of the deep structure of the natural world, discovered by chemical investigation. The second condition fails. Nor is the proposition knowable a priori. No amount of armchair reflection upon the concept of water would reveal its chemical composition. The truth was discovered empirically — through the experiments of Cavendish, Lavoisier, and their successors — and could not have been known otherwise. The third condition fails. The definition has identified necessity with logical truth and a priori knowability, when in truth some of the most important necessary truths are known only through empirical investigation and are not grounded in logic or in the meanings of words.

definition: Two or more objects compose a further object W if and only if (i) the objects are in spatial contact with one another, (ii) the objects are arranged in a manner that serves some recognisable function or purpose, and (iii) the objects are of the same general kind — that is, they share a common nature or material.
conditions: the objects are in spatial contact with one another
the objects are arranged in a manner that serves some recognisable function or purpose
the objects are of the same general kind — they share a common nature or material
counterexample: Consider a bird's nest. A nest is composed of twigs, leaves, feathers, scraps of moss, and perhaps fragments of string or paper — materials of diverse kinds that share no common nature. The components are in spatial contact and are arranged in a manner that serves a function, but the third condition fails: the parts are not of the same general kind. Yet the nest is a composite whole; it is an object composed of parts.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the parts share a common nature, but composite objects may be composed of heterogeneous materials. What is wanted is an account that does not restrict composition to objects of the same kind.
passage: One might hold that two or more objects compose a further object if and only if the objects are in spatial contact with one another, they are arranged so as to serve some recognisable function or purpose, and they are of the same general kind — that is, they share a common nature or material. The definition captures certain cases of composition tolerably well. A wall is composed of bricks that are in contact with one another, arranged so as to serve the function of enclosure or support, and all of the same kind — they are all bricks, all made of clay. A necklace is composed of pearls strung together, in contact, arranged for the purpose of adornment, and all of the same kind — they are all pearls. One might suppose that these features characterise composition generally. Yet one must consider the case of a bird's nest. A nest is, by any ordinary account, a composite object — an object composed of parts. Its parts are twigs, leaves, feathers, scraps of moss, strands of grass, and perhaps fragments of string or paper gathered by the bird from its surroundings. The parts are in spatial contact; they are interwoven and pressed together. They are arranged so as to serve a recognisable function — the housing and protection of eggs and young. But they are not of the same general kind. Twigs are woody, leaves are membranous, feathers are keratinous, moss is a plant of a quite different sort from the grasses, and string is a manufactured article. The parts share no common nature or material; they are as heterogeneous a collection as one could well assemble. The third condition of the definition fails. The definition has taken a feature of certain particularly homogeneous composite objects — walls of brick, strings of pearls — and treated it as a necessary condition of composition as such, when in truth the world is full of composite objects whose parts are drawn from diverse kinds and share nothing beyond their spatial arrangement and their contribution to a common function.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular entity — that is, the existence of X does not logically require the existence of any other concrete particular, (ii) X is the bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything, and (iii) X persists through time as a numerically identical individual.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular entity
X is the bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything
X persists through time as a numerically identical individual
counterexample: Consider a wave upon the surface of the ocean. The wave has properties — a certain height, a certain velocity, a certain wavelength — and it is not itself a property of anything; one does not say that the ocean is wave-like in the way that one says a rose is red. The wave persists through time as a numerically identical individual: we track a particular wave as it travels across miles of open water, and we speak of the same wave arriving at the shore that was generated by a distant storm. Yet the wave does not exist independently of any other particular entity. Its existence logically requires the existence of the water through which it propagates; without the ocean there is no wave. The first condition fails, yet the wave possesses many of the marks of substance — it bears properties and persists as an individual through time.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a substance exist independently of every other particular, but entities such as waves, which depend upon a medium for their existence, may nevertheless function as subjects of predication and as persisting individuals. What is wanted is an account that distinguishes genuine substances from mere properties without requiring absolute ontological independence.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a substance if and only if it exists independently of any other particular entity, it is the bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything, and it persists through time as a numerically identical individual.
