The definition has considerable historical support. A block of marble exists independently of the table upon which it rests; it bears properties — whiteness, hardness, a certain weight — and it is not itself a property of anything; and it persists as the selfsame block through changes in its environment. A horse, likewise, exists in its own right, bears innumerable properties, and remains the same horse from foal to old age. One might suppose that these features — independence, property-bearing, and persistence — jointly capture what it is to be a substance. Yet one must consider a wave upon the surface of the ocean. The wave has properties of its own: a certain height measured from trough to crest, a velocity determined by the depth of the water and the force of the wind, a wavelength that can be measured with precision. The wave is not itself a property of the ocean; we do not predicate the wave of the water in the way that we predicate blueness of the sky. And the wave persists through time as a numerically identical individual. A mariner may track a particular wave as it crosses hundreds of miles of open sea, identify it by its distinctive profile, and watch it arrive at the shore — the very same wave that was generated by a storm far out in the Atlantic. Yet the wave does not exist independently of any other particular entity. Its existence is logically dependent upon the existence of the water through which it propagates. If the ocean were annihilated, the wave would be annihilated with it; the wave cannot be separated from its medium and set down elsewhere. The first condition of the definition fails. The definition has taken a feature of paradigmatic substances — their independence from any particular medium — and elevated it to a necessary condition of substance as such, when in truth there may be entities that bear properties and persist as individuals whilst depending for their existence upon a supporting medium.

definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous — that is, there is no gap in space or time between the occurrence of C and the occurrence of E, and (iii) events of the type of C are invariably followed by events of the type of E under relevantly similar conditions.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
events of the type of C are invariably followed by events of the type of E under relevantly similar conditions
counterexample: Consider the case of a person who drinks from a poisoned well. He drinks at noon; the poison enters his blood and is carried slowly through his body; twelve hours later, at midnight, he dies. The drinking is the cause of the death. Yet the cause and the effect are not spatiotemporally contiguous. Between the drinking and the dying there is a gap of twelve hours, during which the man moves about, converses, and occupies many different places. The poison operates through a chain of intermediate processes, but the cause — the drinking — and the effect — the death — are separated by a considerable interval. The second condition fails, yet the causal relation between the drinking and the dying is not in doubt.
missing_condition: The definition requires that cause and effect be spatiotemporally contiguous, but many causal relations operate across gaps in space and time through chains of intermediate processes. What is wanted is an account that permits causation to be transmitted through a series of intervening links without requiring direct contiguity between the initial cause and the final effect.
passage: One might hold that an event is the cause of another if and only if the first temporally precedes the second, the two are spatiotemporally contiguous, and events of the first type are invariably followed by events of the second type under relevantly similar conditions. The definition captures a good many familiar instances of causation. The striking of a match is contiguous with its ignition; the pressing of a lever is contiguous with the movement of the mechanism; the collision of one billiard ball with another is contiguous with the second ball's motion. In each case, the cause immediately precedes the effect, the two are adjacent in space and time, and a regularity of the relevant kind obtains. Yet one must consider the case of a person who drinks from a poisoned well at noon. The water contains a slow-acting toxin. The man drinks, goes about his business, returns home, dines, reads, retires to bed, and at midnight dies. The drinking of the poisoned water is the cause of the death; no one who knows the circumstances would hesitate to affirm the causal connexion. But the cause and the effect are not spatiotemporally contiguous. Twelve hours separate the drinking from the dying. The man has moved from the well to his house, has occupied many places and passed through many states in the interval. The poison, to be sure, operates through a chain of intermediate chemical processes — absorption into the blood, transport to the organs, disruption of cellular function — but the cause, as we ordinarily identify it, is the drinking, and the effect is the death, and between these two events there is a gap of twelve hours and a change of location. The second condition of the definition fails. The definition has modelled causation upon cases of immediate, contiguous action, in which the cause produces the effect without delay or intervening distance, and has overlooked the many instances in which causation operates through extended chains of intermediate processes, so that the original cause and the ultimate effect are widely separated in space and time.

definition: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the events constituting T have taken place, (ii) T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now, and (iii) T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it.
conditions: T has occurred — the events constituting T have taken place
T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now
T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it
counterexample: Consider the formation of the earth approximately four and a half thousand million years ago. This event occurred; it is no longer present; and it is unquestionably in the past. Yet no conscious being experienced it, and consequently it is not preserved in any being's memory. There were no observers — no minds of any sort — when the molten rock condensed and the first crust formed. The third condition fails entirely, yet the event is as firmly in the past as any event could be.
missing_condition: The definition ties the pastness of a moment to its preservation in some conscious being's memory, but the past extends far beyond the reach of any consciousness. What is wanted is an account of pastness that does not make the existence of the past dependent upon the contingent existence of minds.
passage: One might propose that a moment is in the past if and only if it has occurred, it is no longer present, and it is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it. The definition has a certain intuitive appeal. When we think of past events — a childhood birthday, a morning walk last Tuesday, the signing of a treaty — we think of events that have occurred, that are no longer occurring, and that are preserved in the memories of those who were present. Memory seems to be the faculty by which the past is kept alive; without it, one might suppose, the past would be nothing at all — a mere absence, a gap where something once was. Yet one must consider the formation of the earth. This event occurred some four and a half thousand million years ago, long before any conscious being existed. The molten rock condensed from a cloud of dust and gas; the surface cooled; the first crust solidified over millions of years. These events have occurred and are no longer present. They are, by any reasonable account, in the past. But no conscious being experienced them; no memory preserves them. We infer their occurrence from the geological record — from the composition of rocks, the decay of radioactive isotopes, the analysis of meteorites — but this inference is not memory. Memory requires a conscious being who was present at the event and who retains an impression of it, and there was no such being when the earth was formed. The third condition of the definition fails. The definition has confused the means by which we most commonly apprehend the past — namely memory — with the constitution of pastness itself. A moment does not require a witness in order to have occurred and to be no longer occurring; the past extends incomparably beyond the reach of any consciousness, and most of what has happened has happened without any mind to observe it.

definition: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are composed of the same material parts, (ii) A and B have the same form or structure — that is, their parts are arranged in the same way, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 with B at t2 along which no disruption of existence occurs.
conditions: A and B are composed of the same material parts
A and B have the same form or structure
there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 with B at t2
counterexample: Consider the human body. A man at the age of sixty is, by every ordinary reckoning, the same man who at the age of twenty celebrated his birthday. He bears the same name, possesses the same memories, and is recognised by his friends and relations. Yet the material parts of his body have been almost entirely replaced in the intervening forty years. The cells that compose his tissues have been renewed many times over; the atoms that constitute his flesh, his bones, his blood are not the atoms that constituted them four decades earlier. The first condition fails. He is the same man, yet he is not composed of the same material parts.
missing_condition: The definition requires identity of material composition, but living organisms and many artefacts persist through the wholesale replacement of their material parts. What is wanted is an account of identity through time that allows for the gradual replacement of matter provided that some further condition — perhaps continuity of organisation or of causal processes — is satisfied.
passage: One might hold that an object at one time is identical with an object at another time if and only if the two are composed of the same material parts, they have the same form or structure, and there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting them. The definition captures certain straightforward cases. A block of granite quarried from the hillside and placed in a field is, twenty years later, the same block of granite, provided it is composed of the same stone, retains the same form, and has occupied its position continuously. The identity of simple, unchanging objects seems to rest upon precisely these three conditions. Yet one must consider the human body. A man at sixty is the same man who at twenty blew out the candles on his birthday cake. His friends recognise him; the law holds him responsible for contracts entered into decades earlier; he remembers events from his youth as things that happened to him. But the material composition of his body has been almost entirely replaced in the intervening forty years. The atoms that composed his bones at twenty are not the atoms that compose them at sixty; the cells of his skin, his blood, his organs have been renewed many times over through the ordinary processes of biological maintenance. Physiologists estimate that in the course of seven to ten years the material substance of the human body is very nearly completely replaced. The first condition of the definition fails outright: the man at sixty is not composed of the same material parts as the man at twenty. Yet his identity through time is not in serious doubt. The definition has taken the persistence of material composition — which suffices for the identity of inert objects like stones — and treated it as a necessary condition of all identity through time, overlooking the fact that living organisms persist precisely by replacing their material parts in a continuous process of renewal and repair.

definition: A universal U exists if and only if (i) U is instantiated — that is, there is at least one particular that exemplifies U, (ii) U can be simultaneously present in more than one particular at the same time, and (iii) U is perceivable — that is, U can be apprehended through sense experience when it is instantiated in a particular.
conditions: U is instantiated — at least one particular exemplifies U
U can be simultaneously present in more than one particular
U is perceivable through sense experience when instantiated
counterexample: Consider the property of being a prime number. Primality is instantiated: the number seven is prime, as are two, three, five, and infinitely many others. Primality can be simultaneously present in more than one particular: seven and eleven are both prime at the same time. Yet primality is not perceivable through sense experience. One does not see or hear or touch the primality of a number. One grasps it through intellectual apprehension — through understanding the definition of a prime number and verifying that a given number satisfies it. The third condition fails, yet primality appears to be a genuine universal if any universal is.
missing_condition: The definition requires that universals be perceivable through sense experience, but mathematical and logical properties — which are among the strongest candidates for genuine universals — are apprehended through the intellect rather than through the senses. What is wanted is an account that admits both sensible and intelligible universals.
passage: One might propose that a universal exists if and only if it is instantiated in at least one particular, it can be simultaneously present in more than one particular, and it is perceivable through sense experience when so instantiated. The definition has a certain empiricist respectability. Redness is instantiated in this rose and that pillar-box; it is simultaneously present in both; and one perceives the redness of the rose by looking at it. Roundness is instantiated in this ball and that wheel; it is simultaneously exemplified by both; and one perceives the roundness of the ball by sight or by touch. One might suppose that a universal is, in essence, a repeatable feature of the sensible world — something that one can encounter again and again in experience, always the same quality showing up in different particulars. Yet one must consider the property of being a prime number. Primality is instantiated: the number seven exemplifies it, as do two, three, five, eleven, thirteen, and infinitely many others. Primality can be simultaneously present in more than one particular: at this very moment, seven is prime and eleven is prime and forty-three is prime. The first two conditions of the definition are satisfied. But primality is not perceivable through sense experience. One does not see the primality of seven in the way that one sees the redness of a rose. One does not hear it or touch it or taste it. One grasps it through the intellect — by understanding the definition of a prime number as a number divisible only by itself and one, and by verifying through reasoning that seven satisfies this definition. The apprehension of primality is an act of thought, not of perception. The third condition of the definition fails. The definition has modelled universals upon the sensible qualities of physical objects — colours, shapes, textures — and has required perceivability as a condition of existence, thereby excluding the vast domain of mathematical and logical properties that are apprehended through reason rather than through the senses, yet which have as strong a claim to universality as any sensible quality.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S does not involve a logical contradiction, (ii) S is compatible with the fundamental laws of nature, and (iii) there exists or has existed a physical mechanism capable of bringing about S.
conditions: S does not involve a logical contradiction
S is compatible with the fundamental laws of nature
there exists or has existed a physical mechanism capable of bringing about S
counterexample: Consider the state of affairs in which the gravitational constant has a value slightly different from its actual value — say, one per cent greater. This state of affairs does not involve a logical contradiction; there is nothing self-contradictory in the idea that the constant might have been different. Yet it is not compatible with the fundamental laws of nature as they actually obtain, since altering a fundamental constant would alter the laws themselves. And there exists no physical mechanism, within the universe as it is, capable of bringing about a different value of the gravitational constant. Both the second and third conditions fail. Yet the state of affairs appears to be genuinely possible in the broadest metaphysical sense: it is conceivable, it involves no incoherence, and it is the sort of thing one discusses intelligibly in physical cosmology.
missing_condition: The definition restricts possibility to what is compatible with the actual laws of nature and achievable by actual physical mechanisms, but genuine metaphysical possibility extends beyond the actual laws to include states of affairs in which the laws themselves might have been different. What is wanted is an account that distinguishes logical possibility from nomological possibility and recognises the broader reach of the former.
passage: One might hold that a state of affairs is possible if and only if it involves no logical contradiction, it is compatible with the fundamental laws of nature, and there exists or has existed a physical mechanism capable of bringing about it. The definition captures a large class of possibilities that concern us in everyday life and in the sciences. It is possible that a coin, when tossed, should land on heads: this involves no contradiction, is compatible with the laws of mechanics, and the mechanism is the familiar one of a flicked coin spinning through the air. It is possible that a new species of beetle should evolve in the forests of Borneo: no contradiction, no violation of natural law, and the mechanisms of mutation and natural selection are well understood. Yet one must consider the state of affairs in which the gravitational constant has a value slightly different from its actual value — let us say, one per cent greater than it is. Physicists and cosmologists discuss such possibilities freely. They ask what the universe would look like if the gravitational constant were stronger or weaker, how the formation of stars and galaxies would be affected, whether life could arise under different physical constants. These are intelligible questions, and the states of affairs they describe seem genuinely possible; there is nothing incoherent or self-contradictory in the idea that gravity might have been stronger. Yet the state of affairs is not compatible with the fundamental laws of nature as they actually are, since the value of the gravitational constant is woven into the very fabric of those laws; to alter the constant is to alter the laws. And there exists no physical mechanism, within the actual universe, capable of producing a different value of the gravitational constant. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has conflated possibility with what is achievable within the actual physical order, overlooking the broader sense of possibility in which the physical order itself might have been otherwise.

definition: An object O undergoes change if and only if (i) O possesses a property P at time t1, (ii) O lacks P at a later time t2, and (iii) O remains numerically the same object throughout the interval from t1 to t2.
conditions: O possesses a property P at time t1
O lacks P at a later time t2
O remains numerically the same object throughout the interval
counterexample: Consider a region of empty space. At time t1 the region is dark and cold — no radiation passes through it. At time t2 a beam of light traverses the region, and it is illuminated and warm. The region possesses a property at t2 — being illuminated — that it lacked at t1. Yet it appears strained to say that the region of space is a numerically identical object that has undergone change. A region of space is not an object in the ordinary sense; it does not bear properties in the way that a stone or a tree does, and it does not persist as a numerically identical individual in the manner presupposed by the definition. The condition of numerical identity seems to fail, or at best to apply only by a considerable stretch of the concept. Yet something has changed in that region; the state of affairs there is different at t2 from what it was at t1.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes that change is always the alteration of a persisting object, but change may occur in regions, fields, or states of affairs that are not objects in any robust sense. What is wanted is an account of change that encompasses alterations of spatial regions and field values as well as alterations of ordinary objects.
passage: One might propose that an object undergoes change if and only if it possesses a property at one time, lacks that property at a later time, and remains numerically the same object throughout the interval. The definition seems to capture the paradigmatic cases admirably. A leaf changes colour: it is green in summer and red in autumn, and it is the same leaf throughout. A piece of iron changes temperature: it is cold in the morning and hot after being placed in the fire, and it is the selfsame piece of iron. In each case, an object persists through time and gains or loses a property. Yet one must consider a region of empty space — a cubic metre, let us say, far from any star, through which no radiation passes and in which no matter resides. At time t1 this region is dark and cold; nothing is happening there. At time t2 a beam of light from a distant supernova traverses the region, and the region is illuminated and warm. The state of affairs in that region has changed: at t1 there was darkness, at t2 there is light. One is tempted to say that the region has undergone change, that something is different there. But the region of space is not an object in the ordinary sense. One does not point to a region of empty space and say 'that thing' in the way one points to a stone or a horse. The notion of numerical identity — the idea that the region is the selfsame individual at t2 as it was at t1 — does not apply straightforwardly, for a region of space is not an individual that persists through time in the way that a horse or a stone persists. The third condition of the definition sits uneasily with the case. The definition has modelled change upon the alteration of ordinary objects — things that have clear criteria of identity and that persist as recognisable individuals — and has overlooked the possibility that change may occur in regions, fields, or distributions that are not objects in any robust sense, yet in which genuine alterations of state undeniably take place.

definition: An object O persists through an interval of time if and only if (i) O exists at the beginning of the interval, (ii) O exists at the end of the interval, and (iii) at every moment within the interval there is some spatial region continuously occupied by O.
conditions: O exists at the beginning of the interval
O exists at the end of the interval
at every moment within the interval there is some spatial region continuously occupied by O
counterexample: Consider a watch that is taken apart by a watchmaker for cleaning. At the beginning of the week the watch exists on the customer's wrist. On Monday the watchmaker disassembles it entirely: every spring, wheel, cog, and plate is removed, cleaned separately, and laid upon the workbench. For three days the watch does not exist as an assembled whole — its parts are scattered across the bench, and no spatial region is occupied by anything that could be called the watch. On Friday the watchmaker reassembles the parts in their original arrangement, and the watch exists once more. The customer collects the same watch he brought in on Monday. The watch persists through the week — it is the same watch at the end as at the beginning — yet the third condition fails: there were three days during which no spatial region was continuously occupied by the watch.
missing_condition: The definition requires continuous spatial occupation, but objects may persist through intervals during which they are temporarily disassembled, provided their parts are reassembled in the appropriate way. What is wanted is an account that allows for gaps in spatial occupation when there is continuity of parts and of the intention to reassemble.
passage: One might hold that an object persists through an interval of time if and only if it exists at the beginning and at the end of the interval, and at every moment within the interval some spatial region is continuously occupied by it. The definition seems unexceptionable for many cases. A mountain persists through the centuries, occupying its region of the earth's surface without interruption. A book on a shelf persists from one year to the next, occupying a region of space in the bookcase continuously. The notion of persistence seems bound up with the continuous occupation of space — an object that vanished entirely for a time and then reappeared would seem, intuitively, to be a new object rather than the old one returning. Yet one must consider the case of a watch brought to a watchmaker for a thorough cleaning. On Monday the watchmaker disassembles the watch entirely. Every component — every spring, every wheel, every jewel, every screw — is removed, cleaned individually, and placed upon the workbench. For three days the watch does not exist as an assembled whole. Its parts lie scattered across the bench; no spatial region is occupied by anything that could be called the watch. On Friday the watchmaker reassembles the parts in their original arrangement, and the watch exists once more. The customer collects it, wears it, and refers to it as the same watch he has owned for twenty years. And he is right to do so: it is the same watch. The same parts, in the same arrangement, performing the same function, bearing the same serial number. The watch has persisted through the week. Yet the third condition of the definition fails: for three days no spatial region was continuously occupied by the watch. The definition has taken continuity of spatial occupation — which suffices for the persistence of mountains and books — and elevated it to a necessary condition of all persistence, overlooking the fact that some objects may survive temporary disassembly provided their parts are preserved and correctly reassembled.

definition: A collection of objects C constitutes a whole W if and only if (i) the members of C are in spatial contact with one another, (ii) the members of C are arranged in a stable configuration — that is, a configuration that resists disruption under normal conditions, and (iii) the members of C jointly perform a function that none of them performs individually.
conditions: the members of C are in spatial contact with one another
the members of C are arranged in a stable configuration
the members of C jointly perform a function that none performs individually
counterexample: Consider a flock of geese flying in formation across the autumn sky. The geese are not in spatial contact with one another; each bird is separated from its neighbours by several feet of air. Their configuration is not stable in the required sense; it shifts and reforms as the birds adjust their positions, and a gust of wind may scatter the formation temporarily. Yet the flock is a whole of a kind. We refer to it as a single entity — 'a flock' — we track it across the sky, we say that the flock has changed direction or that the flock has landed. The flock, moreover, achieves something that no individual goose achieves alone: it reduces the aerodynamic drag upon each member, enabling the birds to fly farther than any one of them could fly in isolation. The third condition is satisfied, but the first two fail, yet the flock is a recognisable whole.
missing_condition: The definition requires spatial contact and stable configuration, but some wholes — flocks, swarms, constellations of cooperating agents — are composed of spatially separated and dynamically rearranging members. What is wanted is an account of composition that allows for wholes constituted by relational or functional unity rather than by spatial contiguity and rigidity.
passage: One might propose that a collection of objects constitutes a whole if and only if its members are in spatial contact, they are arranged in a stable configuration, and they jointly perform a function that none performs individually. The definition captures many familiar cases. The parts of a clock — the springs, the gears, the hands, the case — are in contact, stably arranged, and jointly perform the function of keeping time. The bricks of a wall are in contact, stably stacked, and jointly perform the function of bearing a load. One might suppose that these features — contact, stability, joint function — are what distinguish a genuine whole from a mere aggregate. Yet one must consider a flock of geese flying in formation across the autumn sky. The geese are not in spatial contact; each bird is separated from its neighbours by several feet of open air. Their configuration is not stable in the sense required by the definition; it shifts and reforms as the birds adjust their positions, now stretching into a long line, now contracting into a tight wedge, now scattering momentarily before reassembling. The arrangement resists disruption only in a loose, statistical sense — the formation tends to re-form, but it is never rigid. Yet the flock is a whole. We refer to it as a single entity: we say 'a flock of geese flew over the house' as naturally as we say 'a clock stood on the mantelpiece.' We track the flock as it moves across the landscape, and we attribute properties to it — its direction, its altitude, its size. Moreover, the flock achieves something that no individual goose achieves alone: by flying in formation, each bird benefits from the aerodynamic uplift created by the wingbeats of the bird ahead, and the flock as a whole can cover distances that would exhaust any single member. The third condition is satisfied, but the first two fail. The definition has modelled composition upon rigid, contiguous assemblages — clocks, walls, machines — and has overlooked the possibility that a whole may be constituted by entities that are spatially separated and dynamically rearranging, held together not by contact and rigidity but by a pattern of coordinated behaviour.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in the actual world, (ii) the denial of P is inconceivable — that is, no coherent state of affairs corresponding to the denial of P can be imagined, and (iii) P is knowable by a priori reasoning alone, without recourse to empirical investigation.
conditions: P is true in the actual world
the denial of P is inconceivable
P is knowable by a priori reasoning alone
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H2O. This proposition is, by wide philosophical agreement, necessarily true: in any possible circumstance in which something is water, it has the molecular composition H2O. The proposition is true in the actual world. Yet its denial is not inconceivable. One can coherently imagine — as philosophers have shown at length — a world in which the clear, potable liquid that fills the rivers and lakes has a different molecular composition entirely. It is only through empirical chemistry, not through the mere exercise of the imagination, that we learn that such a liquid would not be water. And the proposition is not knowable by a priori reasoning alone; it was discovered through the empirical investigations of Cavendish and Lavoisier. Both the second and third conditions fail, yet the proposition is necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition equates necessity with inconceivability of denial and a priori knowability, but some necessary truths are discoverable only through empirical investigation and their denials are perfectly conceivable. What is wanted is an account that separates the metaphysical status of a proposition from the means by which it is known.
passage: One might hold that a proposition is necessarily true if and only if it is true in the actual world, its denial is inconceivable, and it is knowable by a priori reasoning alone. The definition accords well with many classic examples of necessary truth. The proposition that no bachelor is married is true, its denial is inconceivable — one cannot coherently imagine a married bachelor — and it is knowable by reflection upon the meanings of the words, without any recourse to experience. The proposition that two plus three equals five is true, its denial is inconceivable, and it is knowable by a priori reasoning. One might suppose that necessity, inconceivability of denial, and a priori knowability always go together. Yet one must consider the proposition that water is H2O. Modern chemistry has established that water is composed of two atoms of hydrogen and one of oxygen, bonded in a particular molecular arrangement. This proposition is, by wide philosophical consensus, necessarily true: any substance that lacked this composition would not be water, regardless of its superficial resemblance to water — its clarity, its potability, its behaviour as a solvent. But the denial of this proposition is not inconceivable. One can coherently imagine a world in which the clear, tasteless, thirst-quenching liquid that fills the lakes and rivers has a wholly different molecular composition — some compound unknown to our chemistry. The imagination does not rebel; the scenario can be described in perfect detail without contradiction. It is only through empirical investigation — through the experiments of chemists who decomposed water into its elements — that we learn that such a liquid would not be water. And the proposition is not knowable by a priori reasoning. No amount of conceptual analysis could have revealed that water is H2O; the discovery required laboratory work, careful measurement, and the development of atomic theory. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has conflated the metaphysical status of a proposition — whether it is necessarily true — with the manner in which it is known, assuming that what is necessary must be a priori and that what is a priori must be such that its denial is inconceivable, when in truth these notions come apart.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular entity — that is, X does not depend for its existence upon being a property or mode of something else, (ii) X persists through time as a numerically identical individual, and (iii) X is the ultimate subject of predication — properties are predicated of X, but X is not itself predicated of anything.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular entity
X persists through time as a numerically identical individual
X is the ultimate subject of predication
counterexample: Consider a particular ocean wave. The wave exists independently of any other particular wave; it is not a property or mode of another wave. It persists through time as a seemingly identical individual — one can track it for minutes or hours as it travels across the sea. And properties are predicated of it — one says the wave is tall, or fast, or breaking — while it is not itself predicated of anything else. Yet a wave is not a substance in any robust sense; it is a pattern of motion through a medium, and the water that composes it at one moment is entirely different from the water that composes it at another. The three conditions are satisfied, but the wave lacks the material self-sufficiency we expect of a substance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the entity be composed of the same underlying matter or constituents throughout its persistence, or that it possess an intrinsic principle of unity distinct from the organisation of an external medium.
passage: One might propose that an entity qualifies as a substance if and only if it exists independently of any other particular entity, persists through time as a numerically identical individual, and serves as the ultimate subject of predication. This definition has deep roots in the philosophical tradition: the first condition captures the intuition that substances are ontologically self-standing, the second ensures that a substance is not a fleeting occurrence but an enduring thing, and the third reflects the logical observation that substances are those things of which we speak when we ascribe properties. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken together, are not so restrictive as they initially appear. Consider a wave moving across the surface of the ocean. It exists independently of any other particular wave; it is not a mode or attribute of another individual. It persists through time — one can point to it at one moment and again five minutes later and say with confidence that it is the same wave. And properties are predicated of it: one says it is high, or fast, or cresting. Yet a wave is not a substance in any philosophically satisfying sense. It is a pattern of disturbance propagated through a medium, and the water of which it is composed at any given instant is entirely replaced as it moves forward. What the definition appears to lack is a condition requiring that the entity's persistence be grounded in something more than the continuity of a pattern — that it involve a genuine identity of constituents or an intrinsic principle of unity.

definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous — there is no gap in space or time between C and E, and (iii) events of the type of C are regularly followed by events of the type of E.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
Events of the type of C are regularly followed by events of the type of E
counterexample: Consider the regular succession of day and night. The coming of night temporally precedes the coming of day; there is no gap in time between the last moment of night and the first moment of day; and events of this type — nights — are invariably followed by events of the corresponding type — days. All three conditions are satisfied, yet we should not say that night causes day. Both are effects of a common cause, namely the rotation of the earth, and the mere regularity of their succession does not establish a causal relation between them.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement that the connection between C and E be productive or generative rather than merely sequential; it cannot distinguish genuine causation from the regular succession of effects that share a common cause.
passage: A natural proposal concerning causation holds that an event C causes an event E if and only if C precedes E in time, C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and events of the type of C are regularly followed by events of the type of E. This proposal owes much to Hume's analysis, and it captures a great deal of what we ordinarily mean when we speak of causes. The requirement of temporal precedence rules out backward causation, the contiguity condition excludes action at a distance, and the regularity condition ensures that the causal relation is not a matter of singular coincidence but reflects a pattern in nature. Yet one might observe that these three conditions are satisfied by cases that we should hesitate to call causal. Consider the familiar alternation of day and night. Night precedes day with unfailing regularity; the two are temporally contiguous, with no interval separating the last moment of darkness from the first moment of light; and the succession is perfectly regular — every night is followed by a day. The three conditions are met, yet it would be absurd to say that night causes day. Both are consequences of a single underlying process — the rotation of the earth relative to the sun — and their regular succession is a symptom of this common cause, not evidence of a causal relation between them. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that C produce or bring about E, rather than merely precede it in a regular fashion.

definition: A moment M is present if and only if (i) M is the moment at which experience is occurring — it is the moment of immediate awareness, (ii) M is distinct from all past moments and all future moments, and (iii) M is the boundary between what has been and what will be.
conditions: M is the moment at which experience is occurring
M is distinct from all past and all future moments
M is the boundary between what has been and what will be
counterexample: Consider a person under general anaesthesia. There is a moment at which the anaesthetised body exists and biological processes continue, yet no experience is occurring and there is no immediate awareness. That moment is distinct from all past and future moments, and it is the boundary between what has been and what will be. The second and third conditions are met, but the first is not — yet we should not say that the present ceases to exist when consciousness is suspended. The definition ties the present too closely to subjective experience and cannot account for the presentness of moments at which no one is aware.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the present moment be characterised by occurring experience, but it should instead require only that the moment be objectively distinguished — perhaps by the obtaining of certain facts — independently of whether any conscious being is aware at that moment.
passage: It is tempting to characterise the present moment as the moment at which experience is occurring, the moment that is distinct from all past and future moments, and the boundary between what has been and what will be. This definition binds the concept of the present to the structure of conscious awareness: to be present is to be experienced, and to be experienced is to occupy the narrow point of immediate awareness that separates memory from anticipation. There is something phenomenologically compelling about this picture — the present does seem to be given to us in experience in a way that the past and the future are not. Yet one might observe that the definition, by tethering the present to experience, renders it dependent upon the existence of a conscious observer. Consider a person who has been placed under general anaesthesia. At each moment during the anaesthesia, the person's body exists and biological processes continue, but there is no experience occurring and no immediate awareness. That moment is distinct from all past and all future moments, and it is surely the boundary between what has been and what will be. We should not wish to say that the present ceases to exist simply because a particular individual — or even all individuals — happen to be unconscious. The definition has confused a feature of our access to the present — the fact that we encounter it through experience — with a feature of the present itself. What is needed is a characterisation that captures the objective distinctness of the present moment without presupposing that someone is aware of it.

definition: An object A at time t1 is numerically identical to an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B share all of the same intrinsic properties, (ii) A and B are spatiotemporally continuous — there is an unbroken path through space and time connecting the location of A at t1 to the location of B at t2, and (iii) A and B fall under the same sortal concept — that is, they are the same kind of thing.
conditions: A and B share all of the same intrinsic properties
A and B are spatiotemporally continuous
A and B fall under the same sortal concept
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar at time t1 and a butterfly at time t2 that developed from that caterpillar. The caterpillar and the butterfly share very few intrinsic properties — their shape, colour, internal structure, and mode of locomotion are radically different. Yet they are spatiotemporally continuous, and biologists regard them as the same individual organism. The first condition is violated, yet identity holds. Conversely, two mass-produced ball bearings may share all intrinsic properties and fall under the same sortal concept without being the same object. The conditions are neither individually necessary nor jointly sufficient for numerical identity across time.
missing_condition: The definition's first condition — sameness of all intrinsic properties — is too demanding, since objects routinely change their intrinsic properties over time while remaining the same individual. What is needed is a condition permitting gradual change of properties while preserving the continuity of the entity's organising principle or causal history.
passage: One might propose that an object A at one time is numerically identical to an object B at a later time if and only if they share all the same intrinsic properties, are spatiotemporally continuous, and fall under the same sortal concept. The first condition ensures that the object has not altered in any respect; the second provides a physical path linking the earlier and later stages; and the third prevents us from confusing objects of different kinds. Taken together, these conditions appear to furnish a rigorous criterion of identity across time. Yet the first condition is far too strict. Consider the familiar case of a caterpillar and the butterfly into which it metamorphoses. The caterpillar is a crawling, segmented creature with a certain weight and colour; the butterfly is a winged, delicate creature with an entirely different shape, colour, and mode of life. Their intrinsic properties are so dissimilar that the first condition is plainly violated. Yet there is an unbroken spatiotemporal path from caterpillar to butterfly, they fall under the sortal 'organism,' and we should not hesitate to say that they are the same individual. The difficulty reveals that the demand for complete sameness of intrinsic properties is unsuitable as a condition of identity through time, since objects characteristically change their properties as they persist. What the definition requires, but does not contain, is a condition that permits gradual alteration of properties while preserving whatever it is — a causal history, a biological continuity, an organising principle — that constitutes the identity of the individual.

definition: A property F is a universal if and only if (i) F is instantiated by more than one particular — that is, F is shared by numerically distinct entities, (ii) F is wholly present in each of its instances — the redness in one rose is numerically the same redness as in another, and (iii) F is mind-independent — F would exist even if no thinking being had ever conceived of it.
conditions: F is instantiated by more than one particular
F is wholly present in each of its instances
F is mind-independent
counterexample: Consider the property of being-a-member-of-the-committee-appointed-on-Tuesday. This property may be instantiated by several distinct individuals, it may be said to be wholly present in each member (each is fully a member, not partially so), and it appears to be mind-independent in the sense that the committee's existence is a social fact not reducible to any single person's thought. All three conditions are satisfied, yet we should hesitate to regard membership in a particular committee as a genuine universal on a par with redness or mass. It is a relational, context-dependent, and institutionally constituted property that owes its existence to a specific act of human arrangement rather than to the structure of reality itself.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish properties that reflect the genuine ontological structure of the world from those that are merely shared by several particulars as a consequence of conventional or institutional arrangements. A condition requiring that the property be natural or joint-carving is needed.
passage: One might define a universal as a property that is instantiated by more than one particular, is wholly present in each of its instances, and is mind-independent. The first condition captures the intuition that universals are shareable — the same redness can characterise many roses. The second condition distinguishes universals from collections or aggregates: when we say that redness is present in each red thing, we mean that each thing possesses the whole of redness, not a fragment of it. The third condition ensures that universals are not mere concepts or mental constructions. Together, these conditions appear to delineate the class of genuine universals with considerable precision. Yet one might observe that the conditions are satisfied by properties that we should not wish to admit into the company of the universals. Consider the property of being a member of the committee appointed on a particular Tuesday. This property is shared by several individuals; it is wholly present in each — each is fully a member, not partially so; and the committee's existence, one might argue, is a social fact not reducible to any single person's thought. Yet we should hesitate to say that being-a-member-of-this-committee is a universal in the same sense as redness or mass. It is a product of a specific institutional arrangement, not a feature that carves nature at its joints. The definition, it appears, requires a further condition to distinguish properties that reflect the genuine structure of reality from those that are merely shared as a consequence of human contrivance.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S does not entail a logical contradiction, (ii) S is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature, and (iii) S can be coherently conceived — that is, a thinking being can form a clear and distinct idea of S obtaining.
conditions: S does not entail a logical contradiction
S is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature
S can be coherently conceived
counterexample: Consider a state of affairs in which the gravitational constant has a value slightly different from its actual value. Such a state of affairs entails no logical contradiction and can be coherently conceived — physicists routinely imagine universes with different constants. Yet it is not consistent with the fundamental laws of nature as they actually stand, since those laws incorporate the actual value of the gravitational constant. The second condition would rule out a variation in constants that most philosophers regard as genuinely possible. Conversely, certain states of affairs that are consistent with the laws of nature may be inconceivable to us owing to the limitations of our cognitive faculties, yet we should not wish to say that they are impossible.
missing_condition: The definition conflates different grades of possibility by requiring consistency with the actual laws of nature. A condition distinguishing logical from nomological from metaphysical possibility is needed, so that a change in the constants of nature may be logically possible even if it is not physically possible.
passage: A natural analysis of possibility holds that a state of affairs is possible if and only if it entails no logical contradiction, is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature, and can be coherently conceived. These conditions correspond to three familiar tests: the logical test (no contradiction), the physical test (no violation of natural law), and the conceivability test. Together, they appear to capture a robust sense of what it means for something to be possible. Yet one might observe that the three conditions pull in different directions and do not jointly characterise a single, unified notion of possibility. Consider the hypothesis that the gravitational constant might have had a slightly different value. This hypothesis entails no logical contradiction, and it is coherently conceivable — physicists and cosmologists regularly reason about universes with different constants. Yet it is not consistent with the fundamental laws of nature as they actually operate, since those laws specify the actual value of the gravitational constant. The second condition would therefore exclude such variations, but most philosophers would regard them as possible in some legitimate sense. The difficulty is that the definition conflates distinct grades of possibility: logical possibility (consistency), physical possibility (consistency with actual laws), and something like conceivability-based possibility. What is needed is a recognition that these tests may diverge, and that a state of affairs may be possible in one sense — logically, say — while being impossible in another — physically.

definition: An entity X undergoes change if and only if (i) X possesses a property F at time t1, (ii) X does not possess F at a later time t2, and (iii) X is numerically the same entity at t1 and t2.
conditions: X possesses a property F at time t1
X does not possess F at a later time t2
X is numerically the same entity at t1 and t2
counterexample: Consider Cambridge change: when Socrates was alive, he was taller than Theaetetus; after Theaetetus grew, Socrates was no longer taller than Theaetetus. Socrates possesses the relational property of being-taller-than-Theaetetus at time t1 and does not possess it at t2, and he is numerically the same individual. All three conditions are met, yet Socrates has not changed at all — it is Theaetetus who has grown. The definition counts as a genuine change in Socrates what is merely a change in his relational context.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property in question be intrinsic to the entity rather than merely relational, so that genuine change in X is distinguished from the mere alteration of X's relational surroundings.
passage: It seems straightforward to say that an entity undergoes change if and only if it possesses a property at one time and lacks that property at a later time while remaining numerically the same entity. This definition appears to capture the core of what we mean by change: something was one way and then became another way. It neatly handles simple cases — a leaf that is green in summer and brown in autumn has changed, for it has lost the property of greenness while remaining the same leaf. Yet the definition, as it stands, admits cases that one would hesitate to describe as genuine change. Consider the case of Socrates and Theaetetus. At a certain time, Socrates possesses the property of being taller than Theaetetus. At a later time, after Theaetetus has grown, Socrates no longer possesses this property. Socrates is the same individual throughout. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition therefore pronounces that Socrates has undergone a change. But this verdict is peculiar: Socrates has done nothing; he has not grown, shrunk, moved, or altered in any respect. It is Theaetetus who has changed. What has happened is that a relational property of Socrates — a property that depends upon the state of another individual — has altered because of a change in that other individual. The definition requires a further condition restricting the properties in question to those that are intrinsic to the entity, thereby excluding mere relational or Cambridge changes.

definition: An object W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is a proper part of W — that is, each Pi is contained within W and is not identical to W, (ii) the parts collectively exhaust W — there is no part of W that is not a part of one of the Pi, and (iii) removing any one of the Pi would destroy W — that is, no Pi is dispensable.
conditions: Each Pi is a proper part of W
The parts collectively exhaust W
Removing any one Pi would destroy W
counterexample: Consider a living cat. Its cells are proper parts of it, and the totality of its cells collectively exhaust its material composition. Yet one may remove a single cell — or indeed a great many cells — without destroying the cat. Cats, like most biological organisms, routinely lose and replace cells without ceasing to exist. The third condition is violated, yet the cat is plainly a whole composed of parts. The definition is too demanding in requiring that every part be indispensable.
missing_condition: The definition needs to distinguish between essential and dispensable parts, or to replace the third condition with a requirement that the organising principle or structure that unifies the parts be preserved, rather than insisting that each individual part be necessary.
passage: One might define a whole as an object composed of parts such that each part is a proper part of the whole, the parts collectively exhaust the whole, and removing any single part would destroy the whole. The first two conditions are relatively uncontroversial: they say that the parts are genuine constituents of the whole and that they account for the whole without remainder. The third condition is more ambitious — it is intended to ensure that each part contributes essentially to the composition of the whole, so that the whole is not an accidental aggregate but a genuine unity. Yet this third condition, however attractive in the abstract, fails when applied to biological organisms and many artefacts. Consider a living cat. Its cells are proper parts of it, and the totality of its cells collectively exhaust its material composition. Yet a single cell may be removed — and indeed thousands of cells are shed and replaced daily — without any diminution of the cat's identity. The cat persists through the loss and replacement of its parts in a way that the definition does not permit. The difficulty reveals that the indispensability condition is too strong: what makes the cat a whole is not the indispensability of each cell but the persistence of an organising principle — a biological structure, a pattern of life — that is maintained even as individual parts come and go. The definition requires a condition that captures this notion of structural or functional unity rather than demanding the indispensability of every part.

definition: An object X persists through an interval of time if and only if (i) X exists at the beginning of the interval, (ii) X exists at the end of the interval, and (iii) there is no moment within the interval at which X ceases to exist.
conditions: X exists at the beginning of the interval
X exists at the end of the interval
There is no moment within the interval at which X ceases to exist
counterexample: Consider a watch that is completely disassembled at the midpoint of an interval, its every spring and wheel separated and laid out on a table, and then reassembled from the same parts into the very same configuration at a later point. The watch exists at the beginning of the interval and at the end, and one might argue that the watch never ceases to exist — its parts persist throughout — or one might argue that it does cease to exist during the period of disassembly. The three conditions do not settle the question, because they say nothing about what constitutes the existence of a complex object at a moment. The watch's parts exist continuously, but whether the watch itself does depends on whether a disassembled collection of parts counts as the watch.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify what constitutes the continued existence of a complex object at a given moment. A condition is needed requiring that the entity maintain its characteristic structure or functional organisation throughout the interval, not merely that its material constituents persist.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an object persists through an interval of time if and only if it exists at the beginning of the interval, exists at the end, and at no moment within the interval ceases to exist. This definition has the virtue of simplicity: it asserts continuous existence throughout the interval in question. For many ordinary objects — a stone lying in a field, a mountain enduring through the centuries — the definition seems perfectly adequate. Yet one might observe that it leaves unanswered a crucial question: what does it mean for a complex object to exist at a moment? Consider a watch that is completely disassembled at the midpoint of an interval. Every spring, wheel, and case is separated and laid out on a table. Some time later, the very same parts are reassembled into the very same configuration. The watch exists at the beginning of the interval and at the end. But does it exist during the period of disassembly? Its parts exist throughout, but a collection of separated parts on a table is not, it appears, a watch. The three conditions do not settle this question, because they presuppose that we already know what it is for the watch to exist at a given moment. What is needed is a further condition specifying that the entity maintain its characteristic structure or functional organisation — that its parts be arranged in the manner that constitutes a watch, not merely that they be somewhere in the world.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the negation of P is inconceivable — no coherent scenario in which not-P obtains can be described, and (iii) P is true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms alone.
conditions: P is true
The negation of P is inconceivable
P is true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H2O. This proposition is true, and many philosophers regard it as necessarily true — in no possible situation is water anything other than H2O. Yet the negation of the proposition is quite conceivable: one can coherently describe a scenario in which a substance with all the observable properties of water turns out to have a different chemical composition. Moreover, the proposition is not true in virtue of the meanings of its terms alone — it required empirical investigation to discover that water is H2O. The proposition satisfies the first condition but fails the second and third, even though it is, by many accounts, a necessary truth.
missing_condition: The definition captures only analytic or a priori necessity and fails to accommodate a posteriori necessities — truths that are necessary in virtue of the nature of things rather than the meanings of words, and whose negation is conceivable even though it is metaphysically impossible.
passage: One might hold that a proposition is necessarily true if and only if it is true, its negation is inconceivable, and it is true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms alone. This definition captures a familiar class of necessary truths: mathematical truths, logical tautologies, and analytic truths such as 'All bachelors are unmarried.' For these propositions, the negation does indeed seem inconceivable — one cannot coherently imagine a married bachelor — and their truth rests upon the meanings of the words rather than upon any empirical fact. Yet the definition is, one might observe, too narrow. Consider the proposition that water is H2O. Many philosophers hold that this proposition is necessarily true — that there is no possible situation in which water is composed of anything other than hydrogen and oxygen atoms in the relevant bonding arrangement. Yet the negation of the proposition is perfectly conceivable: one can describe a scenario in which a liquid with all the superficial properties of water turns out to have a different molecular structure. And the proposition is clearly not true in virtue of the meanings of its terms alone — it took considerable chemical investigation to establish that water is H2O. The definition therefore excludes a class of propositions that are plausibly necessary but are necessary in virtue of the nature of the things they concern, not in virtue of semantic relations. What is missing is a recognition that necessity may arise from the essential natures of things and not solely from the meanings of words.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular entity — that is, its existence does not depend upon the existence of anything else, (ii) X persists through change — that is, X can undergo alteration in its properties while remaining the same entity, and (iii) X is the bearer of properties — that is, properties are predicated of X, but X is not itself predicated of anything further.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular entity
X persists through change while remaining the same entity
X is the bearer of properties and is not predicated of anything further
counterexample: Consider a wave travelling across the surface of the ocean. The wave exists independently of any other particular wave — it maintains its identity as it moves across thousands of miles of open water, requiring no other wave for its existence. It persists through change: the wave can grow taller or shorter, faster or slower, while remaining identifiably the same wave. And it is the bearer of properties — we say of the wave that it is six feet high, that it moves at twenty knots, that it is breaking or smooth. All three conditions are met. Yet a wave is not a substance in any philosophically robust sense; it is a pattern of disturbance propagated through a medium, and the water through which it passes is constantly being replaced. The wave is a process, not a thing.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the entity be constituted by its own matter or stuff, rather than being a pattern or process sustained by the successive involvement of different material constituents. A wave satisfies the conditions by being a persistent, independent, property-bearing pattern, but it lacks the material self-sufficiency one expects of a genuine substance.
passage: One might propose that an entity qualifies as a substance if and only if it exists independently of any other particular entity, persists through change while remaining the same entity, and is the bearer of properties without itself being predicated of anything further. This definition captures much of what philosophers have traditionally meant by 'substance': the independent, enduring, property-bearing particular that stands at the foundation of the ontological hierarchy. The independence condition excludes mere modes and accidents, which depend for their existence on the substances in which they inhere. The persistence condition captures the intuition that a substance is not annihilated by every alteration. And the bearer condition ensures that a substance occupies the fundamental subject-position in predication. Yet one might observe that these three conditions, though individually plausible, are jointly satisfied by entities that we should hesitate to call substances. Consider a wave travelling across the surface of the ocean. The wave exists independently of any other particular wave; it persists through considerable changes in height, speed, and shape while remaining recognisably the same wave; and it is the bearer of properties — we ascribe to it a height, a velocity, a direction. All three conditions are met. Yet a wave is not a substance. It is a pattern of disturbance propagated through a medium; the water that constitutes it at one moment is not the water that constitutes it at the next. The wave is a process, not a thing. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring that the entity be constituted by its own material, rather than being a pattern sustained by the successive participation of different material constituents.

definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C is temporally prior to E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous — that is, there is no gap in space or time between them, and (iii) events of the same type as C are regularly followed by events of the same type as E.
conditions: C is temporally prior to E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
Events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E
counterexample: Consider the relationship between a factory whistle sounding at noon and the workers leaving the factory moments later. The whistle is temporally prior to the departure; the whistle and the departure are spatiotemporally contiguous — the workers hear the whistle in the very building from which they then depart; and events of this type are regularly conjoined — every day, the whistle sounds and the workers leave. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it appears wrong to say that the whistle causes the workers to leave. A whistle that sounded at an unexpected time would not produce the same effect; it is the hour, and the social convention governing the working day, that causes the departure. The whistle is a signal, not a cause.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the relationship between C and E be one of productive or generative connection, rather than mere regular temporal succession. A signal that reliably precedes an event satisfies all three conditions without being the productive source of that event.
passage: One might define causation by saying that an event C causes an event E if and only if C is temporally prior to E, C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and events of the same type as C are regularly followed by events of the same type as E. This Humean analysis captures a great deal of what we mean by causation in everyday life: we look for what comes before, what is connected in space and time, and what recurs with regularity. The temporal priority condition ensures that causes precede their effects. The contiguity condition rules out action at a distance. And the regularity condition distinguishes genuine causal sequences from one-off coincidences. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken together, are too permissive. Consider the factory whistle that sounds at noon each day, followed immediately by the departure of the workers. The whistle is temporally prior to the departure. The whistle and the departure occur in the same place and at adjacent moments. And the conjunction is perfectly regular — every working day, the whistle sounds and the workers leave. All three conditions are met, yet we should hesitate to say that the whistle causes the departure. Were the whistle to sound at three in the morning, the workers would not appear; were it to sound on a holiday, no one would leave. The whistle is a conventional signal marking the hour, not the productive source of the workers' movement. What the definition lacks is a condition requiring a productive or generative connection between C and E — a condition that would distinguish genuine causes from mere signals, symptoms, and regular antecedents.

definition: Two events A and B are simultaneous if and only if (i) an observer situated at the midpoint between the locations of A and B would receive signals from both events at the same moment, (ii) the signals travel at equal speeds from the events to the observer, and (iii) no temporal ordering of A and B can be established by any physical procedure.
conditions: A midpoint observer receives signals from both events at the same moment
The signals travel at equal speeds from the events to the observer
No temporal ordering of A and B can be established by any physical procedure
counterexample: Consider two lightning bolts striking at opposite ends of a moving train. An observer on the embankment, situated at the midpoint between the two strikes, receives the light signals simultaneously, and the signals travel at equal speeds. No physical procedure available to this observer can establish a temporal ordering. All three conditions are met for this observer. Yet a second observer, situated at the midpoint of the train but moving with it, receives the signal from the front strike before the signal from the rear strike — not because the signals travel at different speeds, but because the observer has moved towards one source and away from the other. For this observer, condition (i) fails, and a temporal ordering is established. The definition thus makes simultaneity depend upon the state of motion of the observer, yet it does not acknowledge or accommodate this dependence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the frame of reference relative to which simultaneity is being assessed. Without an explicit relativisation to a particular inertial frame, the definition yields contradictory results — the same pair of events being simultaneously both simultaneous and non-simultaneous — depending on which observer is chosen.
passage: One might suppose that two events are simultaneous if and only if an observer at their spatial midpoint receives signals from both at the same moment, the signals travel at equal speeds, and no physical procedure can establish a temporal ordering between them. This definition is attractively operational: it reduces the abstract notion of simultaneity to a concrete procedure involving signals and observers. The equal-speed condition ensures fairness — neither event is advantaged by a faster messenger. And the third condition, requiring that no physical procedure establish a temporal ordering, captures the intuition that genuinely simultaneous events are temporally indistinguishable. Yet one might observe that the definition, as stated, harbours a concealed difficulty. Consider two lightning bolts striking at opposite ends of a railway carriage that is moving at considerable speed. An observer on the embankment, standing at the midpoint between the two strikes, receives the light signals at the same instant, satisfying all three conditions. But an observer standing at the midpoint of the moving carriage, travelling with it, receives the forward signal before the rearward signal — not because light travels at different speeds in different directions, but because the observer has moved towards one source and away from the other during the transit of the signals. For this observer, the conditions are not met, and a temporal ordering is established. The same pair of events is simultaneous for one observer and non-simultaneous for another. The definition, it appears, tacitly presupposes a privileged observer or frame of reference, yet it does not specify which frame is to be used. Without an explicit relativisation to a particular inertial frame, the definition generates contradictory verdicts about the very same events.

definition: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B share all their intrinsic properties — that is, every property that A has at t1, B has at t2, and vice versa, (ii) A and B are spatiotemporally continuous — that is, there is an unbroken path through space and time connecting A at t1 with B at t2, and (iii) A and B are composed of the same matter.
conditions: A and B share all their intrinsic properties
A and B are spatiotemporally continuous
A and B are composed of the same matter
counterexample: Consider an oak tree observed as a sapling in 1920 and again as a mature tree in 1980. The tree in 1980 does not share all the intrinsic properties of the sapling in 1920 — it is larger, differently shaped, has bark where the sapling had smooth green shoots, and bears acorns where the sapling bore none. Moreover, the tree in 1980 is not composed of the same matter as the sapling in 1920 — its cells have been replaced many times over through the ordinary processes of growth and metabolism. Yet the tree is spatiotemporally continuous, and we have no hesitation in saying that the mature oak is the same tree as the sapling. Two of the three conditions fail, yet identity plainly obtains.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring exact property-sharing and material continuity. What is needed is a condition permitting gradual change in both properties and material composition, so long as the changes are governed by the entity's characteristic processes of growth, repair, and development — that is, a condition of continuity of form or organisation rather than of matter or properties.
passage: One might propose that an object A at one time is identical with an object B at a later time if and only if A and B share all their intrinsic properties, are spatiotemporally continuous, and are composed of the same matter. This definition attempts to provide jointly sufficient conditions for diachronic identity by combining three plausible requirements: qualitative sameness, spatiotemporal continuity, and material sameness. The property-sharing condition captures the intuition that the same object should be qualitatively unchanged; the continuity condition ensures that the object traces a connected path through space and time; and the material condition requires that the very same stuff compose the object at both times. Yet one might observe that this definition is far too demanding for the ordinary cases of identity through time that we recognise without hesitation. Consider an oak tree observed as a sapling in 1920 and again as a mature tree in 1980. The mature oak does not share all the intrinsic properties of the sapling — it is vastly larger, differently shaped, covered in rough bark, and laden with acorns. Nor is it composed of the same matter — the cells of the sapling have been replaced many times over through the processes of growth and metabolism. Yet the tree is spatiotemporally continuous, and no one would deny that the mature oak is the same tree as the sapling. The definition requires exact property-sharing and material sameness, but what actually sustains identity through time for a living organism is continuity of form and biological organisation — the ongoing processes of growth, repair, and development that connect the sapling to the mature tree through an unbroken chain of life.

definition: A property P is a universal if and only if (i) P is instantiated by more than one particular — that is, there are at least two distinct objects that have P, (ii) P is fully present in each of its instances — that is, when two objects share P, neither possesses merely a part of P, and (iii) P can be apprehended by the intellect independently of any particular instance — that is, one can grasp what P is without attending to any specific bearer of P.
conditions: P is instantiated by more than one particular
P is fully present in each of its instances
P can be apprehended by the intellect independently of any particular instance
counterexample: Consider the property of being a surviving member of the crew of the Titanic's last voyage. This property was instantiated by more than one particular — some seven hundred survivors bore it.
