It was fully present in each instance: each survivor was fully and completely a surviving crew member, not partially so. And it can be apprehended by the intellect independently of any particular survivor — one can understand what it is to be a surviving member of that crew without thinking of any specific individual. All three conditions are met. Yet this property is not a universal in any philosophically interesting sense; it is a merely accidental and historically contingent grouping. There is no genuine nature or character shared by these individuals beyond the brute fact of their common survival.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property in question correspond to a genuine nature or qualitative character — a respect in which the instances are intrinsically alike — rather than a merely accidental or extrinsic grouping. A condition requiring that the property be grounded in a real qualitative similarity among its bearers, rather than in an arbitrary or historical circumstance, is needed.
passage: One might propose that a property is a universal if and only if it is instantiated by more than one particular, is fully present in each of its instances, and can be apprehended by the intellect independently of any specific instance. The first condition is meant to capture the multiplicity that distinguishes universals from mere individuals. The second ensures that the universal is not diminished by being shared — that redness, for example, is not divided between the red things but is wholly in each. The third captures the thought that universals are objects of abstract intellectual apprehension. Yet one might observe that these conditions are satisfied by properties that we should hesitate to dignify with the title of universals. Consider the property of being a surviving member of the crew of the Titanic's final voyage. This property is instantiated by several hundred individuals. It is fully present in each: each survivor is completely, not partially, a surviving crew member. And one can grasp what it is to be a surviving member of that crew without attending to any specific individual. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet there is no genuine universal here — no nature or qualitative character that the survivors share by virtue of which they are intrinsically alike. They were bound together by the accident of having been aboard the same vessel on the same night. What the definition lacks is a requirement that the property correspond to a real qualitative similarity among its instances — that the sharing of the property reflect a genuine respect in which the bearers are intrinsically, and not merely accidentally, alike.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) no logical contradiction can be derived from the supposition that S obtains, (ii) S is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature, and (iii) there exists at least one conceivable arrangement of existing things that would realise S.
conditions: No logical contradiction follows from the supposition that S obtains
S is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature
There exists at least one conceivable arrangement of existing things that would realise S
counterexample: Consider the state of affairs in which the gravitational constant has a value slightly different from the one it actually has. No logical contradiction follows from this supposition — the laws of logic do not dictate the value of the gravitational constant. One might maintain that the state of affairs is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature, on the grounds that those laws specify the form of the gravitational interaction but do not logically necessitate a particular value for the constant. And one can conceive an arrangement of existing matter in which the gravitational constant differs. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet there is a long tradition in natural philosophy according to which the constants of nature could not have been otherwise — that their values are in some deep sense necessary, even if we cannot yet demonstrate why. The definition cannot distinguish between states of affairs that are genuinely open and those whose apparent possibility reflects merely our ignorance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between what is possible in the full metaphysical sense and what is merely conceivable given the current limits of our understanding. A condition requiring that the consistency with natural law be grounded in a genuine openness in the structure of reality, rather than in our mere inability to see why the state of affairs is impossible, is needed.
passage: One might propose that a state of affairs is possible if and only if no logical contradiction can be derived from the supposition that it obtains, it is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature, and there exists at least one conceivable arrangement of existing things that would realise it. This definition attempts to locate possibility at the intersection of logical consistency, nomological compatibility, and constructive conceivability. The first condition ensures that the impossible is excluded; the second tethers possibility to the actual structure of the physical world; the third requires that possibility be more than a bare absence of contradiction, demanding a positive conception of how the state of affairs might be realised. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken jointly, are satisfied by states of affairs whose genuine possibility is deeply uncertain. Consider a world in which the gravitational constant has a value slightly different from its actual value. No contradiction follows from this supposition; the laws of nature, in their mathematical form, do not dictate a particular value for the constant; and one can conceive a distribution of matter governed by a slightly different constant. All three conditions are met. Yet it is far from clear that the gravitational constant could genuinely have been otherwise. There is a venerable line of thought according to which the constants of nature are in some profound sense necessary — that their values are fixed by the deepest structure of reality, even if we cannot yet see why. The definition treats as possible anything that we cannot show to be impossible, but this conflates our ignorance with genuine openness. What is needed is a condition distinguishing states of affairs whose possibility is grounded in the actual structure of reality from those whose apparent possibility reflects merely the limits of our present understanding.

definition: An entity X undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) X possesses a property P at time t1 and lacks P at a later time t2, (ii) X continues to exist throughout the interval from t1 to t2, and (iii) the transition from having P to lacking P is not instantaneous — that is, there is a continuous temporal process connecting the two states.
conditions: X possesses property P at t1 and lacks P at t2
X continues to exist throughout the interval
The transition from having P to lacking P is not instantaneous but continuous
counterexample: Consider a radioactive atom that decays, transforming from one element into another at a single, sharply defined instant. At time t1, the atom possesses the property of being, say, uranium; at time t2, it lacks this property, having become thorium. The atom — or rather, the nucleus that is its core — continues to exist throughout the interval. Yet the transition is instantaneous: at a single moment, the nucleus emits an alpha particle and becomes a different element. There is no continuous process of gradual transmutation connecting the two states. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not. Yet it would be strange to deny that radioactive decay constitutes genuine change. If anything, it is among the most dramatic changes in nature.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an unnecessarily restrictive requirement that change be continuous and temporally extended. What is needed instead is merely the requirement that the transition between states be physically grounded — that there be a real physical process, whether continuous or instantaneous, responsible for the alteration.
passage: One might propose that an entity undergoes genuine change if and only if it possesses a property at one time and lacks it at a later time, continues to exist throughout the interval, and the transition between the two states is not instantaneous but continuous. This definition is motivated by the thought that change is not a mere logical relation between successive states but a real temporal process — something that happens over time, not in an instant. The persistence condition ensures that we are not confusing the replacement of one entity by another with genuine change in a single entity. The continuity condition captures the intuition that real change unfolds gradually, that nature does not leap. Yet one might observe that the continuity condition, however natural it may seem, is at odds with certain of the most fundamental changes in nature. Consider a radioactive atom that decays, transmuting from uranium into thorium at a single, sharply defined instant. At one moment the nucleus possesses the property of being uranium; at the next, it is thorium. The nucleus continues to exist — it has merely altered its composition by emitting an alpha particle. The first two conditions are met. But the transition is not continuous; it occurs instantaneously, without any intermediate stages of gradual transmutation. The third condition fails, yet radioactive decay is surely a paradigmatic instance of change — indeed, of the most profound sort, in which one element becomes another. The definition has confused a feature that is common in everyday experience — the gradualness of most changes we observe — with a necessary condition of change as such. What is missing is the recognition that change requires only a physically grounded transition between states, whether that transition unfolds gradually or occurs at a single instant.

definition: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the boundaries of W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn jointly exhaust W — that is, there is no region of W that is not occupied by some Pi, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — that is, no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.
conditions: Each part is spatially contained within the boundaries of W
The parts jointly exhaust W with no unoccupied region
The parts do not spatially overlap
counterexample: Consider the human body as a whole, and consider its circulatory system and its nervous system as two of its parts. Each system is spatially contained within the boundaries of the body. Together with the other organ systems, they jointly exhaust the body — no region of the body is left unaccounted for. Yet the circulatory system and the nervous system do spatially overlap: blood vessels run alongside and through nervous tissue, and capillaries penetrate the brain, the spinal cord, and peripheral nerves. The third condition is violated. Yet the circulatory system and the nervous system are plainly parts of the body, and the body is plainly a whole composed of such parts. The definition excludes a perfectly legitimate and scientifically standard decomposition of a whole into its functional parts.
missing_condition: The definition requires that parts be spatially non-overlapping, but this is too restrictive for wholes whose natural decomposition is into functional systems rather than spatial regions. A condition permitting overlap when the parts are distinguished by function rather than by spatial exclusivity is needed.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a whole composed of parts if and only if each part is spatially contained within the boundaries of the whole, the parts jointly exhaust the whole with no region left over, and the parts do not spatially overlap. This definition embodies a tidy spatial picture: a whole is like a jigsaw puzzle whose pieces fit together without gaps or overlaps. The containment condition ensures that no part extends beyond the whole. The exhaustion condition ensures that the parts account for the entirety of the whole. And the non-overlap condition ensures that each region of the whole belongs to exactly one part. Yet one might observe that this spatial model of composition is too restrictive for many of the wholes we encounter in the natural world. Consider the human body and its organ systems. The circulatory system and the nervous system are both parts of the body. Each is spatially contained within the body's boundaries. Together with the skeletal, muscular, digestive, and other systems, they jointly exhaust the body. But they overlap spatially: blood vessels run through nervous tissue, capillaries penetrate the brain and the spinal cord, and neither system can be isolated into a region that the other does not also occupy. The non-overlap condition fails, yet no one would deny that the circulatory and nervous systems are parts of the body. The definition is shaped by the model of spatial decomposition — cutting an object into non-overlapping spatial regions — but the natural decomposition of a living organism is into functional systems, which interpenetrate one another freely. What is needed is a more capacious notion of parthood that permits overlap when parts are distinguished by function rather than by spatial exclusivity.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in the actual world, (ii) the negation of P is inconceivable — that is, no coherent thought can be formed in which P fails to hold, and (iii) P can be established by reason alone, without recourse to empirical observation.
conditions: P is true in the actual world
The negation of P is inconceivable
P can be established by reason alone without empirical observation
counterexample: Consider the proposition that nothing travels faster than light. This proposition is true in the actual world. Many physicists would say that its negation is, in a certain sense, inconceivable — not merely that we cannot imagine it, but that the very structure of relativistic physics renders superluminal travel incoherent, since it would require infinite energy and would violate the causal order. Yet the proposition cannot be established by reason alone; it was discovered empirically, through the Michelson-Morley experiment and confirmed by the whole edifice of relativistic physics. The third condition fails. Yet one might argue that the speed of light is a necessary feature of the physical world — that it could not have been otherwise, given the structure of spacetime. The definition excludes necessary truths that are discoverable only through experience.
missing_condition: The definition conflates necessity with a priori knowability. A condition is needed that allows for necessary truths whose necessity is grounded in the structure of reality rather than in the structure of thought — truths that could not have been otherwise, even if their truth could be discovered only empirically.
passage: One might propose that a proposition is necessarily true if and only if it is true in the actual world, its negation is inconceivable, and it can be established by reason alone without recourse to empirical observation. This definition combines an actualist anchor with two conditions drawn from the rationalist tradition: inconceivability of the negation, which is meant to capture the idea that necessary truths are those whose denial is incoherent, and a priori knowability, which reflects the thought that what must be true can be recognised as such by the unaided intellect. The definition fits well the paradigmatic necessary truths of logic and mathematics: that two plus two equals four is true, its negation is inconceivable, and it is known by reason alone. Yet one might observe that the definition is too narrow to accommodate all plausible candidates for necessity. Consider the proposition that nothing travels faster than light. It is true in the actual world. Its negation is, in a physically informed sense, inconceivable — superluminal travel would require infinite energy and would violate the causal structure of spacetime. Yet the proposition was not established by reason alone; it was an empirical discovery, emerging from experiment and observation and codified in the theory of special relativity. The third condition fails. Yet many would argue that the speed of light is not merely a contingent fact but a necessary feature of the physical world — that, given the structure of spacetime, nothing could travel faster. The definition conflates two things that may come apart: the necessity of a proposition and our capacity to recognise that necessity without experience. What is needed is a condition that grounds necessity in the structure of reality rather than in the structure of cognition — allowing for necessary truths that are discoverable only through the empirical investigation of the world.

definition: An entity X persists from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) X is wholly present at both t1 and t2 — that is, no temporal part of X is absent at either time, (ii) X occupies a continuous spatial region throughout the interval, and (iii) a continuous causal process links X's state at t1 to X's state at t2.
conditions: X is wholly present at both t1 and t2
X occupies a continuous spatial region throughout the interval
A continuous causal process links X's state at t1 to X's state at t2
counterexample: Consider a migratory bird that flies from its summer nesting ground in Scandinavia to its winter territory in North Africa and back again each year, persisting across many such cycles. The bird is wholly present at each moment; there is a continuous causal process linking its earlier states to its later ones. But during each migration, the bird does not occupy a continuous spatial region in any fixed sense — it traverses thousands of miles, leaving its summer region entirely and occupying wholly different spatial regions in between and at the end of the journey. Condition (ii) fails if we interpret it as requiring that the entity remain in a continuous spatial region. Yet the bird plainly persists through the migration. No one would say that the bird that arrives in Africa is a different entity from the one that left Scandinavia.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an overly restrictive spatial continuity requirement that excludes entities which persist while moving through space. What is needed is the weaker condition that the entity occupy some spatial region at each moment in the interval, not that the region be continuous or constant — that is, the entity must be spatially located at each time, but the location may change.
passage: One might propose that an entity persists from one time to another if and only if it is wholly present at both times, occupies a continuous spatial region throughout the interval, and a continuous causal process links its earlier state to its later one. The first condition captures the endurantist intuition that a persisting entity is fully there at each moment, not merely represented by a temporal part. The second condition ties persistence to spatial stability, ensuring that the entity maintains its place in the world. The third condition provides the causal thread connecting the entity's successive states. Yet one might observe that the second condition is far too restrictive. Consider a migratory bird — say, a barn swallow that breeds in Sweden in the summer and winters in South Africa. The bird persists through each cycle of migration; it is the same bird that departs Scandinavia and arrives in South Africa months later. It is wholly present at every moment of the journey. And a continuous causal process — the bird's metabolic activity, its wing-beats, its navigational behaviour — links its state at departure to its state at arrival. But the bird does not occupy a continuous spatial region throughout the interval. It leaves its nesting ground entirely, traverses thousands of miles of airspace, and arrives at a spatial region utterly disconnected from the one in which it began. The spatial continuity condition fails, yet the bird plainly persists. What the definition lacks is the recognition that spatial location may change, even drastically, without disrupting persistence. The relevant requirement is not that the entity remain in one place but that it occupy some spatial region at each moment — that it never cease to be located somewhere.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular entity, (ii) X persists through changes in its properties, and (iii) X is the bearer of properties rather than itself a property of something else.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular entity
X persists through changes in its properties
X is the bearer of properties rather than itself a property of something else
counterexample: Consider a wave travelling across the surface of the ocean. The wave exists independently of any other particular wave; it persists through changes in its properties, growing taller or flatter as it crosses different stretches of water; and it bears properties — height, velocity, wavelength — rather than being a property of something else. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one would hesitate to call a wave a substance, for a wave is a disturbance in a medium and cannot exist apart from the water through which it propagates.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that X's existence not be constitutively dependent upon a medium or substrate of which it is a mere modification, rather than an entity in its own right.
passage: One might propose that an entity counts as a substance if and only if it exists independently of other particulars, persists through changes in its properties, and serves as the bearer rather than the borne. This proposal has a venerable pedigree, echoing formulations one encounters in the tradition from Aristotle through Descartes to the present day. It captures the paradigmatic cases admirably: a lump of gold exists independently, persists through heating and cooling, and bears its colour, malleability, and density. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is near at hand. Consider a wave upon the ocean. It exists independently of any other particular wave; it persists through changes in height and speed as it traverses the sea; and it bears properties — amplitude, frequency, velocity — in the manner required. All three conditions are met. But a wave is not a substance in any philosophically respectable sense. It is a pattern of disturbance propagated through a medium, and its existence is constitutively dependent upon the water in which it inheres. What the definition neglects is a condition requiring that the entity not be a mere modification of some underlying substrate without which it could not exist at all.

definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) events of the type of C are regularly followed by events of the type of E.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
Events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E
counterexample: Consider the case of a person who drinks coffee each morning and then walks to his office. The drinking temporally precedes the walking. The two events are spatiotemporally contiguous, occurring in the same locale within a short span. And events of this type are regularly conjoined — every weekday morning, the coffee is followed by the walk. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet no one would say that the drinking of the coffee causes the walking to the office; both are effects of the man's settled morning routine, but neither is the cause of the other.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the relation between C and E not be merely the result of a common background condition or habit that independently produces both events.
passage: A venerable tradition in philosophy analyses causation in terms of temporal precedence, contiguity, and constant conjunction. One might accordingly propose that an event is the cause of another if and only if it precedes the other, is spatiotemporally contiguous with it, and events of the first type are regularly followed by events of the second. This analysis captures many familiar cases: the striking of a match is followed by its lighting, with precedence, contiguity, and regularity all present. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the most ordinary circumstances. Consider a man whose morning habit is to drink a cup of coffee and then walk to his office. The drinking precedes the walking. The two events occur in spatial proximity and temporal contiguity. And the regularity is impeccable — every working day, the coffee precedes the walk. All three conditions are met. Yet it would be absurd to say that the coffee-drinking causes the walk. Both events are products of the same settled routine, but neither brings the other about. What is wanted is a further condition excluding cases in which the regular conjunction is the product of a common background condition rather than a genuine productive relation between the events themselves.

definition: Two events A and B are simultaneous if and only if (i) A and B occur at the same time as measured by a clock, (ii) an observer at rest with respect to both A and B would judge them to occur together, and (iii) neither A nor B is causally prior to the other.
conditions: A and B occur at the same time as measured by a clock
An observer at rest with respect to both would judge them simultaneous
Neither A nor B is causally prior to the other
counterexample: Consider two lightning strikes occurring at the two ends of a moving railway carriage, and suppose that a clock fixed to the midpoint of the carriage registers both strikes at the same time. An observer at rest with respect to the carriage judges them simultaneous. Neither strike is causally prior to the other. All three conditions are satisfied relative to this observer. Yet an observer standing on the platform, at rest with respect to the ground, would judge the two strikes to occur at different times. The definition yields contradictory verdicts depending upon the frame of reference, because it fails to specify which clock and which observer are authoritative.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to specify that simultaneity must be relativised to a frame of reference, so that the question of whether two events are simultaneous has no absolute answer but depends upon the state of motion of the observer and clock employed.
passage: It is natural to suppose that two events are simultaneous if they occur at the same clock-time, are judged concurrent by a suitably placed observer, and stand in no relation of causal priority. One might observe that this definition accommodates the everyday case tolerably well: two bells rung at the same moment in the same church tower satisfy all three conditions and are uncontroversially simultaneous. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider events separated by a significant distance and observed from frames in relative motion. Imagine two lightning strikes at opposite ends of a moving railway carriage. A clock at the carriage's midpoint registers both strikes at the same instant. An observer riding on the carriage, at rest relative to both events, judges them simultaneous. Neither strike is causally prior to the other. All three conditions are satisfied with respect to this frame. But an observer standing on the platform, in a different state of motion, equipped with an equally accurate clock, judges the two strikes to occur at different times. The definition delivers one verdict within the carriage and another upon the platform, yet offers no means of choosing between them. What is missing is a recognition that simultaneity is not an absolute relation between events but must be relativised to a frame of reference.

definition: A region R is a spatial boundary between two objects A and B if and only if (i) R separates A from B such that no part of A overlaps any part of B, (ii) R is itself of zero thickness, and (iii) R is uniquely determined by the surfaces of A and B.
conditions: R separates A from B with no overlap
R is of zero thickness
R is uniquely determined by the surfaces of A and B
counterexample: Consider two soap bubbles that meet and share a common film. The film separates the two bubbles such that no part of one overlaps the other. The film, being a thin membrane, may for idealisation be treated as having zero thickness. Yet the boundary is not uniquely determined by the surfaces of the two bubbles independently, for the shared film is a product of their mutual interaction and exists as a surface belonging to neither bubble alone — or, equally, to both. The third condition is ambiguous in this case, and the definition provides no guidance as to which object owns the boundary.
missing_condition: The definition fails to address the question of topological ownership — whether the boundary belongs to A, to B, to both, or to neither — a question that must be settled before the boundary can be said to be uniquely determined.
passage: One might propose that a spatial boundary between two objects is a region of zero thickness that separates them without overlap and is uniquely determined by their surfaces. This analysis captures the standard geometrical intuition: the boundary between a sphere and the air surrounding it is the sphere's surface, a two-dimensional manifold of zero thickness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider objects in contact. Suppose two soap bubbles meet and share a common film. The film separates the interior of one bubble from the interior of the other; no part of the first overlaps the second. For purposes of idealisation, we may treat the film as having zero thickness. But to which bubble does this shared surface belong? It cannot be uniquely determined by the surface of either bubble taken in isolation, for it is a creation of their mutual contact. If we assign it to the first bubble, the second is an open region without a boundary at the point of contact; if to the second, the first suffers the same deficiency; if to both, we have introduced a new ontological category of shared boundaries that the definition does not contemplate. The definition omits any condition addressing the question of topological ownership of the boundary — a question that must be resolved before the boundary can be considered uniquely determined.

definition: An object X at time t1 is identical with an object Y at time t2 if and only if (i) X and Y share all the same intrinsic properties, (ii) X and Y occupy a continuous spatiotemporal path from t1 to t2, and (iii) there is no moment between t1 and t2 at which X ceases to exist.
conditions: X and Y share all the same intrinsic properties
X and Y occupy a continuous spatiotemporal path
There is no moment between t1 and t2 at which X ceases to exist
counterexample: Consider two billiard balls of identical manufacture — same mass, colour, composition, and dimensions — rolling toward one another on a table. They collide and rebound. After the collision, each ball occupies a continuous spatiotemporal path, and neither ceases to exist at any moment. Ball A after the collision shares all its intrinsic properties with Ball B before the collision, and vice versa. The definition provides no means of distinguishing between the interpretation that each ball rebounded and the interpretation that they passed through one another, since both satisfy all three conditions equally well.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require a criterion of individuation that goes beyond shared intrinsic properties and spatiotemporal continuity — something that would distinguish genuine identity from mere qualitative indiscernibility along a continuous path.
passage: A natural analysis of identity over time might require that the earlier and later objects share all intrinsic properties, occupy a continuous spatiotemporal path, and enjoy unbroken existence throughout the interval. One might observe that this definition accommodates many straightforward cases: a stone carried from one room to another retains its properties, traces a continuous path, and exists at every intervening moment. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the analysis presents itself when we consider indistinguishable objects in interaction. Imagine two billiard balls, identical in every intrinsic respect — mass, colour, material composition, surface texture — rolling toward one another on a smooth table. They meet, collide, and rebound. After the collision, we may trace two continuous spatiotemporal paths: one extending forward from each ball's pre-collision trajectory. But since the balls are intrinsically identical, the definition provides no means of determining whether Ball A rebounded along the leftward path or continued along the rightward path. Both interpretations satisfy all three conditions equally well. What the definition lacks is a criterion of individuation that could settle the matter — a condition appealing to something beyond shared intrinsic properties and continuity of path.

definition: A property P is a universal if and only if (i) P is instantiated by more than one particular, (ii) P is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) P can be predicated of its instances without ambiguity.
conditions: P is instantiated by more than one particular
P is wholly present in each of its instances
P can be predicated of its instances without ambiguity
counterexample: Consider the property of being a game. Many particulars are called games — chess, football, solitaire, ring-around-the-rosy. The property is instantiated by more than one particular. One might say it is wholly present in each instance, since each is fully a game. And we predicate 'game' of each without obvious ambiguity. Yet, as has been much discussed, there is no single feature common to all games: some are competitive, others are not; some involve skill, others pure chance; some are played alone, others in groups. The instances are bound by a network of overlapping resemblances rather than by a single shared nature, and it becomes doubtful whether 'game' picks out a genuine universal rather than a family of loosely related concepts.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property constitute a single determinate nature shared by all its instances, rather than a cluster of overlapping resemblances that might be unified only by convention or linguistic habit.
passage: One might propose that a universal is a property instantiated by more than one particular, wholly present in each instance, and predicable of its instances without ambiguity. This definition captures the traditional realist picture: the property of being red is instantiated by many roses, is wholly present in each, and is predicated of each without equivocation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the concept of a game. Chess, football, solitaire, and ring-around-the-rosy are all called games. The property of being a game is multiply instantiated. Each instance may be said to be wholly a game. And we predicate the term without obvious ambiguity — no one is uncertain whether we mean something different by 'game' when applied to chess and to football. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet on closer examination, there is no single feature that all games share. Some are competitive, others cooperative; some require skill, others rely upon chance; some are played in groups, others in solitude. The instances are bound together not by a common nature but by a web of overlapping and crisscrossing resemblances. If the definition is to distinguish genuine universals from mere family-resemblance concepts, it must add a condition requiring that the property represent a determinate shared nature rather than a loose cluster of similarities.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S involves no logical contradiction, (ii) S is consistent with the fundamental laws governing the domain in question, and (iii) there exists a coherent description of a situation in which S obtains.
conditions: S involves no logical contradiction
S is consistent with the fundamental laws governing the domain
There exists a coherent description of a situation in which S obtains
counterexample: Consider the state of affairs in which a traveller journeys backward in time and prevents his own birth. There is no logical contradiction in the bare description — the terms 'traveller,' 'journey,' 'backward in time,' and 'prevents his own birth' do not generate a formal contradiction. Some physicists have argued that certain solutions to the equations of general relativity permit closed timelike curves, so consistency with fundamental law is at least arguable. And one can describe, with apparent coherence, a narrative in which the traveller arrives in the past and interferes with the relevant events. Yet the scenario generates a paradox: if the traveller prevents his own birth, he never exists to make the journey; if he never makes the journey, his birth is not prevented. The description, though superficially coherent, harbours a concealed incoherence that the three conditions fail to detect.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the description of S be not merely locally coherent but globally consistent — that is, free of self-undermining causal loops or implicit contradictions that emerge only when the full consequences of S are traced out.
passage: One might analyse possibility as the conjunction of logical consistency, conformity with fundamental laws, and the existence of a coherent description. This analysis accommodates many clear cases: the possibility of a golden mountain involves no contradiction, violates no law, and admits of coherent description. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in the much-discussed scenario of backward time travel. Suppose a traveller journeys into the past and prevents the circumstances that led to his own birth. No logical contradiction is apparent in the bare description — the words are individually meaningful and their combination does not take the form 'P and not-P.' Certain solutions to the field equations of general relativity have been alleged to permit closed timelike curves, lending at least the appearance of consistency with fundamental law. And one can narrate the scenario with apparent coherence: the traveller arrives, locates the relevant persons, and interferes. All three conditions seem to be met. But the scenario is self-undermining in a way that only emerges upon reflection. If the traveller succeeds, he was never born to make the journey; if the journey is never made, his birth proceeds as before. The superficially coherent description conceals a causal loop that renders the state of affairs globally incoherent, and the definition provides no means of detecting this deeper difficulty.

definition: A proposition P is necessary if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P is knowable a priori.
conditions: P is true
The denial of P leads to a contradiction
P is knowable a priori
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H2O. This proposition is true. Its denial — that water is not H2O — appears to lead to a contradiction, for water just is H2O. And many philosophers hold that if water is necessarily H2O, then its necessity is genuine. Yet the proposition that water is H2O is not knowable a priori; it was discovered through empirical chemical investigation. The third condition is not satisfied, yet there are strong grounds for regarding the proposition as necessary.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the category of a posteriori necessities — propositions that are necessary and whose denial is contradictory, but whose truth could only be established through empirical enquiry rather than pure reason.
passage: A traditional analysis holds that a proposition is necessary if it is true, its denial contradictory, and it is knowable a priori. This analysis captures the paradigmatic cases of logical and mathematical necessity: the proposition that all bachelors are unmarried is true, its denial self-contradictory, and it is knowable without recourse to experience. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider propositions about the essential nature of natural kinds. The proposition that water is H2O appears to be necessary in a robust sense — we should hesitate to say that water might have turned out to be something other than H2O. Its denial does seem to generate a contradiction, for if water just is H2O, then to say that water is not H2O is to deny an identity. Two of the three conditions are satisfied. But the proposition is not knowable a priori. No amount of armchair reflection could have revealed the chemical composition of water; the matter was settled by Lavoisier, Cavendish, and their successors through painstaking experiment. If we insist upon a priori knowability as a condition of necessity, we are forced to deny that the identity of water with H2O is necessary — a conclusion that strikes many philosophers as deeply counterintuitive. What the definition overlooks is the possibility of propositions that are necessary yet discoverable only through empirical investigation.

definition: An object X has undergone change if and only if (i) X exists at two distinct times t1 and t2, (ii) X possesses a property F at t1 that it lacks at t2, and (iii) X retains sufficient properties throughout the interval to remain the same object.
conditions: X exists at two distinct times t1 and t2
X possesses a property F at t1 that it lacks at t2
X retains sufficient properties to remain the same object
counterexample: Consider a perfectly homogeneous sphere rotating about its axis at a constant angular velocity. At time t1 the sphere is rotating, and at t2 it is still rotating in exactly the same manner. Every intrinsic property of the sphere — its shape, mass, temperature, angular velocity — remains the same. It exists at both times and retains all its properties. Yet it appears that something is happening: the sphere is in motion, its constituent parts are continually exchanging positions. One might observe that the sphere is changing in some sense, for motion is a species of change. But the definition cannot register this change, since no property is possessed at one time and lacked at the other.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases of change that consist in the continuous re-arrangement of parts or the sustained occurrence of a process, where no monadic property of the whole is gained or lost over the interval.
passage: One might analyse change as the possession by an object of a property at one time that it lacks at another, while the object retains enough of its character to remain identifiable. This analysis captures the commonest cases: a leaf changes colour from green to brown; a river changes its course; a person grows older. In each instance, a property is possessed at one time and absent at the next. Yet there is a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Consider a perfectly homogeneous sphere — uniform in density, colour, and composition — rotating about its axis at a constant rate. At every moment, the sphere possesses exactly the same intrinsic properties: the same shape, the same mass distribution, the same angular velocity. No property is gained or lost from one moment to the next. Yet the sphere is in motion — its parts are continually exchanging positions, tracing circular paths through space. There is, one might say, something happening; the sphere is not in a state of perfect quiescence. But the definition cannot capture this, for it requires the gain or loss of a property, and no such gain or loss obtains. A further condition is needed to accommodate changes that consist in the ongoing rearrangement of parts or the sustained occurrence of a dynamic process that leaves the overall character of the whole unaltered.

definition: An object W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is a part of W, (ii) the parts jointly exhaust W, leaving no remainder, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — no region of W belongs to more than one Pi.
conditions: Each Pi is a part of W
The parts jointly exhaust W with no remainder
The parts do not overlap
counterexample: Consider a chess club whose members are also members of a book club. The chess club is composed of its members, who jointly exhaust its membership without remainder. But the same individuals are parts of the book club as well. If we ask whether the chess club and the book club are distinct wholes, the definition offers no guidance, for the very same parts compose both. Two apparently distinct wholes satisfy conditions (i) through (iii) with respect to the same collection of parts, yet they are not identical — they have different rules, different purposes, and different meeting times.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require a principle of composition specifying what unifies the parts into this whole rather than some other — a structural, functional, or relational condition beyond the mere exhaustive and non-overlapping partition.
passage: A natural analysis of wholes and parts holds that an object is a whole composed of certain parts if each part belongs to the whole, the parts jointly exhaust it without remainder, and no two parts overlap. One might observe that this captures the geometrical case neatly: a square is composed of two non-overlapping triangles that jointly exhaust it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider wholes constituted by the same parts under different descriptions. Suppose a chess club has five members: Adams, Brown, Clarke, Davies, and Evans. These five jointly exhaust the club's membership without remainder, and no member is counted twice. Now suppose that precisely the same five individuals also constitute a book club. The book club, too, is exhausted by these members without overlap. The definition's three conditions are satisfied equally well for both clubs with respect to the same collection of parts. Yet the chess club and the book club are manifestly distinct entities — they have different constitutions, different purposes, different schedules, and different histories. What the definition lacks is a condition specifying the principle of composition that binds these particular parts into this particular whole — some structural, functional, or relational requirement that distinguishes one mode of composition from another.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X is a particular rather than a universal, (ii) X is capable of existing independently of any other substance, and (iii) X has a determinate nature that distinguishes it from all other substances.
conditions: X is a particular rather than a universal
X is capable of existing independently of any other substance
X has a determinate nature distinguishing it from all other substances
counterexample: Consider a shadow cast by a tree upon a wall. The shadow is a particular rather than a universal — it is this shadow, here and now, distinct from any other. It has a determinate nature: a specific shape, size, and degree of darkness that distinguish it from all other shadows. And it appears capable of existing independently of other substances: no other shadow or particular need exist for this shadow to be present. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one would hesitate to call a shadow a substance, for it is a privation of light rather than an entity in its own right, and its existence is wholly parasitic upon the objects that produce it.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the entity possess its own intrinsic causal powers, rather than being merely the absence or privation of something else whose existence is entirely derivative from other entities.
passage: One might propose that a substance is a particular that is capable of independent existence and possesses a determinate nature distinguishing it from all other substances. This formulation has the virtue of clarity: it rules out universals, which are general rather than particular; it rules out dependent entities, which cannot exist on their own; and it rules out indeterminate or featureless substrata, which lack a distinguishing nature. The paradigmatic cases — a block of marble, a living horse, a grain of sand — satisfy all three conditions without difficulty. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the common phenomenon of shadows. Consider the shadow cast by an oak tree upon a garden wall on a summer afternoon. The shadow is undeniably a particular: it occupies a definite region of the wall, it has a specific shape determined by the tree's branches, and it is numerically distinct from any other shadow. It has a determinate nature — a characteristic outline, a measurable degree of darkness, a position that changes as the sun moves. And it appears to exist independently of any other substance in the relevant sense: no other particular substance need exist for this shadow to be cast, beyond the tree and the wall themselves. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to say that a shadow is a substance. A shadow is not an entity in its own right but a privation — the absence of light in a region where light would otherwise fall. It has no intrinsic causal powers: it cannot push, pull, heat, or cool anything. Its existence is entirely parasitic upon the tree, the light source, and the wall. The definition, by requiring only particularity, independence, and determinate nature, admits entities that are mere privations or absences, and thereby fails to capture the positive, causally efficacious character that we ordinarily attribute to substances.

definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C occurs before E in time, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) events of the same type as C are regularly followed by events of the same type as E.
conditions: C occurs before E in time
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
Events of the same type as C are regularly followed by events of the same type as E
counterexample: Consider the relationship between the fall of a barometer's mercury column and the subsequent arrival of a storm. The fall occurs before the storm. There is spatiotemporal contiguity in the sense that the barometric fall occurs in the same locale where the storm later breaks. And falls of barometers are regularly followed by storms — this is precisely why barometers are useful instruments. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we would not say the fall of the barometer caused the storm; both the fall and the storm are effects of a common cause, namely the drop in atmospheric pressure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish genuine causation from cases in which two events are regularly conjoined because both are effects of a shared prior cause, rather than because one brings the other about.
passage: It appears natural to propose that an event is the cause of another if the first occurs before the second, the two are spatiotemporally contiguous, and events of the first type are regularly followed by events of the second type. This Humean formulation has the attraction of relying upon nothing beyond observable features of the world: temporal priority, contiguity, and constant conjunction. It accommodates the paradigmatic cases with ease. Striking a match is followed by its lighting; the collision of a billiard ball is followed by the motion of the ball it strikes; the application of heat to water is followed by its boiling. In each case the three conditions are met and the causal judgement is secure. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the barometer and the storm. When atmospheric pressure drops, the mercury in a barometer falls, and a storm subsequently arrives. The fall of the mercury occurs before the storm. The two events are spatiotemporally contiguous, at least in the loose sense that they occur in the same region of the countryside. And falls in barometric readings are regularly followed by storms — the conjunction is constant enough to have made the barometer an indispensable tool of weather prediction. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet no one would say that the fall of the barometer caused the storm. The barometric fall and the storm are both effects of a common cause: the drop in atmospheric pressure. One might observe that the definition, by relying solely upon temporal priority, contiguity, and regular conjunction, is unable to distinguish genuine causal connections from cases of common causation, in which two events are regularly conjoined not because one produces the other but because both are produced by a third event that the definition does not require us to identify.

definition: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are composed of the same matter, (ii) A and B occupy continuous spatial regions across the interval from t1 to t2, and (iii) A and B fall under the same sortal concept.
conditions: A and B are composed of the same matter
A and B occupy continuous spatial regions across the interval
A and B fall under the same sortal concept
counterexample: Consider a living oak tree over the course of fifty years. The tree at the end of the period falls under the same sortal concept as the tree at the beginning — both are oak trees. It has occupied a continuous spatial region throughout. Yet the matter composing the tree has been almost entirely replaced: the cells of the trunk, the leaves, the roots — all have been exchanged many times over through metabolism and growth. The first condition fails. Yet we would say without hesitation that the tree at the end of the fifty years is the same tree as the tree at the beginning.
missing_condition: The definition requires identity of matter, but living organisms — and many other persisting objects — maintain their identity through the gradual and continuous replacement of their material constituents, so that material identity is too strong a requirement for persistence through time.
passage: One might hold that an object at one time is identical with an object at a later time if and only if the two are composed of the same matter, occupy continuous spatial regions across the interval, and fall under the same sortal concept. The insistence upon material continuity has a powerful intuitive appeal: it is the very same gold that composed the ring a century ago, and it is this fact that makes it the same ring. The requirement of spatial continuity excludes cases of disassembly and reassembly, and the sortal condition ensures that we do not confuse a lump of clay with the statue it constitutes. For artefacts composed of durable materials — a bronze sword, a marble column, a gold coin — the definition yields the right results. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by any living organism considered over a sufficient span of time. Consider an oak tree that stands in a meadow for fifty years. At the end of this period it is, we would say, the same tree that was planted half a century ago. It falls under the same sortal concept — it is an oak tree at both times. It has occupied a continuous spatial region, growing upward and outward but never detaching from its root. But the matter composing the tree has been almost entirely replaced. Through the continuous processes of metabolism, growth, and decay, every cell in the trunk has been renewed, the leaves have fallen and regrown fifty times, the roots have extended and died back. The matter present in the tree today is not the matter that was present fifty years ago. One might observe that the definition has been modelled upon the persistence of inert objects, whose material constituents remain fixed, and has overlooked the characteristic mode of persistence of living things, which maintain their identity not through the retention of matter but through the continuity of an organised process.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S does not involve a logical contradiction, (ii) S is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature, and (iii) S is conceivable by a rational mind without confusion or error.
conditions: S does not involve a logical contradiction
S is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature
S is conceivable by a rational mind without confusion or error
counterexample: Consider the state of affairs in which a particle travels faster than the speed of light. This state of affairs involves no logical contradiction — there is nothing self-contradictory in the description of superluminal travel. It is conceivable by a rational mind: science fiction writers and physicists alike have entertained the idea without confusion. Yet it is inconsistent with the fundamental laws of nature as we understand them, specifically the special theory of relativity. The definition counts this as impossible. But now consider the state of affairs before 1905, when the constancy of the speed of light had not been established. At that time, the very same state of affairs was consistent with the accepted laws of nature. Whether something counts as possible, on this definition, shifts with our changing understanding of natural law, which seems to conflate the limits of our current theories with the limits of possibility itself.
missing_condition: The definition conflates physical possibility with possibility simpliciter; it ties what is possible to our present understanding of the laws of nature, whereas something may be genuinely possible even if it is inconsistent with laws that are themselves revisable and provisional.
passage: One might define a possible state of affairs as one that involves no logical contradiction, is consistent with the fundamental laws of nature, and is conceivable without confusion by a rational mind. This definition has the virtue of combining logical, physical, and cognitive constraints into a single account. It excludes round squares, which are logically contradictory; it excludes perpetual motion machines, which violate natural law; and it excludes confused conceivings that rest upon misunderstanding rather than genuine insight. For a wide range of cases the definition delivers the intuitively correct verdicts. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider how our understanding of natural law changes over time. Before 1905, a physicist might have conceived without any confusion of a particle travelling at twice the speed of light. This state of affairs involves no logical contradiction. It was consistent with the laws of nature as then understood — Newtonian mechanics imposed no upper limit on velocity. And it was conceivable to rational minds. All three conditions were satisfied, and the definition would have pronounced superluminal travel possible. After 1905, when Einstein's special theory of relativity revealed that no massive particle can exceed the speed of light, the very same state of affairs became inconsistent with the fundamental laws of nature, and the definition now pronounces it impossible. The possibility of the state of affairs has not changed; what has changed is our understanding of the laws. One might observe that the definition has made possibility hostage to the current state of physical theory, so that what counts as possible shifts whenever our best theories are revised — a consequence that confuses the limits of our present understanding with the limits of reality itself.

definition: An event E is present if and only if (i) E is occurring now, at the moment of utterance, (ii) E is directly accessible to perception, and (iii) E stands in no temporal relation of earlier-than or later-than to the moment of awareness.
conditions: E is occurring now, at the moment of utterance
E is directly accessible to perception
E stands in no temporal relation of earlier-than or later-than to the moment of awareness
counterexample: Consider the light from a distant star arriving at the eye of an astronomer. The perception is present — the astronomer sees the star now, at the moment of utterance. The event appears directly accessible to perception; the astronomer is looking at the star and seeing its light. Yet the event that produced the light — the nuclear reaction on the star's surface — occurred thousands of years before the moment of awareness. The astronomer perceives a present event (the arrival of light at the retina) that is the causal consequence of a vastly earlier event (the emission of that light). The definition cannot distinguish between the present perception and the past event that is its source, and thereby conflates the presentness of an experience with the presentness of what the experience is of.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish the temporal location of a perceptual experience from the temporal location of the event that causally produces it, conflating the immediacy of perception with the contemporaneity of the perceived event.
passage: One might propose that an event is present if and only if it is occurring now, at the moment of utterance, it is directly accessible to perception, and it stands in no relation of earlier-than or later-than to the moment of awareness. This proposal attempts to capture the peculiar immediacy of the present — its availability to consciousness, its freedom from the pastness of memory or the futurity of expectation. For many ordinary cases it delivers the correct result. The ringing of a bell in the room where I sit is occurring now, is directly accessible to my perception, and stands in no temporal distance from my awareness of it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by astronomical observation. An astronomer gazes through her telescope and perceives the light of a distant star. The perception is present: she sees the star now, at this very moment. The perception is directly accessible: she is looking at the star and seeing its light with her own eyes. And the perception, considered as an experience, stands in no temporal distance from her awareness — she is aware of it as it occurs. All three conditions are met with respect to the perceptual event. But the event that produced the light — the thermonuclear reaction on the star's surface — took place thousands of years ago. The light has been travelling through space for millennia before arriving at the astronomer's retina. One might observe that the definition, by tying presentness to perceptual accessibility, has conflated two quite different things: the presentness of a perceptual experience and the presentness of the event that causes the experience. What is directly accessible to the astronomer is a present effect of a vastly past cause, and the definition provides no resources for drawing this distinction.

definition: A property F is a genuine universal if and only if (i) F is predicable of more than one particular, (ii) F is wholly present in each particular that instantiates it, and (iii) F can be apprehended by the intellect independently of any particular instance.
conditions: F is predicable of more than one particular
F is wholly present in each particular that instantiates it
F can be apprehended by the intellect independently of any particular instance
counterexample: Consider the property of being the exact shade of vermilion on the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel. This shade — call it Sistine Vermilion — is predicable of more than one particular: one could mix a paint to match it precisely and apply it to a different surface. It is wholly present in each surface that instantiates it. And it can be apprehended by the intellect independently of any particular instance, for one can entertain the idea of this shade without looking at any sample. All three conditions are met. Yet one might question whether so specific and accidental a shade constitutes a genuine universal, for it appears to be an arbitrary point in colour-space rather than a natural kind of quality that the world is organised around.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement that the property carve nature at its joints — that it correspond to a genuine similarity that plays a role in the causal or explanatory structure of the world, rather than being merely a predicable attribute that the mind can abstract from experience.
passage: One might hold that a property is a genuine universal if and only if it is predicable of more than one particular, wholly present in each particular that instantiates it, and apprehensible by the intellect independently of any instance. This formulation captures much of what the tradition has meant by universals. Redness satisfies all three conditions: it is predicable of many things, wholly present in each red object, and graspable by thought alone. Triangularity, hardness, and warmth likewise satisfy the definition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the infinite variety of the colour spectrum. Consider the exact shade of vermilion that appears on a particular section of the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel — a shade so specific that it can be identified only by precise colorimetric measurement. This shade is predicable of more than one particular: one could, with sufficient care, reproduce it exactly on another surface. It is wholly present in each surface that bears it. And it can be apprehended by the intellect independently of any particular instance — one can entertain the thought of this shade in the absence of any sample. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet we should hesitate to say that this maximally specific shade constitutes a genuine universal of the sort that metaphysics ought to recognise. It appears to be an arbitrary point in colour-space, an accidental quality that plays no special role in the organisation of nature. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only predicability, total presence, and intellectual apprehensibility, admits an unmanageable profusion of pseudo-universals — every precisely specifiable shade, every exactly measurable degree of warmth, every determinate length — and thereby fails to distinguish those properties that correspond to genuine similarities in the world from those that are mere artefacts of our capacity for discrimination.

definition: An artefact A persists through a change if and only if (i) A exists before and after the change, (ii) A retains its characteristic function throughout, and (iii) the change does not involve the replacement of all of A's parts simultaneously.
conditions: A exists before and after the change
A retains its characteristic function throughout
The change does not involve the replacement of all parts simultaneously
counterexample: Consider a museum ship that has been taken out of service and permanently dry-docked as an exhibit. Over many decades, every plank, nail, and fitting is gradually replaced by conservators to prevent decay, but at no point are all parts replaced simultaneously. The ship retains its characteristic function — it is a museum exhibit throughout — and it exists before and after each change. All three conditions are satisfied at every stage. Yet the question arises whether the ship at the end of the process, composed of entirely new materials, is the same artefact as the ship that was first dry-docked, or merely a replica that has been assembled one plank at a time.
missing_condition: The definition permits an artefact to survive the replacement of all its parts provided the replacements occur gradually rather than simultaneously, but gradual total replacement may be no less destructive of identity than simultaneous replacement. What is wanted is a condition that addresses the cumulative effect of piecemeal changes, not merely the absence of a single catastrophic transformation.
passage: One might hold that an artefact persists through a change if and only if it exists before and after the change, retains its characteristic function throughout, and the change does not involve the replacement of all its parts at once. This definition has considerable plausibility. A knife whose blade is replaced while its handle remains is still the same knife; the replacement is partial, the function is retained, and the knife exists continuously. A building that is repainted retains its function and its parts, with only the surface coating altered. The definition captures these cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the phenomenon of gradual total replacement. Consider a museum ship, permanently dry-docked and maintained as a public exhibit. Over the course of many decades, the conservators replace every component of the ship — every plank of the hull, every nail, every fitting, every rope and sail — but they do so gradually, replacing one part at a time over many years. At no point are all the parts replaced simultaneously; at each stage the majority of the ship's components are originals or earlier replacements. The ship retains its characteristic function as a museum exhibit throughout. It exists continuously. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied at every stage of the process. Yet at the end of the process, the ship is composed of entirely new materials. Not a single atom of the original ship remains. One might observe that the definition, by forbidding only simultaneous total replacement, has drawn the line in the wrong place. The cumulative effect of many partial replacements may be just as complete as a single act of total replacement, and the definition provides no resources for addressing this possibility. It has prohibited sudden destruction whilst permitting a gradual dissolution that achieves precisely the same result.

definition: An entity X exists if and only if (i) X occupies a determinate region of space, (ii) X persists through some interval of time, and (iii) X is capable of entering into causal relations with other entities.
conditions: X occupies a determinate region of space
X persists through some interval of time
X is capable of entering into causal relations with other entities
counterexample: Consider the number seven. Mathematicians would say without hesitation that the number seven exists — it is the fourth prime, it is the sum of three and four, it is indispensable to arithmetic and to the sciences that depend upon arithmetic. Yet the number seven does not occupy a determinate region of space; it is not located anywhere. It does not persist through time in the way that physical objects do; it is, if it exists at all, timeless. And its capacity to enter into causal relations is at best obscure — the number seven does not cause anything in the way that a falling stone causes a dent in the earth. All three conditions fail, yet its existence is presupposed by every branch of mathematics and science.
missing_condition: The definition restricts existence to spatiotemporal, causally efficacious entities and thereby excludes abstract objects — numbers, sets, propositions — whose existence is presupposed by the very sciences that the definition takes as paradigmatic.
passage: One might propose that an entity exists if and only if it occupies a determinate region of space, persists through some interval of time, and is capable of entering into causal relations with other entities. This definition has the merit of capturing what we most readily mean by existence in ordinary discourse. Tables, mountains, persons, and storms all satisfy the three conditions, and we affirm their existence without hesitation. The definition also has the virtue of excluding certain dubious candidates — hallucinations, fictional characters, merely imagined objects — from the realm of the existent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the abstract objects of mathematics. Consider the number seven. Every mathematician and every scientist who employs arithmetic presupposes the existence of the number seven. It is the fourth prime number. It is the successor of six. It enters into the fundamental theorems of number theory. Its existence appears to be required by any adequate account of the truths of mathematics. But the number seven does not occupy a region of space — it is not in London, not in a desk drawer, not in anyone's head. It does not persist through time in the manner of a physical object; it is, if it exists at all, eternal and immutable. And its capacity to enter into causal relations is deeply obscure. The number seven does not cause the chalk to move across the blackboard when a mathematician writes '7'; it is the mathematician's hand that does that. One might observe that the definition has been modelled upon the existence of material objects and has made spatiotemporal location and causal efficacy necessary conditions, thereby excluding from existence the very entities whose reality is presupposed by every natural science — a consequence that should give us pause.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X is ontologically self-sufficient, requiring nothing other than itself for its existence, (ii) X possesses a determinate nature that distinguishes it from all other entities, and (iii) X is not reducible to a collection or aggregate of more basic entities.
conditions: X is ontologically self-sufficient, requiring nothing other than itself for its existence
X possesses a determinate nature that distinguishes it from all other entities
X is not reducible to a collection or aggregate of more basic entities
counterexample: Consider a magnetic field surrounding a bar magnet. The field is ontologically self-sufficient in the relevant sense: once the magnet exists, the field exists necessarily and requires no further entity to sustain it. The field possesses a determinate nature — it has a specific strength, orientation, and spatial extent that distinguish it from all other fields. And the field is not reducible to an aggregate of more basic entities; it is a continuous structure, not a collection of parts. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one would hesitate to call the magnetic field a substance, for it depends constitutively upon the magnet that generates it and has no existence apart from the source that produces it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that X's existence not be parasitic upon or constitutively generated by some other entity, so that derived or dependent existences — such as fields generated by their sources — are excluded from the category of substance.
passage: One might propose that an entity counts as a substance if and only if it is ontologically self-sufficient, possesses a determinate nature, and is irreducible to an aggregate of simpler things. This characterisation has evident merit: it captures the independence, determinacy, and unity that we associate with the paradigmatic substances — a stone, a lump of iron, a living organism. A stone requires nothing beyond itself for its continued existence; it has a nature that distinguishes it from sand or water; and it is not a mere heap of unrelated particles but a unified whole. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the physics of electromagnetism. A magnetic field surrounding a bar magnet satisfies all three conditions. It is, in the relevant sense, self-sufficient: given the magnet, the field exists without requiring any further sustaining entity. It possesses a determinate nature — a measurable strength and orientation at every point in space. And it is not reducible to an aggregate; it is a continuous structure, not a collection of discrete parts. Nevertheless, one might observe that the field depends for its very existence upon the magnet that generates it. Remove the magnet and the field vanishes. The field is not an independent existent in the way that a stone or a lump of iron is; it is, as it were, an emanation or consequence of another entity. The definition, by requiring only self-sufficiency without distinguishing intrinsic from derivative self-sufficiency, fails to exclude entities whose existence is parasitically bound to the existence of something else.

definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C is temporally prior to E, (ii) there is a law of nature such that, given C together with the relevant background conditions, E follows necessarily, and (iii) C and E are distinct events.
conditions: C is temporally prior to E
There is a law of nature such that, given C together with the relevant background conditions, E follows necessarily
C and E are distinct events
counterexample: Consider the gravitational attraction between the Earth and the Moon. One might wish to say that the Earth's mass causes the Moon to orbit in its observed path. Yet on the Newtonian account, gravitational attraction is instantaneous: the force exerted by the Earth upon the Moon operates at the very same moment as the Moon's accelerated motion. The cause is not temporally prior to the effect; they are simultaneous. There exists a law relating the two, and the events are distinct. Yet the first condition fails, and the definition must pronounce the gravitational influence a non-cause, which is manifestly unsatisfactory.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases of simultaneous causation, in which cause and effect occur at the same moment yet stand in an asymmetric relation of dependence, by demanding temporal priority rather than some broader relation of productive dependence.
passage: It is natural to define a cause as an event that is temporally prior to its effect, that is connected to it by a law of nature rendering the effect necessary given the cause and background conditions, and that is distinct from the effect. This definition captures a great many paradigmatic cases: the striking of the match precedes the flame, a law connects the friction to the ignition, and the striking is plainly distinct from the burning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of Newtonian mechanics. On Newton's account, the gravitational force exerted by the Earth upon the Moon is instantaneous — it does not precede the Moon's accelerated motion but is strictly contemporaneous with it. One might observe that we nonetheless wish to say that the Earth's gravitational pull causes the Moon to follow its elliptical orbit. The force and the orbital motion are distinct events, and there is certainly a law of nature that connects them. But the first condition — temporal priority — fails, for the cause and the effect are simultaneous.
