If we insist upon temporal priority as a necessary condition for causation, we are compelled to deny that the Earth's gravity causes the Moon's orbit, a conclusion that appears plainly at odds with our understanding of the physical world. The difficulty is that the definition treats temporal priority as essential to causation when in fact it may be merely a frequent accompaniment of it.

definition: An object A at time t1 is numerically identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B occupy a continuous spatiotemporal trajectory from t1 to t2, (ii) every property possessed by A at t1 that belongs to it essentially is also possessed by B at t2, and (iii) the material composition of A overlaps substantially with that of B.
conditions: A and B occupy a continuous spatiotemporal trajectory from t1 to t2
Every essential property of A at t1 is also possessed by B at t2
The material composition of A overlaps substantially with that of B
counterexample: Consider a human being over the course of seventy years. Biologists inform us that nearly every atom in the human body is replaced over a span of roughly seven to ten years, so that by the time a man reaches old age, his material composition shares virtually no atoms with his infant self. The third condition — substantial material overlap — fails utterly. Yet we do not hesitate to say that the old man is the same person as the infant. The spatiotemporal path is continuous and the essential properties may plausibly be preserved, but the material condition excludes precisely the case that most demands accommodation.
missing_condition: The definition requires substantial overlap of material composition, but fails to recognise that identity may be preserved through a gradual and complete replacement of matter, provided some other continuity — of biological process, of psychological life, of causal connection — bridges the successive stages.
passage: One might attempt to characterise the identity of an object over time by requiring a continuous spatiotemporal trajectory, the preservation of essential properties, and a substantial overlap of material composition between the earlier and later stages. This analysis has considerable plausibility when applied to the short-term persistence of ordinary objects. A bronze statue examined on Monday and again on Friday occupies a continuous trajectory, retains its essential form, and is composed of the very same bronze. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when one considers the long-term persistence of living organisms. It appears to be established beyond reasonable doubt that the material constituents of the human body are replaced with great thoroughness over a period of years. The calcium in one's bones, the iron in one's blood, the carbon in one's tissues — all are exchanged through the ordinary processes of metabolism. An elderly man shares virtually no material with the infant he once was. The third condition of the definition is therefore violated. But it would be absurd to conclude that the old man is not the same individual as the infant; indeed, this identification is among the firmest we make. One might observe that what secures identity in such a case is not material overlap but the continuity of a biological process that replaces material gradually, each stage causally connected to its predecessor, so that the organism persists through total material change. The definition, by insisting upon material overlap, excludes the very entities whose persistence is most fundamental to our understanding of the world.

definition: A moment M is present if and only if (i) M is the moment at which conscious experience is occurring, (ii) M divides the temporal continuum into a determinate past and a determinate future, and (iii) M is unique — there is exactly one present moment.
conditions: M is the moment at which conscious experience is occurring
M divides the temporal continuum into a determinate past and a determinate future
M is unique — there is exactly one present moment
counterexample: Consider two astronomers, one on Earth and one on a planet orbiting a distant star, moving at a significant fraction of the speed of light relative to the Earth. According to the special theory of relativity, there is no absolute simultaneity between them: events that are present for the Earth-bound astronomer may be future for the distant one, and vice versa. Each experiences a moment that satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) — each is consciously experiencing a moment that divides time into past and future. But these moments are not the same, and condition (iii) — that there is exactly one present moment — cannot be satisfied for both observers. The definition presupposes an absolute simultaneity that the physics of our world does not provide.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that the present is absolute and frame-independent, failing to accommodate the relativity of simultaneity, which reveals that different observers in relative motion may disagree about which events are present without either being in error.
passage: A natural account of the present holds that it is the unique moment at which conscious experience is occurring, the moment that divides the continuum of time into a settled past and an open future. This characterisation captures something important about our phenomenological relation to time: we seem to inhabit a singular, moving present, with the past receding behind us and the future approaching. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account is provided by the special theory of relativity. Consider two observers in relative motion — an astronomer on Earth and an astronomer on a distant planet, travelling at a significant fraction of the speed of light. Each is conscious; each experiences a present moment that divides time into past and future. But the theory of relativity informs us that these two present moments are not the same. Events that are present for the Earth-bound astronomer may lie in the future according to the reference frame of the distant one. There is no privileged frame and no absolute simultaneity. The third condition of the definition — that there is exactly one present moment — cannot be satisfied for both observers at once. We should hesitate to say that one observer is in the true present and the other is not, for the theory provides no basis for such a distinction. The definition, by requiring uniqueness, presupposes an absoluteness of the present that the physical structure of space-time does not support.

definition: A property P is a universal if and only if (i) P is instantiated by more than one particular, (ii) P is wholly present in each particular that instantiates it, and (iii) P can be identified independently of any particular instantiation.
conditions: P is instantiated by more than one particular
P is wholly present in each particular that instantiates it
P can be identified independently of any particular instantiation
counterexample: Consider the property of being the tallest building in London. At any given time, this property is instantiated by exactly one building, so the first condition fails. Yet one would hardly deny that being the tallest building in London is a genuine property — it is wholly present in the building that possesses it, it can be identified independently of that particular building, and it is the sort of thing that could be instantiated by a different building were the skyline to change. The definition excludes uniquely instantiated properties from the class of universals, yet such properties appear to be every bit as general and repeatable as multiply instantiated ones.
missing_condition: The definition requires actual multiple instantiation, but fails to recognise that a property may be genuinely universal — general, repeatable, and sharable — even if it happens, as a matter of contingent fact, to be instantiated by only one particular at a given time.
passage: One might propose that a property counts as a universal if and only if it is instantiated by more than one particular, is wholly present in each of its instances, and can be identified independently of any particular instantiation. This proposal captures many familiar universals: the property of being red is instantiated by countless particulars, is wholly present in each red thing, and can be grasped independently of any particular red object. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed without great difficulty. Consider the property of being the tallest building in London. At any given moment, this property is instantiated by exactly one building — whichever building happens to stand tallest. The first condition of the definition therefore fails. Yet it appears unreasonable to deny that being the tallest building in London is a genuine property. It is general in character — it could be possessed by any sufficiently tall building. It is repeatable — were the current tallest building demolished, a different building would come to possess it. And it can be identified independently of the particular building that instantiates it. One might observe that the definition confuses the actual multiplicity of instances with the possibility of multiple instantiation, and that a property may be thoroughly universal in character even if the contingent facts of the world restrict its instantiation to a single particular.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S does not involve a logical contradiction, (ii) S is compatible with the laws of nature, and (iii) S is compatible with the actual history of the world up to the present moment.
conditions: S does not involve a logical contradiction
S is compatible with the laws of nature
S is compatible with the actual history of the world up to the present moment
counterexample: Consider the state of affairs in which Napoleon wins the Battle of Waterloo. This state of affairs involves no logical contradiction; it is entirely compatible with the laws of nature — there is no physical law that forbade Napoleon's victory. Yet it is incompatible with the actual history of the world, for Napoleon did in fact lose at Waterloo. The third condition excludes it from the domain of the possible. But one might observe that we routinely and intelligibly speak of this as a genuine possibility — historians discuss what would have happened had Napoleon prevailed, and this counterfactual reasoning presupposes that her victory was possible. The definition, by tethering possibility to actual history, conflates what is possible with what is still achievable, and excludes the vast domain of unrealised but perfectly intelligible possibilities.
missing_condition: The definition conflates possibility with future realisability by requiring compatibility with actual history, thereby excluding counterfactual possibilities — states of affairs that could have obtained but did not — which constitute a central and legitimate category of the possible.
passage: A natural account of possibility holds that a state of affairs is possible if and only if it involves no logical contradiction, accords with the laws of nature, and is compatible with the history of the world up to the present moment. This definition has the merit of distinguishing the genuinely possible from the merely conceivable: it excludes both logical impossibilities and physically impossible scenarios, and it further restricts the possible to what is achievable given the way the world has actually developed. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers historical counterfactuals. The state of affairs in which Napoleon wins the Battle of Waterloo is logically consistent and compatible with the laws of nature — no physical law forbade a French victory on that day in June 1815. The outcome depended upon contingencies of weather, morale, and the timing of reinforcements, any of which might have gone differently. Yet this state of affairs is plainly incompatible with the actual history of the world, and the definition must therefore pronounce it impossible. We should hesitate to accept this verdict. Historians engage in serious and illuminating analysis of what would have followed from a Napoleonic victory, and such analysis presupposes that the victory was, in the relevant sense, possible. It appears that the definition, by requiring compatibility with actual history, excludes a vast and important class of possibilities — those that were genuinely achievable at some past moment but were not in fact realised.

definition: An entity E undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) E possesses a property P at time t1 and lacks P at time t2, (ii) E at t1 and E at t2 are numerically the same entity, and (iii) the transition from possessing P to lacking P is a real alteration in E rather than a mere relational shift.
conditions: E possesses a property P at time t1 and lacks P at time t2
E at t1 and E at t2 are numerically the same entity
The transition is a real alteration in E rather than a mere relational shift
counterexample: Consider Socrates, who is taller than Theaetetus when Theaetetus is young but shorter than Theaetetus when Theaetetus has grown. Socrates himself has not altered in any intrinsic respect — his height, his bodily constitution, his physical properties are unchanged. Yet he has lost the property of being taller than Theaetetus and acquired the property of being shorter than Theaetetus. The third condition is designed to exclude precisely such cases. But now consider the property of being the oldest living person. When the previous holder of this distinction dies, someone else acquires the property without undergoing any intrinsic alteration — yet one might argue that this transition is not a mere relational shift but a genuine change in status, carrying real social and legal consequences. The boundary between real alteration and mere relational shift is far less determinate than the definition assumes.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes a sharp and unproblematic distinction between real alterations and mere relational shifts, but fails to provide any criterion for drawing this boundary, with the result that cases of mixed or ambiguous character cannot be classified.
passage: One might define genuine change as the process by which a numerically identical entity possesses a property at one time and lacks it at another, where this transition constitutes a real alteration rather than a mere relational shift. This definition is designed to exclude the well-known puzzle of so-called Cambridge changes — cases in which an entity acquires or loses a relational property without undergoing any intrinsic modification. Socrates becomes shorter than Theaetetus when Theaetetus grows, but Socrates himself has not changed; only the relational property has shifted. The definition handles this case well enough by stipulating that the transition must be a real alteration. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this approach arises when we consider properties that appear to occupy an intermediate position between the purely relational and the genuinely intrinsic. Consider the property of being the oldest living person. When the previous oldest person dies, someone acquires this property without undergoing any intrinsic physical alteration. One might therefore classify this as a mere relational shift. But it appears that this transition carries real consequences — legal, social, perhaps even biological in its effects upon the person's treatment and self-understanding — that distinguish it from the trivially relational case of Socrates and Theaetetus. The definition, by assuming that the distinction between real alteration and relational shift is sharp, provides no resources for handling cases in which the two categories interpenetrate.

definition: An object W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the region occupied by W, (ii) the Pi collectively exhaust the region occupied by W without remainder, and (iii) the Pi do not overlap — no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.
conditions: Each part is spatially contained within the region occupied by the whole
The parts collectively exhaust the region of the whole without remainder
The parts do not overlap — no region is occupied by more than one part
counterexample: Consider a chessboard with its sixty-four squares. One might decompose it into thirty-two dark squares and thirty-two light squares, obtaining sixty-four non-overlapping parts that exhaust the board. But one might equally decompose it into eight horizontal ranks, each comprising eight squares. These ranks also satisfy the three conditions. And one might also decompose it into eight vertical files. The definition permits all three decompositions, pronouncing each a legitimate partition into parts. Yet it provides no basis for preferring one decomposition over another, and it appears to entail that the same whole has three entirely different sets of parts, with no fact of the matter about which is correct.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify whether parthood is relative to a scheme of decomposition or absolute, and consequently cannot determine which among several equally legitimate decompositions reveals the genuine parts of a whole.
passage: A natural analysis of the part-whole relation holds that a whole is composed of parts if and only if each part is spatially contained within the whole, the parts collectively exhaust the spatial region of the whole without remainder, and no two parts overlap. This analysis is admirably precise: it reduces composition to clear spatial conditions that can in principle be verified. It accounts well for the paradigmatic cases — a wall is composed of bricks, a floor of tiles, a body of organs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the multiple decomposability of even the simplest objects. Take a standard chessboard. One may decompose it into sixty-four individual squares — thirty-two dark and thirty-two light — and this decomposition satisfies all three conditions. But one may equally decompose it into eight horizontal ranks, each consisting of eight contiguous squares, and this decomposition too satisfies all three conditions. Or one may decompose it into eight vertical files. Each of these decompositions is a legitimate partition in the sense of the definition, for in each case the parts are contained within the whole, they exhaust it without remainder, and they do not overlap. Yet the parts are different in each case. One might observe that the definition provides no guidance as to which decomposition reveals the genuine parts of the board, if indeed there are genuine parts to be discovered. The difficulty is that the definition treats parthood as an absolute relation, when in fact it may be relative to a scheme of decomposition.

definition: An entity E persists from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) E exists at t1 and at t2, (ii) E at t2 bears a relation of causal continuity to E at t1, and (iii) no event occurs between t1 and t2 that wholly destroys E and replaces it with a qualitatively similar but numerically distinct entity.
conditions: E exists at t1 and at t2
E at t2 bears a relation of causal continuity to E at t1
No event between t1 and t2 wholly destroys E and replaces it with a numerically distinct entity
counterexample: Consider a watch that is sent to the manufacturer for repair. The manufacturer disassembles the watch entirely — every spring, gear, casing, and crystal is removed. The parts are cleaned and then reassembled into the same watch. Between disassembly and reassembly, there is no entity that can be identified as the watch: it exists as a collection of disconnected parts, not as a functioning timepiece. It appears that the watch has been wholly destroyed as a watch and then reconstituted. The third condition seems to be violated, yet the owner receives back what she regards — and what we should regard — as the very same watch, not a new one.
missing_condition: The definition cannot accommodate cases in which an entity is temporarily disassembled and then reconstituted from the very same parts in the very same arrangement, because it treats any event of total destruction as severing the chain of persistence, even when the subsequent reconstitution preserves all material and structural features.
passage: One might propose that persistence requires existence at both the earlier and later times, causal continuity between the two stages, and the absence of any intervening event that wholly destroys the entity and replaces it with a qualitatively similar duplicate. This proposal is designed to exclude the surreptitious replacement cases that trouble simpler definitions — if someone secretly replaces your bicycle with an exact copy, the copy is not your bicycle despite its qualitative similarity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises in the commonplace practice of complete disassembly and reassembly. Consider a watch sent to its manufacturer for servicing. The horologist disassembles the watch entirely, separating every component — the mainspring, the escapement, the balance wheel, the casing, the crystal. For a period of time, the watch does not exist as a watch; there is only a collection of separate parts upon a workbench. The horologist then cleans each part and reassembles them into precisely the same configuration. One might observe that between disassembly and reassembly, an event has occurred that appears to satisfy the description of wholly destroying the watch. There is no continuously existing entity that one can point to and call the watch during the interval. Yet we should hesitate to say that the reassembled timepiece is a new watch. The owner receives back what she regards as her own watch, and it appears that she is right to do so. The parts are the same parts; the arrangement is the same arrangement. The definition, by treating any event of total destruction as fatal to persistence, cannot accommodate entities that survive disassembly and reconstitution.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the negation of P is inconceivable, and (iii) P can be established by pure reason without recourse to empirical observation.
conditions: P is true
The negation of P is inconceivable
P can be established by pure reason without recourse to empirical observation
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H2O. This proposition is, by wide scientific consensus, true. Its negation — that water is not H2O — appears inconceivable once one understands the molecular structure of water; to deny it is to deny the very identity of the substance. And yet the proposition cannot be established by pure reason alone: it was discovered through empirical investigation, through the chemical analyses of Cavendish and Lavoisier. The third condition fails. But one might observe that the proposition has every mark of necessity — it holds in every possible circumstance, it could not have been otherwise — and a definition that excludes it from the class of necessary truths is too narrow.
missing_condition: The definition conflates necessity with a priori knowability, failing to accommodate necessary truths that can only be discovered through empirical investigation — the so-called necessary a posteriori — and thereby excludes a significant class of propositions whose necessity is beyond dispute.
passage: A traditional account of necessary truth holds that a proposition is necessarily true if and only if it is true, its negation is inconceivable, and it can be established by the exercise of pure reason without reliance upon empirical observation. This account assimilates necessity to the a priori: what is necessarily true is what reason alone can establish. The paradigmatic examples fit well — the truths of logic and mathematics are true, their negations are inconceivable, and they are established by pure reason. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the discoveries of chemistry. The proposition that water is H2O satisfies the first two conditions. It is true. And its negation is, once one grasps the molecular constitution of the substance, genuinely inconceivable — a liquid that was not composed of hydrogen and oxygen atoms in the ratio two to one would simply not be water. It would be some other substance entirely, however closely it might resemble water in its superficial properties. Yet the proposition was not established by pure reason. It was discovered through the painstaking empirical work of chemists who decomposed water into its elements and measured their proportions. The third condition therefore fails. One might observe that this result is deeply unsatisfactory, for the proposition that water is H2O has every mark of a necessary truth. It holds in every possible circumstance; no conceivable alteration of the contingent features of the world could make water cease to be H2O. The definition, by requiring accessibility to pure reason, excludes from the class of necessary truths a proposition that manifestly belongs there.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular entity, (ii) X is the bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything, and (iii) X persists through change in its non-essential properties.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular entity
X is the bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything
X persists through change in its non-essential properties
counterexample: Consider an electron in a helium atom. The electron exists independently in the sense that it does not depend for its existence upon any one particular atom — it could be stripped away and persist. It bears properties such as charge and spin but is not itself a property of anything. And it persists through changes in its non-essential properties, such as its energy level. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the electron's identity is not well-defined in the quantum-mechanical sense: two electrons in the same atom cannot be distinguished even in principle, and the question of which electron is which has no determinate answer.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that X possess a determinate individual identity distinguishing it from other entities of the same kind, so that entities which are in principle indistinguishable may nonetheless qualify as substances.
passage: One might propose that a substance is an entity which exists independently of any other particular, bears properties without being itself a property, and persists through change in its non-essential features. This characterisation has a long pedigree in the philosophical tradition: Aristotle's primary substances, Descartes's res cogitans and res extensa, and Locke's substrata all conform in broad outline to this pattern — though one recalls Anscombe's observation that the concept of substance is less transparent than its long philosophical career might suggest. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from modern physics. Consider an electron within a helium atom. The electron exists independently of any particular atom — it may be removed and continue to exist. It bears properties such as mass, charge, and spin, but is not itself a property of the atom. And it persists through changes in its energy level, which is plausibly a non-essential feature. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard the electron as a substance in the traditional sense. Quantum mechanics teaches that two electrons in the same atom are in principle indistinguishable: there is no fact of the matter about which electron occupies which orbital. It appears that the definition, by attending only to independence, property-bearing, and persistence, takes no account of the requirement that a substance possess a determinate individual identity.

definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C occurs temporally prior to E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E.
conditions: C occurs temporally prior to E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
Events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E
counterexample: Consider a factory whistle that sounds every day at noon, immediately before the workers leave the factory. The whistle occurs prior to the departure; the two events are spatiotemporally contiguous; and whistles of that type are invariably followed by departures of that type. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the whistle does not cause the workers to leave — both the whistle and the departure are effects of a common cause, namely the factory clock striking twelve.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that there be no common cause of both C and E which renders their regular conjunction a mere epiphenomenon of a deeper causal structure.
passage: It is natural to suppose, following a well-known tradition, that causation consists in the regular succession of spatiotemporally contiguous events: the cause precedes the effect, they are adjacent in space and time, and events of the relevant types are constantly conjoined. This proposal captures much of what we ordinarily mean by causation — the striking of the match is followed by the flame, the ingestion of arsenic by illness — and it avoids appeal to mysterious powers or necessary connexions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be readily constructed. Consider a factory whistle that sounds every day at noon, immediately before the workers stream out of the factory gates. The whistle precedes the departure. The two events are contiguous in space and time. And the conjunction is perfectly regular — every sounding of the whistle is followed by the departure of the workers. Nevertheless, one might observe that the whistle does not cause the departure. Both events are effects of a common cause: the factory clock striking twelve. The definition, by requiring only temporal priority, contiguity, and regular conjunction, cannot distinguish genuine causal relations from cases in which two events are merely joint effects of a shared antecedent.

definition: A moment T is real if and only if (i) T can be located within a linear temporal ordering of before and after, (ii) events occurring at T are determinate in all their properties, and (iii) T is distinguishable from every other moment by the events that occur at it.
conditions: T can be located within a linear temporal ordering of before and after
Events occurring at T are determinate in all their properties
T is distinguishable from every other moment by the events that occur at it
counterexample: Consider a hypothetical universe in which history is perfectly cyclical: every sequence of events repeats identically after a fixed period. Each moment can be located in a before-and-after ordering within a single cycle, events at each moment are fully determinate, and each moment is distinguishable from its neighbours. Yet at the completion of a cycle, one arrives at a moment that is qualitatively indistinguishable from the first moment of the previous cycle. The definition would count this as a distinct moment, yet one might observe that there is no non-arbitrary ground for distinguishing the two.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the distinguishability of moments be grounded in something beyond qualitative differences in the events occurring at them, so that qualitatively identical but numerically distinct moments cannot be accommodated.
passage: One might propose that a moment is real if it occupies a place in a linear temporal ordering, if the events occurring at it are fully determinate, and if it is distinguishable from every other moment by the events that occur at it. This proposal has the merit of grounding temporal reality in the concrete character of events rather than in any mysterious temporal substance. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be imagined without great difficulty. Suppose a universe in which the entire history of events repeats in a perfectly cyclical fashion: after a fixed period, every event recurs in exactly the same order, with exactly the same properties, and exactly the same relations to other events. Within any single cycle, each moment is distinguishable from its neighbours. But the first moment of the second cycle is qualitatively identical to the first moment of the first cycle — the same events occur, with the same properties, in the same relations. One might observe that the definition provides no ground for regarding these as distinct moments rather than as a single moment recurring. It appears that the definition, by tying the individuation of moments to the qualitative character of the events occurring at them, cannot accommodate the possibility of numerically distinct but qualitatively identical temporal moments.

definition: An object A is identical with an object B if and only if (i) A and B share all the same properties at the time of comparison, (ii) A and B occupy the same spatial location, and (iii) A and B have the same material constitution.
conditions: A and B share all the same properties at the time of comparison
A and B occupy the same spatial location
A and B have the same material constitution
counterexample: Consider a bronze statue and the lump of bronze from which it is fashioned. At any given moment they share all their properties — weight, colour, temperature, chemical composition. They occupy the same spatial location. And they have the same material constitution, being composed of exactly the same atoms of bronze. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the statue and the lump differ in their modal properties: the lump could survive being melted down and recast, whereas the statue could not. They also differ in their histories: the lump existed before the statue was fashioned.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that A and B share the same modal and historical properties, so that entities which are materially and spatially coincident but modally or historically divergent may be counted as identical.
passage: It is natural to suppose that identity between objects consists in the sharing of all properties, co-location in space, and sameness of material constitution. This proposal appears to codify Leibniz's principle of the identity of indiscernibles in a straightforward manner. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from a familiar philosophical puzzle. Consider a bronze statue of Athena and the lump of bronze from which it is fashioned. At any given moment, the statue and the lump share every intrinsic property: they have the same weight, the same colour, the same temperature, and the same chemical composition. They occupy precisely the same spatial region. And they are composed of the very same atoms arranged in the very same configuration. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied in full. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that the statue is identical with the lump. The lump could survive being melted down and recast into a different shape; the statue could not. The lump existed before the sculptor began work; the statue did not. It appears that the definition, by attending only to actual, synchronic properties, cannot accommodate the modal and temporal differences that distinguish coincident objects.

definition: A property P is a genuine universal if and only if (i) P is instantiated by more than one particular, (ii) P is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) two particulars sharing P resemble one another in the respect that P characterises.
conditions: P is instantiated by more than one particular
P is wholly present in each of its instances
Two particulars sharing P resemble one another in the respect that P characterises
counterexample: Consider the property of being such that either one is a red apple or one is a blue whale. This disjunctive property is instantiated by many particulars — every red apple and every blue whale. It is arguably wholly present in each instance. And two particulars sharing it resemble one another in the respect that the property characterises — namely, in both being red-apples-or-blue-whales. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that this disjunctive property does not correspond to any genuine common nature: a red apple and a blue whale have nothing of real significance in common.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P correspond to a genuine natural resemblance rather than a gerrymandered or disjunctive grouping, so that arbitrary Boolean combinations of properties may qualify as universals.
passage: One might propose that a genuine universal is a property instantiated by more than one particular, wholly present in each instance, and such that its instances resemble one another in the relevant respect. This characterisation captures the intuitive idea that universals are what particulars have in common — redness is what red things share, sphericity is what spheres share. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed by considering disjunctive properties. Take the property of being either a red apple or a blue whale. This property is instantiated by a great many particulars, for there are many red apples and many blue whales. One might argue that it is wholly present in each instance — each red apple fully possesses it, as does each blue whale. And two things sharing this property do resemble one another in the respect characterised: both are red-apples-or-blue-whales. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this disjunctive property as a genuine universal. A red apple and a blue whale share no real common nature; their grouping under this property is arbitrary and gerrymandered. It appears that the definition, by attending only to multiple instantiation, whole presence, and resemblance, cannot distinguish genuine universals from merely disjunctive or logically manufactured properties.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S involves no logical contradiction, (ii) S is compatible with the meanings of the terms used to describe it, and (iii) S can be coherently conceived by a rational thinker.
conditions: S involves no logical contradiction
S is compatible with the meanings of the terms used to describe it
S can be coherently conceived by a rational thinker
counterexample: Consider the state of affairs in which water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen. There is no logical contradiction in the proposition 'water is not H2O' — the denial is not of the form 'P and not-P.' The terms are used consistently. And a rational thinker, ignorant of chemistry, could coherently conceive of a substance with all the manifest properties of water that was nevertheless composed of some other elements. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that if 'water' refers to the very substance whose chemical nature is H2O, then the state of affairs described is not genuinely possible — it is ruled out by the nature of the substance itself.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S be compatible with the essential natures of the entities involved, so that states of affairs which are merely conceivable in thought but metaphysically impossible may be counted as genuinely possible.
passage: A natural account of possibility holds that a state of affairs is possible if it involves no logical contradiction, is compatible with the meanings of the terms employed, and can be coherently conceived by a rational thinker. This account has the attractive consequence of grounding possibility in the capacities of rational thought rather than in any mysterious modal facts. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the chemical constitution of familiar substances. Consider the supposition that water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen. There is no logical contradiction in this supposition — the proposition 'water is not H2O' is not self-contradictory in form. The terms are used in accordance with their ordinary meanings. And a rational thinker who was ignorant of chemistry could coherently conceive of a substance possessing all the manifest properties of water — its liquidity, transparency, taste — while being composed of entirely different elements. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard this state of affairs as genuinely possible. If the term 'water' rigidly designates the very substance whose essential nature is to be H2O, then no possible world contains water that is not H2O. It appears that the definition, by attending only to logical consistency, semantic compatibility, and conceivability, cannot distinguish the merely conceivable from the genuinely possible.

definition: An entity X undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) X possesses a property P at time t1 and lacks P at a later time t2, (ii) X exists at both t1 and t2, and (iii) the transition from possessing P to lacking P is not merely a change in the relational context of X.
conditions: X possesses a property P at time t1 and lacks P at a later time t2
X exists at both t1 and t2
The transition from possessing P to lacking P is not merely a change in the relational context of X
counterexample: Consider a piece of iron that is magnetised at t1 and demagnetised at t2. The iron possesses the property of being magnetic at t1 and lacks it at t2. It exists at both times. And the transition is not merely relational — the alignment of the iron's internal domains has genuinely altered. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet suppose the demagnetisation is caused by heating the iron above its Curie temperature, and suppose furthermore that the iron is then cooled and spontaneously re-magnetises in exactly the same pattern. One might observe that the iron has undergone a change and then reversed it, returning to a state qualitatively identical to the original. The definition counts the demagnetisation as genuine change but has no resources for distinguishing reversible from irreversible changes.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that genuine change involve an alteration that is not immediately and fully reversible by the removal of the disturbing condition, so that transient perturbations may be counted as changes of the same kind as permanent transformations.
passage: One might characterise genuine change as the possession of a property at one time and its lack at a later time, where the entity persists through the transition and the change is not merely relational. This characterisation appears to capture paradigmatic instances of change: the leaf that was green and is now brown, the traveller who was in London and is now in Paris. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the physics of magnetic materials. Consider a piece of iron that is magnetised at time t1 and demagnetised at time t2 by heating above its Curie temperature. The iron possesses the property of being magnetic at t1 and lacks it at t2. It persists through the transition. And the change is not merely relational — the internal domain structure of the iron has been genuinely altered. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet if the iron is subsequently cooled, it spontaneously re-magnetises in a pattern qualitatively identical to the original. One might observe that the demagnetisation was a transient perturbation rather than a genuine transformation — the iron's natural state reasserted itself the moment the disturbing condition was removed. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the presence and absence of a property at two times, cannot distinguish permanent transformations from transient and fully reversible perturbations.

definition: An object O at time t1 is the same object as an object O* at a later time t2 if and only if (i) O and O* are spatiotemporally continuous, (ii) O and O* share a sufficient proportion of their material parts, and (iii) O and O* fall under the same sortal concept.
conditions: O and O* are spatiotemporally continuous
O and O* share a sufficient proportion of their material parts
O and O* fall under the same sortal concept
counterexample: Consider a wooden ship that is gradually repaired over many years by replacing one plank at a time. At the end of the process, every original plank has been replaced. The ship is spatiotemporally continuous — at no point does it cease to exist. At each stage, it shares a sufficient proportion of its material parts with the preceding stage. And it falls under the same sortal concept throughout — it is a ship at every moment. All three conditions are met. Yet the original planks, if reassembled in a warehouse, would also constitute a ship that is spatiotemporally continuous with the original (via a more circuitous route), shares all its material parts, and falls under the same sortal concept.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the criterion of identity be sufficient to yield a unique answer in cases of fission or competition, so that two equally qualified candidates for identity with the original may both satisfy the conditions.
passage: One might propose that an object persists through time if it is spatiotemporally continuous, shares a sufficient proportion of its material parts across time, and falls under the same sortal concept throughout. This proposal has considerable intuitive appeal: we judge that a river is the same river despite the constant flow of its waters, and that a person is the same person despite the replacement of bodily cells. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be adapted from a venerable philosophical puzzle. Consider a wooden ship whose planks are gradually replaced, one at a time, over many years. At no stage does the ship cease to exist; the spatiotemporal continuity is unbroken. At each replacement, the ship shares the vast majority of its parts with the preceding stage. And it remains a ship throughout. All three conditions are met at every point. Now suppose that the original planks, as they are removed, are stored in a warehouse and eventually reassembled into a ship. This reassembled ship also shares all its material parts with the original vessel, falls under the same sortal concept, and is spatiotemporally continuous with the original via a more circuitous path. It appears that both candidates satisfy the definition, yet they cannot both be identical with the original. The definition, by not requiring that the identity relation yield a unique result, cannot adjudicate between competing claimants to persistence.

definition: A collection of objects C composes a whole W if and only if (i) each member of C is a part of W, (ii) no part of W is disjoint from every member of C, and (iii) the members of C are in physical contact with one another.
conditions: Each member of C is a part of W
No part of W is disjoint from every member of C
The members of C are in physical contact with one another
counterexample: Consider the solar system: the Sun, the planets, their moons, and the various smaller bodies. One might regard the solar system as a genuine whole. Each of these bodies is a part of the system, and no part of the system is disjoint from all of them. Yet the members of the collection are manifestly not in physical contact with one another — the planets are separated from the Sun and from each other by vast distances. All three conditions cannot simultaneously be satisfied, and yet it seems wrong to deny that the solar system is a genuine composite whole.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for wholes whose parts are unified by causal or dynamical relations — such as gravitational binding — rather than by physical contact, so that spatially scattered but causally integrated systems cannot be recognised as genuine composite objects.
passage: A natural proposal concerning the composition of wholes holds that some objects compose a whole when each is a part of the whole, no part of the whole is disjoint from all of them, and the parts are in physical contact. This proposal captures the intuition that a wall is composed of bricks that are in contact, that a body is composed of organs that are physically connected, and that a machine is composed of parts that touch one another. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from astronomy. Consider the solar system, comprising the Sun, the planets, their moons, and the various smaller bodies. We ordinarily regard the solar system as a genuine composite entity — we speak of its formation, its structure, and its evolution. Each celestial body is a part of the system, and no part of the system is disjoint from every member of the collection. Yet the members are not in physical contact: they are separated by enormous distances. It appears that what unifies them into a whole is not contact but gravitational binding — the dynamical relation that holds the system together. The definition, by requiring physical contact among the parts, cannot accommodate wholes whose unity derives from causal or dynamical relations rather than from spatial adjacency.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P is a priori knowable — that is, its truth can be established independently of experience.
conditions: P is true
The denial of P leads to a contradiction
P is a priori knowable — that is, its truth can be established independently of experience
counterexample: Consider the proposition that Hesperus is Phosphorus — that is, that the evening star is the morning star. This proposition is true. Given that both names rigidly designate the planet Venus, its denial leads to a contradiction: it would assert that Venus is not Venus. Yet the proposition is not a priori knowable: it required empirical astronomical observation to discover that the two celestial appearances are one and the same object. All three conditions cannot simultaneously be satisfied, and yet the proposition appears to be necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for necessary truths that are a posteriori — that is, truths whose necessity arises from the identities of the objects involved rather than from the logical form of the proposition, and which therefore require empirical investigation to discover.
passage: One might propose that a necessarily true proposition is one that is true, whose denial leads to contradiction, and whose truth is a priori knowable. This characterisation has a venerable history: the rationalist tradition from Leibniz to the logical positivists held that necessity, analyticity, and a priori knowability coincided. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the philosophy of language. Consider the proposition that Hesperus is Phosphorus — that the bright celestial body visible in the evening is the very same object as the bright celestial body visible in the morning. This proposition is true. Moreover, since both names rigidly designate the planet Venus, the denial of the proposition would amount to the assertion that Venus is not Venus, which is contradictory. The proposition therefore satisfies the first two conditions. Yet it fails the third: the discovery that the evening star and the morning star are one and the same required empirical astronomical investigation. No amount of a priori reflection could have established the identity. It appears that the definition, by requiring a priori knowability as a condition of necessity, cannot accommodate necessary truths whose necessity arises from the identity of the objects designated rather than from the logical form of the proposition.

definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C occurs temporally prior to E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E.
conditions: C occurs temporally prior to E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
Events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E
counterexample: Consider a factory whistle that sounds every day at noon, and every day at noon the workers in the neighbouring factory stop their machines and go to lunch. The whistle occurs prior to the stoppage. The two events are spatiotemporally contiguous. And events of the first type are regularly followed by events of the second type. All three conditions are met. Yet the whistle does not cause the workers in the neighbouring factory to stop, for they are responding to their own clock, not to the whistle. The whistle and the stoppage are effects of a common cause — the time of day — rather than standing in a causal relation to one another.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the regular succession between C and E not be the product of a common cause that independently produces both, so that mere correlations arising from shared antecedent conditions are mistakenly classified as causal relations.
passage: One might characterise causation by requiring that the cause occur before the effect, that cause and effect be spatiotemporally contiguous, and that events of the relevant types be regularly conjoined. This analysis, reminiscent of certain classical empiricist proposals, captures much of what we ordinarily mean when we speak of one event causing another. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from everyday industrial life. Suppose a factory whistle sounds every day at noon, and every day at noon the workers in a neighbouring factory cease their labours and proceed to lunch. The whistle occurs prior to the cessation of work. The two events are contiguous in space and time — the factories stand side by side. And events of the first type are invariably followed by events of the second. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that the whistle causes the neighbouring workers to stop, for the workers are governed by their own clock and would stop at noon whether the whistle sounded or not. The regular succession is produced by a common cause — the time of day — which independently gives rise to both the whistle and the stoppage. The definition, by attending only to temporal priority, contiguity, and regularity, cannot distinguish genuine causal connections from cases in which two events are regularly correlated because both are effects of a single underlying condition.

definition: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B share all their intrinsic properties, (ii) A and B occupy the same spatial region at their respective times, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 to B at t2.
conditions: A and B share all their intrinsic properties
A and B occupy the same spatial region at their respective times
There is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 to B at t2
counterexample: Consider a wooden ship that is gradually repaired over the course of many years, each plank being replaced as it decays, until no original timber remains. The ship at the end of the process occupies the same berth, is connected to the ship at the beginning by a continuous spatiotemporal path, and — let us suppose — shares all the same intrinsic properties of shape, mass, and design. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the removed planks have been reassembled in a neighbouring yard into a ship of exactly the same design. This second vessel also shares the intrinsic properties and has a continuous spatiotemporal path traced through the salvaged planks. The definition cannot determine which of the two ships is identical with the original.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify a criterion for adjudicating cases of fission or rival claimants, in which two distinct objects each satisfy the conditions for identity with a single predecessor, since identity is a one-one relation that cannot hold between one object and two successors simultaneously.
passage: One might propose that an object at one time is identical with an object at another if they share all intrinsic properties, occupy the same spatial region at their respective times, and are connected by a continuous spatiotemporal path. This proposal captures the intuition that identity through time requires both qualitative sameness and physical continuity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the ancient puzzle of the ship. Suppose a vessel is repaired gradually over many years, each plank being replaced as it decays, until no original timber remains. The ship at the end of the process occupies the same berth, is connected to the ship at the beginning by a continuous spatiotemporal path, and shares all the same intrinsic properties of shape, mass, and design. All three conditions are met. Now suppose the removed planks have been carefully stored and reassembled in a neighbouring yard into a ship of exactly the same design. This second vessel also shares the original's intrinsic properties and is connected to it by a continuous spatiotemporal path traced through the salvaged timber. The definition yields the conclusion that both ships are identical with the original — but identity is a one-one relation, and two distinct objects cannot both be identical with a single predecessor. It appears that the definition, by relying on intrinsic properties and spatiotemporal continuity alone, cannot resolve cases in which rival successors each satisfy the stated conditions.

definition: An object O persists through a change if and only if (i) O exists before the change, (ii) O exists after the change, and (iii) O retains its essential properties throughout.
conditions: O exists before the change
O exists after the change
O retains its essential properties throughout
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that undergoes metamorphosis and emerges as a butterfly. The organism exists before the change and after it. One might argue that its essential properties — being a living organism of species Danaus plexippus — are retained throughout. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the caterpillar and the butterfly differ so profoundly in structure, behaviour, and appearance that one might question whether the same object persists, or whether the caterpillar has been replaced by something altogether new. The definition cannot settle this question because it relies on the notion of essential properties without specifying which properties count as essential.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide an independent criterion for distinguishing essential from accidental properties, so that the question of whether an object persists through a radical transformation reduces to the prior and unsettled question of which properties are essential to it.
passage: A natural suggestion is that an object persists through change just in case it exists before and after the change and retains its essential properties throughout. This analysis has a comforting simplicity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the biological phenomenon of metamorphosis. A caterpillar undergoes a transformation so thorough that within its chrysalis it is reduced to a largely undifferentiated mass before reorganising into a butterfly. The organism exists before the change and after it. If we take its essential properties to be those of a living organism belonging to a particular species, then these are retained throughout — the caterpillar and the butterfly share the same genetic constitution and belong to the same species. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the caterpillar and the butterfly differ so profoundly in bodily form, locomotive capacity, sensory apparatus, and mode of life that one might reasonably question whether the same individual persists or whether a new creature has come into being. The difficulty is that the definition's force depends entirely on which properties are deemed essential, and it offers no independent means of settling this prior question. If essentiality is decided by fiat, the definition becomes circular; if it is left unspecified, the definition is indeterminate.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) P could not have been false under any possible circumstances, and (iii) the truth of P is ascertainable by reason alone, without recourse to observation.
conditions: P is true
P could not have been false under any possible circumstances
The truth of P is ascertainable by reason alone
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. This proposition is true. Many philosophers would hold that it could not have been false — that in any possible circumstances, water is H2O, since the chemical composition is part of what it is to be water. Yet the truth of this proposition is not ascertainable by reason alone; it required the empirical investigations of Cavendish, Lavoisier, and others to establish. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not, even though the proposition is widely regarded as necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition conflates necessity with a priority by requiring that necessarily true propositions be ascertainable by reason alone, thereby excluding necessary truths that are discoverable only through empirical investigation.
passage: One might characterise necessary truth as a proposition that is true, could not have been false under any possible circumstances, and whose truth is ascertainable by reason alone. This characterisation has the virtue of linking necessity to a recognisable mode of access — rational reflection rather than empirical inquiry. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by certain truths of natural science. Consider the proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. The proposition is true. Many philosophers maintain that it could not have been false — that the chemical composition of water is not a contingent feature but part of what it is for something to be water. Were one to encounter a substance that looked and tasted like water but had a different composition, one would say it was not water at all. Both of the first two conditions appear satisfied. Yet the truth of this proposition was not ascertained by reason alone. It required the careful experimental work of Cavendish, who isolated hydrogen, and Lavoisier, who analysed the composition of water. The definition, by requiring that necessary truths be a priori, excludes propositions whose necessity is genuine but whose discovery was empirical. It appears that necessity and a priority come apart more readily than the definition allows.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X is capable of independent existence, (ii) X is a subject of predication that is not itself predicated of anything else, and (iii) X possesses a determinate nature that distinguishes it from all other entities.
conditions: X is capable of independent existence
X is a subject of predication not itself predicated of anything
X possesses a determinate nature distinguishing it from all other entities
counterexample: Consider an electron. An electron is capable of independent existence in the sense that it can be isolated in a vacuum. It is a subject of predication — one says that an electron has a particular mass, charge, and spin — and it is not predicated of anything else. And it possesses a determinate nature, being distinguished from protons, neutrons, and other particles by its properties. All three conditions are met. Yet every electron is qualitatively indistinguishable from every other electron; they share all intrinsic properties. One might question whether an entity that lacks any individual distinguishing mark beyond its spatiotemporal location can properly be called a substance in the full sense of the term.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that X possess an individual principle of distinction — something that distinguishes it from other entities of the same kind — so that entities which satisfy all three conditions but are qualitatively indistinguishable from one another pose a difficulty for the analysis.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a substance if it is capable of independent existence, serves as a subject of predication without being predicated of anything, and possesses a determinate nature distinguishing it from all other entities. This analysis draws on a venerable tradition and captures many paradigmatic cases — a horse, a lump of gold, a human being — admirably well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the elementary particles of modern physics. Consider an electron. It exists independently; it can be isolated in a vacuum chamber. It is a subject of predication — we attribute to it mass, charge, and spin — and it is not itself predicated of anything. It possesses a determinate nature: it is an electron, not a proton, not a neutron. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet every electron is qualitatively indistinguishable from every other. They share the same mass, the same charge, the same spin. Two electrons differ only in their spatiotemporal location, and in certain quantum-mechanical contexts even this distinction becomes problematic. The definition, by requiring only a determinate nature that distinguishes the entity from other kinds, cannot address the difficulty posed by entities of the same kind that are intrinsically indistinguishable. It appears that a satisfactory account of substance must say something about what individuates particular substances, not merely what distinguishes one kind from another.

definition: A property F is a universal if and only if (i) F can be instantiated by more than one particular simultaneously, (ii) F is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) F exists independently of whether it is instantiated.
conditions: F can be instantiated by more than one particular simultaneously
F is wholly present in each of its instances
F exists independently of whether it is instantiated
counterexample: Consider the property of being the tallest building in London. At any given time, this property is instantiated by exactly one building and cannot be simultaneously instantiated by two distinct buildings, since only one can be the tallest. Thus the first condition is not satisfied. Yet it appears to be a genuine property — buildings possess it, lose it when surpassed, and it figures in true predications. If it is not a universal, one must either deny that it is a property at all or acknowledge a class of properties that the definition excludes.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate properties that are necessarily instantiated by at most one particular at a time, thereby excluding relational or superlative properties that are genuine features of the world but inherently unique in their instantiation.
passage: One might define a universal as a property that can be simultaneously instantiated by more than one particular, is wholly present in each instance, and exists independently of instantiation. This analysis captures the standard examples — redness, squareness, being charged — and accords with certain traditional accounts that emphasise the repeatability of universals. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by superlative properties. Consider the property of being the tallest building in London. At any given moment, only one building can possess this property; it is logically impossible for two buildings to be simultaneously the tallest. The first condition is not met. Yet the property figures in true predications, admits of change over time — the Shard replaced the One Canada Square as the tallest — and is as much a feature of the world as any other. If the definition excludes it, one must either deny that such predicates correspond to genuine properties or concede that the definition is too narrow. It appears that the requirement of simultaneous multiple instantiation, while characteristic of many universals, is not essential to universality as such, and that a satisfactory account must accommodate properties whose logical structure precludes multiple simultaneous instantiation.

definition: An object W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is a part of W, (ii) the parts jointly exhaust W so that no portion of W is left over, and (iii) the parts are spatially connected to one another.
conditions: Each Pi is a part of W
The parts jointly exhaust W
The parts are spatially connected to one another
counterexample: Consider the state of Hawaii. Its constituent islands — Oahu, Maui, the Big Island, and the rest — are parts of Hawaii, they jointly exhaust the territory of Hawaii, yet they are not spatially connected to one another; they are separated by stretches of open ocean. All three conditions cannot be simultaneously met if one insists on spatial connection. Yet Hawaii is unquestionably a single political entity composed of these parts.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for wholes whose parts are spatially disconnected, thereby excluding scattered objects — archipelagos, constellations, bikinis, and other familiar entities whose parts are separated by intervening space.
passage: One might propose that an object is a whole composed of parts if each part belongs to it, the parts jointly exhaust it, and the parts are spatially connected. This analysis captures the case of a table composed of legs and a top, or a house composed of bricks, mortar, and timber, where the parts are in physical contact with one another. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is furnished by the state of Hawaii. The islands of the Hawaiian archipelago — Oahu, Maui, Kauai, the Big Island, and the others — are parts of the state. They jointly exhaust its territory; there is no portion of Hawaii that is not one of its islands. But the islands are not spatially connected to one another. Stretches of open ocean separate them by many miles. The third condition fails. Yet Hawaii is a perfectly respectable whole — a political entity, a geographical region, a state of the union — composed of spatially disconnected parts. The definition, by insisting on spatial connection, cannot accommodate what one might call scattered objects: wholes whose parts are separated by intervening matter or empty space. A more adequate analysis would need to replace the requirement of spatial connection with some weaker relation — perhaps functional unity or institutional designation — that can hold between parts that do not touch.

definition: An event E is simultaneous with an event F if and only if (i) E and F occur at the same temporal instant, (ii) an ideally situated observer could witness both E and F without any temporal delay, and (iii) neither E nor F is causally prior to the other.
conditions: E and F occur at the same temporal instant
An ideally situated observer could witness both without temporal delay
Neither is causally prior to the other
counterexample: Suppose two stars, separated by a vast distance, each emit a flash of light at what we would call the same temporal instant. An ideally situated observer, equidistant from both, could witness both flashes simultaneously. Neither flash is causally prior to the other. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet according to the special theory of relativity, the simultaneity of these events is not absolute but depends upon the observer's state of motion. An observer moving towards one star and away from the other would judge the events to occur at different times. The definition presupposes an absolute temporal ordering that modern physics has shown to be untenable.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the frame of reference relative to which simultaneity is assessed, thereby treating as absolute a relation that the special theory of relativity reveals to be frame-dependent.
passage: One might characterise simultaneity as obtaining when two events occur at the same temporal instant, could be witnessed together by an ideally situated observer without delay, and stand in no relation of causal priority. This characterisation captures the ordinary notion of two things happening at the same time. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is drawn from modern physics. Suppose two distant stars each emit a flash of light at what we might call the same temporal instant. An observer positioned equidistant from both would see the flashes arrive together. Neither flash caused the other. All three conditions are met. Yet the special theory of relativity teaches us that simultaneity is not absolute. An observer in a different state of motion — travelling towards one star and away from the other — would judge the two flashes to have occurred at different times. The definition, by speaking of 'the same temporal instant' without specifying a frame of reference, treats simultaneity as an absolute relation holding independently of the observer's motion. It appears that the analysis, while adequate for the pre-relativistic worldview, cannot accommodate the discovery that temporal relations between spatially separated events depend upon the frame from which they are assessed. A satisfactory account must either relativise simultaneity to a frame of reference or provide some other means of accommodating this feature of the physical world.

definition: An object O undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) O possesses a property F at time t1, (ii) O lacks property F at a later time t2, and (iii) O continues to exist throughout the interval from t1 to t2.
conditions: O possesses property F at t1
O lacks property F at t2
O continues to exist from t1 to t2
counterexample: Suppose a man is the tallest person in a room. A taller individual then enters, and the first man is no longer the tallest. He possessed the property of being the tallest at t1, he lacks it at t2, and he continues to exist throughout. All three conditions are met. Yet the man has not undergone any intrinsic alteration — his height, his mass, his physical constitution remain exactly as they were. The change is entirely in his relational standing, brought about by the arrival of another person. One might hesitate to say that the man himself has genuinely changed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property in question be an intrinsic property of O, so that mere Cambridge changes — alterations in relational or extrinsic properties brought about by changes in other objects — are counted as genuine changes in O itself.
passage: One might propose that genuine change consists in an object's possessing a property at one time and lacking it at a later time while continuing to exist. This analysis is simple and elegant. It captures the case of an iron bar that is hot at noon and cold at midnight, or a leaf that is green in summer and brown in autumn. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is near at hand. Suppose a man stands in a room and is, at that moment, the tallest person present. A taller individual enters, and the first man ceases to be the tallest. He possessed the property of being the tallest at t1, lacks it at t2, and continues to exist throughout. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet nothing intrinsic to the man has altered — his height, mass, and physical constitution are precisely what they were. The change has occurred entirely in his relations to other objects. One might observe that this sort of alteration — sometimes called a merely relational or Cambridge change — is quite different from the genuine change that occurs when the iron bar cools or the leaf changes colour. The definition, by requiring only the gain or loss of a property without specifying what kind of property, cannot distinguish these two cases. A more adequate account would need to restrict the relevant properties to intrinsic features of the object.

definition: An entity X exists if and only if (i) X occupies a determinate region of space, (ii) X persists through some interval of time, and (iii) X is capable of entering into causal relations with other entities.
conditions: X occupies a determinate region of space
X persists through some interval of time
X is capable of entering into causal relations
counterexample: Consider the number seven. It does not occupy any region of space; it is not located here or there. Yet mathematicians make true assertions about it — that it is prime, that it is the sum of three and four — and these truths appear to hold independently of what anyone thinks or says. If one denies that numbers exist, one must explain how truths about them are possible. The first condition is plainly unmet, yet many would maintain that numbers exist in some robust sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate abstract entities — numbers, sets, propositions — that do not occupy space yet are the subjects of apparently objective truths, by restricting existence to spatiotemporal, causally efficacious particulars.
passage: A tempting analysis of existence requires that an entity occupy space, persist through time, and be capable of entering into causal relations. This characterisation fits concrete particulars admirably: tables, stones, and organisms all occupy space, endure, and interact causally with their surroundings. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the objects of mathematics. Consider the number seven. It does not occupy any region of space — one cannot point to it, weigh it, or locate it on a map. Yet mathematicians make true assertions about it. It is prime. It is the sum of three and four. It is odd. These truths appear to hold independently of human convention, for they would hold even if no one had ever thought about them. If one denies that the number seven exists, one is faced with the formidable difficulty of explaining how there can be truths about something that does not exist. The definition, by restricting existence to spatiotemporal and causally efficacious entities, excludes the entire domain of abstract objects. It appears that a more adequate account must either widen the criteria for existence to accommodate entities that are non-spatial and causally inert, or provide a compelling explanation of how truths about non-existent entities are possible.

definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C is temporally prior to E, (ii) there exists a law-like generalisation connecting events of the type of C with events of the type of E, and (iii) had C not occurred, E would not have occurred.
conditions: C is temporally prior to E
There exists a law-like generalisation connecting events of the type of C with events of the type of E
Had C not occurred, E would not have occurred
