counterexample: Suppose a man strikes a match and, simultaneously, a bolt of lightning ignites the very same piece of kindling the match was about to light. The striking is temporally prior to the fire. There is a law-like generalisation connecting match-striking with fire. And had the man not struck the match, the fire would still have occurred — courtesy of the lightning. Yet we should not say the match-striking caused the fire; the lightning did. The counterfactual condition is not met, but one might construct a variant in which the lightning would not have struck had the match not been struck — say, the match-striking discharged static that attracted the bolt — and then all three conditions are satisfied even though the match-striking is merely an indirect and accidental precursor.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the connection between C and E be non-deviant — that is, that the law-like generalisation linking them describes the actual causal pathway by which E came about, rather than an accidental or circuitous route.
passage: One might propose that an event C causes an event E if and only if C is temporally prior to E, there exists a law-like generalisation connecting events of the type of C with events of the type of E, and had C not occurred, E would not have occurred. This proposal combines regularity and counterfactual elements in a manner that appears to capture our ordinary notion of causation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed as follows. Suppose a man strikes a match near a pile of dry kindling, and at the very instant the match head flares, a bolt of lightning — attracted, let us say, by the electrical discharge from the friction of the match — ignites the kindling. The striking is temporally prior to the fire. There is a well-confirmed generalisation connecting match-striking with fire. And had the man not struck the match, the lightning would not have been attracted to that spot, so the fire would not have occurred. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the match did not cause the fire in the ordinary sense: it was the lightning that set the kindling ablaze. The match-striking is connected to the fire only through an accidental and deviant causal chain. It appears that the definition, by failing to require that the law-like generalisation describe the actual pathway by which the effect was produced, cannot distinguish genuine causes from events that are merely accidentally implicated in the causal history of an outcome.

definition: An object X at time t1 is identical with an object Y at time t2 if and only if (i) X and Y share all their intrinsic properties, (ii) X and Y occupy the same spatial region at their respective times, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting X at t1 with Y at t2.
conditions: X and Y share all their intrinsic properties
X and Y occupy the same spatial region at their respective times
There is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting X at t1 with Y at t2
counterexample: Consider a ship that is gradually dismantled plank by plank, each plank being replaced by a new one of identical material and dimensions. At the end of the process, the ship in the harbour shares all intrinsic properties with the original, occupies the same berth, and is connected to the original by a continuous spatiotemporal path. Yet suppose the old planks are reassembled in a nearby warehouse into a ship that also shares all intrinsic properties with the original. Both ships satisfy all three conditions, but they cannot both be identical with the original, for they are not identical with each other.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require uniqueness — that is, that no other object also satisfies the three conditions with respect to the original, so that identity is a one-to-one relation rather than a one-to-many relation.
passage: A natural proposal concerning the identity of objects across time holds that an object X at one time is identical with an object Y at another time if and only if they share all intrinsic properties, occupy the same spatial region at their respective times, and are connected by a continuous spatiotemporal path. This proposal combines qualitative, spatial, and continuity conditions in a manner that appears to capture the persistence of ordinary objects. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from a well-known puzzle concerning ships. Suppose a vessel is gradually dismantled plank by plank over the course of a year, each plank being replaced by a new plank of identical material and dimensions. At the end of the year, the ship moored in the harbour shares all its intrinsic properties with the original, occupies the same berth, and is connected to the original by a continuous spatiotemporal path through the gradual replacement. All three conditions are satisfied. Now suppose the removed planks are reassembled in a nearby warehouse into a ship that also shares all intrinsic properties with the original. This second ship, too, is connected to the original by a continuous spatiotemporal path — through the removal and reassembly of the planks. It appears that both ships satisfy all three conditions with respect to the original vessel, yet they cannot both be identical with it, for they are manifestly distinct from each other. The definition, by failing to require that the relation of identity be one-to-one, permits two distinct objects to each count as identical with a single predecessor.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in the actual world, (ii) P cannot be conceived as false without contradiction, and (iii) the denial of P would render some established body of reasoning unintelligible.
conditions: P is true in the actual world
P cannot be conceived as false without contradiction
The denial of P would render some established body of reasoning unintelligible
counterexample: Consider the proposition that every even number greater than two is the sum of two primes. Suppose this is true in the actual world. One cannot conceive of its falsity without contradicting every verified instance, which is to say that every attempt to imagine a counterinstance collapses into contradiction with established arithmetic. And denying it would render unintelligible the vast body of number-theoretic reasoning that presupposes it. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the proposition may be a merely contingent truth of arithmetic — a generalisation that happens to hold without being derivable from the axioms — and we should hesitate to call it necessary in the strict sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the impossibility of P's falsity be grounded in the logical or metaphysical structure of the subject matter, rather than in the mere psychological difficulty of conceiving alternatives or the practical indispensability of the proposition to an existing body of reasoning.
passage: One might characterise necessary truth as follows: a proposition is necessarily true if it is true in the actual world, cannot be conceived as false without contradiction, and is such that its denial would render some established body of reasoning unintelligible. This proposal combines actuality with inconceivability and indispensability, and it captures a wide range of propositions we ordinarily regard as necessary — the truths of logic, the axioms of geometry, and the like. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the theory of numbers. Consider Goldbach's conjecture — the proposition that every even number greater than two is the sum of two primes. Let us suppose it is true. Every attempt to conceive a counterinstance has failed; every even number tested has confirmed the conjecture, and an attempt to imagine its falsity appears to collapse into contradiction with the verified instances. Moreover, denying it would render unintelligible a substantial body of number-theoretic reasoning that relies upon it. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one might observe that the truth of the conjecture, if it holds, may be a contingent feature of the natural numbers — a generalisation that happens to hold without being derivable from any deeper principle. The definition, by relying on conceivability and indispensability rather than on logical or metaphysical derivability, cannot distinguish genuinely necessary truths from deeply entrenched contingencies.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X is capable of independent existence, (ii) X is the bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything, and (iii) X persists through changes in its non-essential properties.
conditions: X is capable of independent existence
X is the bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything
X persists through changes in its non-essential properties
counterexample: Consider a particular shadow cast by a tree on a sunny afternoon. The shadow exists independently of any observer. It is the bearer of properties — it has a shape, a size, a position, a degree of darkness — and is not itself a property of anything. It persists through changes in its non-essential properties: it grows longer as the sun descends, changes shape as branches move in the wind, and varies in darkness as clouds pass. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that a shadow is a substance in any philosophically robust sense; it is a mere absence of light, parasitic upon the tree and the sun for its very being.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the entity possess its own intrinsic causal powers — that it be capable of acting and being acted upon in its own right, rather than being a mere derivative or parasitic entity whose existence depends entirely upon the causal activity of other things.
passage: A familiar characterisation of substance holds that an entity is a substance if it is capable of independent existence, bears properties without being itself a property of anything, and persists through changes in its non-essential properties. This proposal captures much of what we ordinarily mean by the term: tables, horses, and lumps of gold all satisfy these conditions and are paradigmatic substances. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed by attending to a humble phenomenon. Consider the shadow cast by a tree on a sunlit pavement. The shadow exists independently of any particular observer. It bears properties — a shape, a size, a position, a degree of darkness — and is not itself a property of anything; one does not say that the pavement has the shadow as one of its qualities. And the shadow persists through changes in its non-essential properties: it lengthens as the afternoon wears on, shifts its shape when the branches move, and varies in darkness as clouds intervene. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet one might observe that a shadow is not a substance in any philosophically serious sense. It is a mere absence of light, entirely parasitic upon the tree and the sun for its being. It possesses no intrinsic causal powers of its own. The definition, by failing to require that a substance have its own causal efficacy, cannot distinguish genuine substances from mere derivative or parasitic entities.

definition: An object X persists from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) X exists at t1, (ii) X exists at t2, and (iii) there is no time between t1 and t2 at which X fails to exist.
conditions: X exists at t1
X exists at t2
There is no time between t1 and t2 at which X fails to exist
counterexample: Consider a watch that is completely disassembled at noon for cleaning, its every component laid out separately on a workbench, and then reassembled by one o'clock into an object qualitatively indistinguishable from the original. At eleven o'clock the watch exists. At two o'clock the watch exists. And at no time between eleven and two does the watch fail to exist — or so one might argue, for the components were present throughout. Yet we should hesitate to say the watch persisted through the interval in the ordinary sense, for at twelve-thirty there was no watch but only a collection of separated parts.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that X maintain its characteristic organisation or structure throughout the interval — that is, that the entity which exists at each intervening moment be not merely the same matter but the same organised whole.
passage: One might propose that an object persists from one time to another if and only if it exists at the earlier time, exists at the later time, and exists at every intervening moment. This characterisation appears straightforward: it requires uninterrupted existence throughout the temporal interval. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from the practice of watchmaking. Suppose a fine mechanical watch exists at eleven o'clock in the morning. At noon, a watchmaker completely disassembles it for cleaning, laying every spring, wheel, and plate separately upon his workbench. At one o'clock he reassembles the components into an object qualitatively indistinguishable from the original. At two o'clock the watch exists once more. Now, did the watch persist from eleven to two? It existed at eleven. It exists at two. And one might argue that at every intervening moment it existed — for its components were present throughout; nothing was destroyed or created. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet one might observe that at twelve-thirty there was no watch on the workbench, but only a scattered collection of parts. The definition, by requiring only continuous existence without specifying that the object must maintain its characteristic organisation or structure throughout the interval, cannot distinguish genuine persistence from a case in which an object is destroyed and a qualitatively identical successor is assembled from the same parts.

definition: A property F is a genuine universal if and only if (i) F is predicable of more than one particular, (ii) F is not reducible to the spatial or temporal location of any particular, and (iii) F is recognised as a natural kind or a natural grouping in at least one well-established science.
conditions: F is predicable of more than one particular
F is not reducible to the spatial or temporal location of any particular
F is recognised as a natural kind or a natural grouping in at least one well-established science
counterexample: Consider the property of being a specimen housed in the British Museum. This property is predicable of many particulars — the Rosetta Stone, the Elgin Marbles, the Portland Vase. It is not reducible to the spatial or temporal location of any particular, for the property concerns membership in a collection, not a position in space-time. And suppose that an established science of museology recognises 'specimens housed in the British Museum' as a natural grouping for the purposes of conservation research. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet one might observe that this property is not a genuine universal; it is an artefact of human institutional arrangements, not a feature that carves nature at its joints.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property be grounded in the intrinsic nature of the particulars that instantiate it, rather than in extrinsic or institutional relations that group particulars together by convention.
passage: One might characterise a genuine universal as a property that is predicable of more than one particular, is not reducible to the spatial or temporal location of any particular, and is recognised as a natural kind or grouping by at least one well-established science. This proposal aims to distinguish genuine universals from mere predicates by requiring scientific respectability. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from institutional practice. Consider the property of being a specimen housed in the British Museum. This property is predicable of a great many particulars: the Rosetta Stone, the Elgin Marbles, the Portland Vase, and thousands of other objects. It is not reducible to the spatial or temporal location of any particular, for what unites the specimens is their membership in a collection, not their occupancy of a single place. And suppose that an established science of conservation and museology recognises this grouping as natural for the purposes of environmental monitoring and preservation research. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet we should hesitate to regard 'being a specimen housed in the British Museum' as a genuine universal. The grouping reflects the contingent decisions of curators and the accidents of imperial acquisition, not the intrinsic natures of the objects so grouped. The definition, by requiring only scientific recognition without specifying that the science must track intrinsic features of the particulars, cannot distinguish universals that carve nature at its joints from classifications that are mere artefacts of human convention.

definition: An object W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the region occupied by W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn collectively exhaust the region occupied by W, and (iii) no Pi overlaps spatially with any other Pj.
conditions: Each Pi is spatially contained within the region occupied by W
The parts P1, ..., Pn collectively exhaust the region occupied by W
No Pi overlaps spatially with any other Pj
counterexample: Consider a brick wall. One might take the bricks as the parts: each brick is spatially contained within the wall, the bricks collectively exhaust the spatial region of the wall, and no brick overlaps any other. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the mortar between the bricks is also a constituent of the wall, and on this partition the mortar has been ignored entirely. One might instead take the bricks and the mortar joints as the parts — but then consider the wall as composed of its left half and its right half: these too satisfy the three conditions, as does the partition into horizontal courses. The definition permits indefinitely many partitions of the same whole, giving no guidance as to which decomposition yields the genuine parts.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the decomposition into parts be privileged or natural — that the parts be distinguished by some structural, functional, or compositional feature of the whole, rather than by an arbitrary spatial subdivision.
passage: A natural proposal in mereology holds that an object is a whole composed of certain parts if each part is spatially contained within the whole, the parts collectively exhaust the whole's spatial region, and no part overlaps any other. This characterisation captures the basic spatial constraints on part-whole relations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed by considering the humble brick wall. Take the bricks as the parts: each is contained within the wall, they collectively exhaust its region, and no brick overlaps any other. All three conditions are met. But the mortar between the bricks is also a genuine constituent of the wall, and the proposed partition has simply omitted it. One might revise the partition to include the mortar joints alongside the bricks, but then one might equally well partition the wall into its left half and its right half, or into horizontal courses, or into any number of arbitrary spatial subdivisions. Each such partition satisfies the three conditions. The definition, by imposing only spatial constraints without requiring that the decomposition be natural or privileged — that the parts be distinguished by some structural or functional feature of the whole — cannot discriminate between a genuine decomposition into parts and an arbitrary spatial carving.

definition: An object X undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) X possesses a property F at time t1, (ii) X lacks property F at a later time t2, and (iii) X continues to exist from t1 to t2.
conditions: X possesses a property F at time t1
X lacks property F at a later time t2
X continues to exist from t1 to t2
counterexample: Suppose Socrates is taller than Theaetetus in the year 400 B.C. Theaetetus then grows, and by 399 B.C. Socrates is no longer taller than Theaetetus. Socrates possessed the relational property of being taller than Theaetetus at the earlier time and lacks it at the later time, and Socrates continues to exist throughout. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet Socrates herself has not changed at all — she has not grown or shrunk; it is Theaetetus who has changed. Socrates has undergone what one might call a merely Cambridge change, not a genuine alteration.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property F be an intrinsic property of X — one whose gaining or losing constitutes an alteration in X itself, rather than a mere change in X's relations to other things.
passage: One might propose that an object undergoes genuine change if and only if it possesses a property at one time, lacks that property at a later time, and continues to exist throughout the interval. This characterisation appears to capture the essence of change: something that was one way becomes another way while remaining the same thing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be drawn from ancient philosophy. Suppose Socrates is taller than Theaetetus in the year 400 B.C. Over the following year, Theaetetus grows considerably, so that by 399 B.C. Socrates is no longer taller than Theaetetus. Socrates possessed the property of being taller than Theaetetus at the earlier time. She lacks this property at the later time. And she continues to exist throughout. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that Socrates has not genuinely changed at all. She has neither grown nor shrunk; her intrinsic constitution is unaltered. It is Theaetetus who has changed. Socrates has merely undergone what might be called a Cambridge change — a shift in her relational properties brought about entirely by alterations in something else. The definition, by failing to require that the property in question be intrinsic to the object, cannot distinguish genuine change from merely relational or Cambridge change.

definition: An entity X exists if and only if (i) X can be referred to by a singular term in a true proposition, (ii) X can be distinguished from every other entity, and (iii) X occupies or is located within the spatiotemporal framework.
conditions: X can be referred to by a singular term in a true proposition
X can be distinguished from every other entity
X occupies or is located within the spatiotemporal framework
counterexample: Consider the number seven. It can be referred to by a singular term in the true proposition 'Seven is a prime number.' It can be distinguished from every other entity — it is neither six nor eight nor the Eiffel Tower. Yet it does not occupy any spatial location, nor does it have a temporal beginning or end. It appears to satisfy the first two conditions whilst failing the third. Conversely, consider the fictional detective Doyle invented: the name refers successfully, the character is distinguishable from all others, and his adventures are set in London in the 1890s — seemingly satisfying all three conditions, though we should hesitate to grant him existence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify what kind of spatiotemporal location is required — whether fictional or imagined settings suffice — and thereby cannot exclude fictional entities whose adventures are set in real places and times, nor accommodate abstract entities such as numbers which plausibly exist without spatial location.
passage: One might attempt to characterise existence by stipulating that an entity exists if and only if it can be referred to by a singular term in a true proposition, can be distinguished from every other entity, and occupies or is located within the spatiotemporal framework. This proposal combines linguistic, logical, and physical conditions in a manner that appears to cover the paradigmatic cases of existing things — chairs, rivers, persons. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed by attending to the boundary between fiction and mathematics. Consider the number seven. It can be referred to by a singular term in the true proposition that seven is prime. It is distinguishable from every other entity. But it has no spatial or temporal location, and so fails the third condition. If the definition is correct, numbers do not exist — a conclusion many mathematicians would resist. Now consider the detective Sherlock Holmes, as depicted by Arthur Conan Doyle. The name functions as a singular term in many propositions readers take to be true within the fiction. The character is distinguishable from all others. And his adventures are set in London in the 1890s, giving him a spatiotemporal location of a sort. All three conditions appear to be met, yet we should hesitate to say Holmes exists. The definition, by failing to specify the kind of spatiotemporal location required, can neither accommodate abstract objects that plausibly exist without location nor exclude fictional entities whose narratives assign them a setting in real places and times.

definition: Two events A and B are simultaneous if and only if (i) A and B each occupy a definite temporal position, (ii) the temporal position of A is identical with the temporal position of B, and (iii) a competent observer could in principle verify that A and B occur together.
conditions: A and B each occupy a definite temporal position
The temporal position of A is identical with the temporal position of B
A competent observer could in principle verify that A and B occur together
counterexample: Suppose two lamps, one in London and one in Sydney, are switched on at what is, by synchronised atomic clocks, the very same instant. Each event occupies a definite temporal position. The temporal positions are identical by the best available measurement. And a competent observer — stationed, say, at the midpoint between them — could in principle verify that the light signals from the two lamps arrive simultaneously. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet an observer travelling at high velocity relative to the Earth would judge the two events to occur at different times, and the theory of relativity gives us no reason to privilege one frame of reference over another. The simultaneity that the definition pronounces absolute turns out to be frame-dependent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the simultaneity relation be invariant across all frames of reference — that it hold not merely for one observer or one coordinate system but for all possible observers, so that merely frame-relative simultaneity is excluded.
passage: One might define simultaneity by stipulating that two events are simultaneous if and only if each occupies a definite temporal position, their temporal positions are identical, and a competent observer could in principle verify that they occur together. This characterisation captures the intuitive notion of events happening at the same time and appears adequate for ordinary purposes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be constructed by appealing to the physics of high velocities. Suppose two lamps, one in London and one in Sydney, are switched on at what is, according to synchronised atomic clocks, the very same instant. Each event occupies a definite temporal position. Their temporal positions are identical by the most precise available measurement. And a competent observer stationed at the midpoint between them could in principle verify their co-occurrence by noting that the light signals arrive simultaneously. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet an observer moving at a substantial fraction of the speed of light relative to the Earth would, by the principles of relativity, judge the two events to occur at different times. The theory of relativity provides no grounds for privileging one frame of reference over another. It appears that the definition, by failing to require that the simultaneity relation be invariant across all frames of reference, cannot distinguish genuine absolute simultaneity from a merely frame-relative co-occurrence that holds for some observers but not for others.

definition: An entity E is a substance if and only if (i) E exists independently of any other particular entity, (ii) E persists through change in its properties, and (iii) E is a bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything.
conditions: E exists independently of any other particular entity
E persists through change in its properties
E is a bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything
counterexample: Consider a wave travelling across the surface of the ocean. The wave persists through changes in its properties — it may grow taller, move faster, or change shape. It bears properties — height, velocity, wavelength. And it is not itself a property of anything. Yet the wave does not exist independently of the water; it is a disturbance in a medium, ontologically dependent upon the ocean for its existence. Nevertheless, we speak of waves as individual entities that persist, move, and interact.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between genuine ontological independence and the mere appearance of independent existence exhibited by entities that are, upon closer analysis, modes or modifications of a more fundamental substance.
passage: One might propose that a substance is an entity that exists independently, persists through change in its properties, and is a bearer of properties without being itself a property. This characterisation captures the traditional notion of substance as the ultimate subject of predication — that which underlies change and supports attributes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from natural phenomena. Consider a wave travelling across the surface of the ocean. The wave persists through changes in its properties: it may grow taller as it approaches the shore, accelerate or decelerate, and alter its shape continuously. It bears properties — height, velocity, wavelength, direction. And it is not itself a property of anything; we do not predicate the wave of the ocean in the way we predicate colour of a surface. Two of the three conditions appear satisfied, and the third — independent existence — is violated, for the wave cannot exist without the water. Yet we readily speak of waves as individual entities that persist, move, and interact with one another. It appears that the definition, by requiring independent existence, cannot easily distinguish substances from persistent patterns or modifications of a more fundamental substrate, and that the line between substance and mode is not so sharply drawn as the proposed conditions suggest.

definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
Events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E
counterexample: Consider the regular succession of day and night. Night temporally precedes day; they are spatiotemporally contiguous; and events of the type 'night' are invariably followed by events of the type 'day.' All three conditions are met. Yet we should not say that night causes day. The succession is explained by a common cause — the rotation of the earth — rather than by a causal relation between the two events themselves.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the regular succession not be the product of a common cause — it cannot distinguish genuine causal relations from mere correlations produced by a shared underlying mechanism.
passage: One might define causation as the relation between temporally ordered, spatiotemporally contiguous events that stand in a relation of regular succession. This analysis, with its empiricist pedigree, captures the observable features most commonly associated with causation: temporal priority, contiguity, and constant conjunction. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed from the most familiar of natural regularities. Consider the succession of night and day. Night temporally precedes day; they are spatiotemporally contiguous; and the regularity is as exceptionless as any in nature — every night is followed by day. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that night causes day. The regular succession is explained by the rotation of the earth upon its axis, which is the common cause of both phenomena. Night does not produce day; both are effects of a single underlying process. It appears that the definition, by attending only to regularity, temporal order, and contiguity, cannot distinguish genuine causal relations from the mere co-occurrence of effects sharing a common origin. The absence of any requirement that the regular succession not be the product of a common cause leaves the account vulnerable to this familiar objection.

definition: Two events are simultaneous if and only if (i) they occur at the same moment of absolute time, (ii) an ideally placed observer could perceive both at the same instant, and (iii) neither event temporally precedes the other.
conditions: They occur at the same moment of absolute time
An ideally placed observer could perceive both at the same instant
Neither event temporally precedes the other
counterexample: Consider two flashes of lightning occurring at great distance from one another. An observer midway between them perceives them at the same instant and judges them simultaneous. An observer closer to one flash perceives it first and judges the events to be non-simultaneous. The relativity of simultaneity to the observer's frame of reference suggests that there is no fact of the matter about which pair of events occurs at the same moment of absolute time — the first condition presupposes a structure that may not obtain.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes the existence of absolute time — a universal 'now' in which all events can be ordered — and thereby fails to accommodate the possibility that simultaneity is relative to a frame of reference rather than an absolute feature of the temporal order.
passage: One might define simultaneity as the relation between events that occur at the same moment of absolute time, could be perceived together by an ideally placed observer, and stand in no relation of temporal precedence. This characterisation presupposes a structure of absolute time — a universal 'now' within which all events are ordered. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this presupposition may be drawn from the observation of distant events. Consider two flashes of lightning occurring at a great distance from one another. An observer positioned precisely midway between them perceives both flashes at the same instant and judges them simultaneous. But an observer positioned closer to one flash perceives it before the other and judges the events to be non-simultaneous. If simultaneity is an absolute relation — a matter of occurring at the same moment of absolute time — then at most one observer can be correct. Yet there appears to be no principled way to privilege one observer's judgement over the other's. It appears that the definition, by presupposing absolute time, cannot accommodate the possibility that simultaneity is relative to the observer's position and state of motion. The first condition may specify a structure that does not obtain, in which case the definition analyses a concept in terms of a framework that reality does not provide.

definition: An object A at time t₁ is identical with an object B at time t₂ if and only if (i) A and B share all their intrinsic properties, (ii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t₁ with B at t₂, and (iii) A and B are composed of the same matter.
conditions: A and B share all their intrinsic properties
There is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t₁ with B at t₂
A and B are composed of the same matter
counterexample: Consider a wooden ship that over the course of many years has every one of its planks replaced, one at a time, with new timber. At the end of the process, the ship at t₂ occupies a continuous spatiotemporal path from the ship at t₁ and shares all relevant intrinsic properties — the same shape, the same function, the same name. But it is not composed of the same matter; every plank is different. The third condition is violated. Yet common usage and maritime law regard it as the same ship.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for identity through gradual material replacement, where continuity of form, function, and organisation — rather than sameness of matter — is what secures the persistence of the object through time.
passage: One might propose that the identity of an object over time requires shared intrinsic properties, spatiotemporal continuity, and sameness of material composition. This characterisation captures the intuition that a persisting object must remain the same thing — the same stuff in the same arrangement — across time. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the material condition may be drawn from a venerable puzzle. Consider a wooden ship whose planks are replaced one at a time over many years. After each replacement, the ship sails on; the community regards it as the same vessel; maritime records attribute to it a single continuous history. At the end of the process, the ship shares all relevant intrinsic properties with the original — the same shape, the same function, the same name — and there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting the earlier and later stages. But the ship at the later time is not composed of the same matter as the ship at the earlier time; every plank has been replaced. The third condition is violated, yet common usage and practical reason regard the ship as persisting throughout. It appears that the definition, by requiring material sameness, cannot accommodate the widespread phenomenon of gradual material replacement, where identity is preserved by continuity of form and organisation rather than by retention of the same constituent matter.

definition: A universal U exists if and only if (i) there is at least one particular that instantiates U, (ii) U is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) U can be predicated of its instances without remainder — that is, the universal exhausts what the instances have in common.
conditions: There is at least one particular that instantiates U
U is wholly present in each of its instances
U can be predicated of its instances without remainder
counterexample: Consider the universal 'redness.' There is no single shade of red that is wholly present in every red thing — a fire engine, a sunset, a ruby, and a beetroot are all red, but the precise shade differs in each case. If redness is wholly present in each, then what is present in the fire engine must be identical with what is present in the sunset; but the phenomenal quality differs. One might respond that redness is a determinable rather than a determinate, but then condition (iii) is violated, for the universal 'redness' does not exhaust what its instances have in common — two scarlet objects share more with each other than either shares with a crimson object.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between determinable and determinate universals, and thereby cannot account for the hierarchical structure of properties, in which higher-order universals relate to their instances through determinates that the definition's conditions cannot accommodate.
passage: One might define the existence of a universal as requiring at least one instantiating particular, the universal's being wholly present in each instance, and the universal's exhausting what its instances have in common. This analysis captures the realist intuition that universals are genuinely shared features of reality, fully present wherever they are instantiated. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the analysis of colour properties. Consider the universal 'redness.' There is at least one particular — a fire engine, say — that instantiates it. But is redness wholly present in each red thing? A fire engine is vivid scarlet; a sunset is deep crimson; a ruby is dark garnet; a beetroot is purplish red. If redness is wholly present in each, then the same entity — the universal redness — is identical in the fire engine and the sunset. But the phenomenal quality plainly differs. One might retreat to the claim that redness is a determinable property realised through different determinates. Yet then condition (iii) is violated: redness does not exhaust what two scarlet fire engines have in common, for they share the more specific universal 'scarlet' in addition to the more general universal 'red.' It appears that the definition cannot accommodate the hierarchical structure of universals without modification.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S is conceivable without contradiction, (ii) S is consistent with the laws of nature, and (iii) there exists no established fact that rules out S.
conditions: S is conceivable without contradiction
S is consistent with the laws of nature
There exists no established fact that rules out S
counterexample: Consider the state of affairs in which an object travels faster than the speed of light. This is conceivable without contradiction — there is no logical inconsistency in the idea of superluminal travel. No established fact directly rules it out for all possible entities. Yet it is inconsistent with the laws of nature as we understand them. The second condition is violated. However, one might argue that the state of affairs is metaphysically possible even if it is physically impossible — that possibility extends beyond what the actual laws of nature permit.
missing_condition: The definition conflates physical possibility with metaphysical possibility by requiring consistency with the laws of nature, thereby failing to distinguish between what is possible given the actual laws and what is possible in the broader sense of what could obtain under different laws.
passage: One might characterise possibility as conceivability without contradiction, consistency with natural law, and the absence of any established fact that rules out the state of affairs in question. This analysis aims to combine logical, physical, and empirical criteria. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this conjunction may be drawn from the concept of superluminal travel. Consider the state of affairs in which an object moves faster than light. It is conceivable without contradiction: there is no logical absurdity in the idea, no self-referential paradox, no violation of the principle of non-contradiction. No single established fact, taken in isolation, conclusively rules it out for every conceivable kind of entity. Yet it is inconsistent with the laws of nature as presently understood. The second condition is violated. Nevertheless, one might argue — and many philosophers have argued — that superluminal travel is metaphysically possible even if it is physically impossible. The laws of nature might have been otherwise; possibility, in the broadest sense, extends beyond what the actual laws permit. It appears that the definition, by requiring consistency with natural law, conflates physical possibility with the broader notion of metaphysical possibility and thereby excludes states of affairs that are genuinely possible in the sense that they involve no logical or metaphysical impossibility.

definition: An object O undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) O possesses a property P at time t₁ and lacks P at a later time t₂, (ii) O exists at both t₁ and t₂, and (iii) the transition from possessing P to lacking P is a real alteration in O and not merely a change in O's relations to other things.
conditions: O possesses a property P at time t₁ and lacks P at a later time t₂
O exists at both t₁ and t₂
The transition is a real alteration in O and not merely a change in O's relations to other things
counterexample: Consider Socrates, who becomes shorter than Theaetetus when Theaetetus grows taller. Socrates was once taller than Theaetetus and is now shorter. He possesses the relational property 'being taller than Theaetetus' at t₁ and lacks it at t₂. He exists at both times. And the change appears to be merely relational — Socrates has not altered in any intrinsic respect. The third condition is designed to exclude this, but the difficulty is that the distinction between intrinsic and relational properties is itself contested and perhaps impossible to draw without circularity.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes a clear distinction between intrinsic and relational change but fails to provide criteria for drawing that distinction, leaving the third condition without determinate content and the analysis without a firm foundation.
passage: One might define genuine change as the transition from possessing to lacking a property, with the further requirement that the object exists at both times and that the alteration is real rather than merely relational. The third condition aims to exclude what the ancients called Cambridge change — the sort of change an object undergoes merely because something else has altered. Yet a case which reveals the difficulty of this exclusion may be drawn from the familiar example of Socrates and Theaetetus. Suppose that Socrates, without altering in any intrinsic respect, becomes shorter than Theaetetus because Theaetetus has grown. Socrates possesses the property 'being taller than Theaetetus' at one time and lacks it at a later time. He exists at both times. The third condition is intended to exclude this as a case of genuine change in Socrates, since the alteration is merely relational. But the difficulty is that the distinction between intrinsic and relational properties — upon which the third condition depends — is itself deeply contested. Is spatial location intrinsic or relational? Is mass? One might observe that the definition, by relying upon a distinction it does not explicate, presupposes more than it analyses, and that the concept of genuine change remains, after this analysis, no clearer than before.

definition: An object O persists from time t₁ to time t₂ if and only if (i) O is wholly present at t₁, (ii) O is wholly present at t₂, and (iii) O at t₁ and O at t₂ are strictly identical — they are one and the same entity.
conditions: O is wholly present at t₁
O is wholly present at t₂
O at t₁ and O at t₂ are strictly identical
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that undergoes metamorphosis and emerges as a butterfly. The caterpillar exists at t₁; the butterfly exists at t₂. There is biological continuity — the organism persists throughout — yet one may question whether the entity at t₁ and the entity at t₂ are strictly identical, for they share almost no observable properties, differ radically in structure, and the transition involves dissolution and reconstruction at the cellular level. If strict identity requires sharing all properties, then the caterpillar and the butterfly are not strictly identical, and the organism does not persist on this definition — yet biologically it plainly does.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement of strict identity — sharing all properties at both times — is incompatible with the fact that persisting objects change their properties, and it fails to specify a criterion of identity that allows for qualitative difference between temporal stages of the same persisting entity.
passage: One might define persistence as the strict identity of a wholly present entity across two times. This analysis, often associated with the endurantist tradition, maintains that persisting objects are three-dimensional entities wholly present at each moment of their existence. Yet a case which tells against the requirement of strict identity may be drawn from the phenomenon of metamorphosis. Consider a caterpillar at time t₁ that undergoes metamorphosis within a chrysalis and emerges as a butterfly at time t₂. The organism persists — biologically, there is a continuous living entity throughout the process. But the caterpillar and the butterfly share almost no observable properties. The caterpillar is a segmented, crawling larva; the butterfly is a winged, flying adult. The transition involves dissolution of most larval structures and reconstruction of the organism at the cellular level. If strict identity requires sharing all properties — as Leibniz's law demands — then the caterpillar at t₁ and the butterfly at t₂ are not strictly identical, for they differ in virtually every observable respect. The definition would entail that the organism does not persist, which contradicts the biological facts. It appears that the definition's requirement of strict identity is too demanding to accommodate the radical qualitative changes that persisting objects — particularly living organisms — routinely undergo.

definition: An object W is a whole composed of parts P₁, ..., Pₙ if and only if (i) each Pᵢ exists, (ii) the parts are arranged in a specific spatial configuration, and (iii) the parts jointly exhaust W — there is nothing in W that is not part of some Pᵢ.
conditions: Each Pᵢ exists
The parts are arranged in a specific spatial configuration
The parts jointly exhaust W
counterexample: Consider a pile of sand and a sandcastle, each composed of the same grains arranged in different configurations. Both satisfy all three conditions: the parts exist, they are spatially arranged, and they jointly exhaust the whole. Yet the sandcastle has a structure, a form, and arguably a unity that the mere pile lacks. The definition cannot distinguish between a structured whole and an arbitrary aggregate, for both are equally composed of existing parts in spatial arrangements.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the parts be unified by a principle of organisation — a form, function, or structural relation — that makes the whole more than a mere aggregate of spatially arranged constituents.
passage: One might define a whole as a composite of existing parts arranged in a spatial configuration that they jointly exhaust. This analysis captures the mereological intuition that wholes are nothing over and above their parts — that to specify the parts and their arrangement is to specify the whole completely. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the comparison of a pile of sand with a sandcastle. Suppose the same grains of sand compose, first, a formless pile, and then a carefully shaped sandcastle. In both cases, the parts exist, they are arranged in a spatial configuration, and they jointly exhaust the whole. All three conditions are satisfied equally by the pile and the castle. Yet there is a clear sense in which the sandcastle is a whole in a way that the pile is not — it has a structure, a recognisable form, and a unity conferred by the intentional arrangement of its parts. The definition, by treating any spatial arrangement of existing parts as sufficient for composition, cannot distinguish between a structured whole and a mere aggregate. One might observe that what is missing is a principle of organisation — a form, function, or structural relation — that makes the whole something more than an arbitrary collection of spatially proximate constituents.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in the actual world, (ii) the negation of P is inconceivable, and (iii) P can be known to be true by reason alone, without appeal to experience.
conditions: P is true in the actual world
The negation of P is inconceivable
P can be known to be true by reason alone
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H₂O. This is necessarily true — water could not have been a different chemical substance. But the negation of this proposition is perfectly conceivable: one can imagine a world in which the clear, potable liquid filling lakes and rivers has a different molecular structure. And the truth of the proposition cannot be known by reason alone; it required empirical investigation to discover that water is H₂O. The second and third conditions are both violated, yet the proposition is necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition conflates metaphysical necessity with a priori knowability and inconceivability of the negation, failing to recognise that some necessary truths are discoverable only through empirical investigation and have negations that are perfectly conceivable.
passage: One might propose that a necessarily true proposition is one that is true, whose negation is inconceivable, and that can be known by reason alone. This characterisation captures the traditional rationalist conception of necessity: necessary truths are those whose denial involves a contradiction detectable by pure thought. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be drawn from the natural sciences. Consider the proposition that water is H₂O. This proposition is, one might argue, necessarily true: water is essentially H₂O, and a substance that is not H₂O is not water, regardless of how closely it resembles water in its observable properties. The proposition is true in the actual world. Yet its negation is perfectly conceivable — one can coherently imagine a world in which the clear, potable, life-sustaining liquid in rivers and lakes has a different molecular composition. And the truth of the proposition was not discoverable by reason alone; it required the empirical investigations of chemistry. Both the second and third conditions are violated. It appears that the definition, by tying necessity to inconceivability and a priori knowability, cannot accommodate necessary truths that are a posteriori — truths whose necessity is real but whose discovery depends upon experience rather than pure reason.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular entity, (ii) X persists through change, and (iii) X is the ultimate subject of predication for its properties.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular entity
X persists through change
X is the ultimate subject of predication for its properties
counterexample: Consider a wave travelling across the surface of a lake. The wave exists independently of any other particular wave; it persists through continuous change as water molecules rise and fall beneath it; and it serves as the subject of predication for properties such as amplitude, wavelength, and velocity. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call the wave a substance, for it is entirely constituted by the successive motions of the water through which it passes and has no material continuity of its own.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that X possess an intrinsic material or constitutive continuity — that it not be merely a pattern propagated through a medium whose parts are themselves the genuine bearers of independent existence.
passage: One might propose that a substance is an entity which exists independently of any other particular, persists through change, and serves as the ultimate subject of predication for its properties. This characterisation has much to recommend it: it captures the intuition that substances are the fundamental furniture of the world, the enduring bearers upon which qualities depend. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the natural world. Consider a wave travelling across the surface of a still lake. The wave exists independently of any other particular wave — it is individuated by its own spatial and temporal boundaries and does not depend for its existence upon the presence of any other wave. It persists through continuous change, maintaining its identity as the same wave even as the water molecules beneath it rise and fall in succession. And it is the subject of predication: we ascribe to it a determinate amplitude, a wavelength, a velocity. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet it would be peculiar indeed to classify the wave as a substance. The wave is constituted entirely by the successive motions of the water through which it passes; it possesses no material continuity of its own, and if the water were removed, the wave would not merely cease to exist but would be revealed never to have been a thing in its own right. What the proposed definition overlooks is the requirement that a substance possess an intrinsic constitutive continuity — that it not be reducible to a mere pattern propagated through a medium whose own parts are the genuine bearers of substantiality.

definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) there is a general regularity such that events of the type of C are always followed by events of the type of E.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
There is a general regularity such that events of the type of C are always followed by events of the type of E
counterexample: Suppose that in a certain factory a whistle sounds every day at precisely noon, and that immediately after the whistle sounds the workers begin their lunch break. The whistle temporally precedes the lunch break; the two events occur in the same place; and there is an exceptionless regularity connecting them. All three conditions are met. Yet the whistle does not cause the workers to eat; rather, both the whistle and the break are effects of a common cause, namely the factory's schedule.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the regularity between C and E not be the product of a common cause — that C itself be the operative factor in bringing about E rather than merely a co-effect of some further condition.
passage: It has long been supposed that causation may be analysed in terms of temporal precedence, contiguity, and constant conjunction. On this view, an event C causes an event E just in case C precedes E, the two are spatiotemporally contiguous, and events of the type of C are invariably followed by events of the type of E. This analysis captures a great deal of our ordinary causal reasoning, and it has the merit of avoiding appeal to unobservable powers or necessary connexions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the analysis may be constructed from mundane industrial life. Suppose that in a certain factory a whistle sounds each day at precisely noon, and that immediately thereafter the workers begin their midday meal. The whistle temporally precedes the meal; the two events occur in the same place; and there is an entirely exceptionless regularity connecting them — every day, without fail, the whistle is followed by the eating of lunch. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it would be absurd to say that the whistle causes the workers to eat. Both the sounding of the whistle and the commencement of the break are effects of a common cause, namely the factory's schedule, which dictates that both shall occur at noon. The proposed analysis, by attending only to regularity and contiguity, is unable to distinguish genuine causal connexions from cases of common causation, in which two events are regularly conjoined not because one produces the other but because both are produced by a third.

definition: A temporal interval T is simultaneous with a temporal interval T* if and only if (i) T and T* have the same duration, (ii) the beginning of T coincides with the beginning of T*, and (iii) the end of T coincides with the end of T*.
conditions: T and T* have the same duration
The beginning of T coincides with the beginning of T*
The end of T coincides with the end of T*
counterexample: Consider two clocks, one stationary on the earth's surface and one aboard a spacecraft travelling at a significant fraction of the speed of light. Each clock measures what its co-located observer regards as one hour. The durations are the same by each observer's reckoning. If both observers start their clocks at the moment of the spacecraft's departure, the beginnings coincide; and if both stop their clocks after what each considers one hour, the ends coincide by each observer's own measure. Yet the intervals are not simultaneous in any absolute sense, for the relativistic dilation of time means that the spacecraft's hour encompasses a different stretch of physical time than the earthbound hour.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the coincidence of beginnings and endings be assessed from a single inertial frame of reference — that the judgement of simultaneity not be relative to the distinct perspectives of differently moving observers.
passage: One might attempt to define simultaneity of temporal intervals by stipulating that two intervals are simultaneous just in case they share the same duration and their respective beginnings and endings coincide. This appears to be a straightforward and adequate characterisation, capturing the intuitive notion that simultaneous intervals are those that unfold together, commencing and concluding at the same moments. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition may be constructed by considering the behaviour of clocks in relative motion. Suppose that one clock sits stationary upon the earth's surface and another is carried aboard a spacecraft travelling at a significant fraction of the speed of light. Each clock measures what its co-located observer regards as precisely one hour. If both observers start their clocks at the moment of departure, the beginnings coincide. If both observers stop their clocks after what each considers one hour to have elapsed, the endings coincide by each observer's own reckoning. The durations are the same. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the two intervals are manifestly not simultaneous in any absolute or physically meaningful sense, for the dilation of time entails that the hour aboard the spacecraft corresponds to a different stretch of physical time than the hour measured on the earth's surface. The definition, by failing to require that coincidence be assessed from within a single frame of reference, permits the attribution of simultaneity to intervals that are simultaneous only in a perspectival and ultimately misleading sense.

definition: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B share all the same intrinsic properties, (ii) A and B occupy the same spatial location, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 to B at t2.
conditions: A and B share all the same intrinsic properties
A and B occupy the same spatial location
There is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 to B at t2
counterexample: Suppose that a craftsman dismantles a wooden ship plank by plank, carrying each plank to an adjacent dock where he reassembles them in exactly the same arrangement. At the conclusion of this process, the reassembled ship shares all the intrinsic properties of the original, occupies the same spatial location to which it has been moved, and there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting each plank from its original position to its new one. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet if, in the meantime, new planks have been substituted into the gaps left behind at the original dock, producing a second ship that also has a continuous spatiotemporal path from the original, we have two equally strong claimants to identity with the original, and the definition provides no means of adjudicating between them.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require the uniqueness of the continuity relation — that there be at most one continuous spatiotemporal successor satisfying the stated conditions, or that the analysis specify which form of continuity takes precedence when competing claimants arise.
passage: It is natural to suppose that the identity of an object over time can be secured by the conjunction of shared intrinsic properties, sameness of spatial location, and continuous spatiotemporal connection. This analysis appears to account for the ordinary cases: a desk that remains in my study overnight is the same desk because it retains its properties, occupies the same place, and there is an unbroken path through space and time connecting its earlier and later stages. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be fashioned from the classical puzzle of the repaired ship. Suppose a craftsman dismantles a wooden vessel plank by plank, transporting each plank to an adjacent dock and there reassembling them in precisely the original arrangement. The reassembled ship shares every intrinsic property of the original; it occupies the location to which it has been conveyed; and there is a continuous spatiotemporal trajectory connecting each plank from its former position to its present one. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. But suppose further that, as each plank was removed, a new plank was inserted in its place, so that at the end of the process a second ship stands at the original dock — a ship that also has a continuous spatiotemporal history stretching back to the original vessel. We now have two objects, each satisfying all three conditions, each claiming to be identical with the original. The definition, by failing to require that the continuity relation yield a unique successor, is powerless to resolve the dispute and must therefore be judged insufficient as an analysis of identity over time.

definition: A property P is a universal if and only if (i) P can be instantiated by more than one particular, (ii) P is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) P remains numerically the same across all its instances.
conditions: P can be instantiated by more than one particular
P is wholly present in each of its instances
P remains numerically the same across all its instances
counterexample: Consider the property of being the eldest child in a family. This property can be instantiated by more than one particular — there are many eldest children across many families. It appears to be wholly present in each instance: each eldest child is fully and completely an eldest child, not partially so. And it remains numerically the same property in every case. All three conditions are met. Yet this property is essentially relational and extrinsic: it depends on the existence of siblings and a particular family structure, and it would cease to obtain if the relevant relational facts changed. One might doubt that such a purely relational and extrinsic feature constitutes a genuine universal in the metaphysically robust sense intended.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P be intrinsic to its bearers — that its instantiation not depend upon the relations in which the particular stands to other entities external to it.
passage: A familiar account of universals holds that a property qualifies as a universal provided it can be instantiated by many particulars, is wholly present in each instance, and remains numerically the same across all instantiations. This characterisation is intended to distinguish genuine universals — the repeatable features that ground objective similarity among things — from mere particulars and tropes. Yet one might construct a case which tells against the adequacy of this formulation. Consider the property of being the eldest child in a family. There are, of course, many eldest children across many families, so the property can be multiply instantiated. Each eldest child is wholly and completely an eldest child — there is no sense in which one is more or less the eldest than another. And the property appears to be numerically the same across all its instances: we do not say that there are many different properties of being the eldest child, one for each family. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it would be a curious result to classify this as a genuine universal in any metaphysically robust sense. The property is entirely relational and extrinsic: it depends not upon any intrinsic feature of the individual but upon the existence and birth order of siblings within a particular family. If the family structure were to change — if an older sibling were discovered, for instance — the property would cease to be instantiated by that individual, not because of any change in the individual but because of a change in the surrounding relational facts. The proposed definition, by neglecting to require that a genuine universal be intrinsic to its bearers, admits as universals properties whose instantiation is wholly a matter of external circumstance.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S involves no logical contradiction, (ii) S is conceivable by a rational agent, and (iii) S is consistent with all known truths about the actual world.
conditions: S involves no logical contradiction
S is conceivable by a rational agent
S is consistent with all known truths about the actual world
counterexample: Consider the state of affairs in which water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen but is instead an elemental substance. This state of affairs involves no logical contradiction — there is nothing formally inconsistent in the proposition that water is elemental. It is conceivable by a rational agent, as chemists before Lavoisier readily conceived of water as an element. And it is consistent with all known truths, provided one does not count the molecular composition of water as a known truth but merely as an empirical discovery that might have been otherwise. Yet it is widely held that water is necessarily H2O, that its molecular composition is not a contingent feature but an essential one, so that the supposed state of affairs is not genuinely possible.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S be compatible with the essential natures of the entities involved — that conceivability and logical consistency alone do not suffice to establish genuine metaphysical possibility when the identity of a natural kind is at stake.
passage: One might propose that a state of affairs is possible just in case it involves no logical contradiction, is conceivable by a rational agent, and is consistent with all known truths about the actual world. This tripartite analysis captures many of our intuitions about possibility: we readily allow that a state of affairs is possible when we can conceive of it without contradiction and when it accords with what we take ourselves to know. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed from the philosophy of natural kinds. Consider the state of affairs in which water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen but is instead an elemental substance, simple and unanalysable. There is no logical contradiction in this supposition — the proposition that water is elemental is formally consistent. The state of affairs is conceivable; indeed, for centuries before Lavoisier, the most accomplished thinkers conceived of water in precisely this way. And one might argue that the supposition is consistent with all known truths, provided one treats the molecular composition of water as an empirical finding that might, in principle, have turned out differently. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet there is a powerful philosophical tradition which holds that water is necessarily H2O — that the molecular composition of water is not a contingent accident but an essential feature, so that there is no genuinely possible world in which water exists but is not constituted by hydrogen and oxygen. The proposed definition, by resting upon conceivability and logical consistency alone, cannot distinguish genuine metaphysical possibilities from states of affairs that merely appear possible because we lack the imagination to see their impossibility.

definition: An object O undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) O has a property P at time t1, (ii) O lacks property P at a later time t2, and (iii) O exists at both t1 and t2.
conditions: O has a property P at time t1
O lacks property P at a later time t2
O exists at both t1 and t2
counterexample: Suppose that Socrates is taller than Theaetetus in 400 BC, and that by 380 BC Theaetetus has grown such that Socrates is no longer taller than Theaetetus. Socrates possesses the property of being taller than Theaetetus at the earlier time and lacks it at the later time, and Socrates exists at both times. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet Socrates has not undergone any genuine change: he has not grown shorter, altered in any intrinsic respect, or been modified in any way. The alteration is entirely in Theaetetus, and the property Socrates has lost is a purely relational one whose loss reflects a change in someone else.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property P be intrinsic to O — that the alteration not consist solely in the change of a relational property brought about by changes in entities external to O.
passage: It is commonly supposed that an object undergoes genuine change when it possesses a property at one time and lacks that property at a later time while continuing to exist throughout. This analysis appears to capture the essence of change: the leaf that is green in summer and brown in autumn has changed; the wax that is hard before the fire and soft afterwards has changed. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the classical literature on so-called Cambridge change. Suppose that Socrates is taller than Theaetetus in the year 400 BC. Over the following years, Theaetetus grows considerably, and by 380 BC Socrates is no longer taller than Theaetetus. Socrates possesses the relational property of being taller than Theaetetus at the earlier time and lacks it at the later time, and Socrates exists at both moments. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet it would be strange indeed to say that Socrates has undergone genuine change. He has not grown shorter; he has not been altered in any intrinsic respect whatsoever. The entire alteration has occurred in Theaetetus, who has grown, and the property that Socrates has lost — being taller than Theaetetus — is a purely relational feature whose loss reflects not a change in Socrates but a change in the world around him. The definition, by failing to distinguish intrinsic properties from relational ones, classifies as genuine change what is in fact nothing more than a shift in the relational profile of an object that has itself remained entirely unaltered.

definition: An object W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ... Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the region occupied by W, (ii) the Pi collectively exhaust the spatial region of W, and (iii) no Pi overlaps spatially with any other Pj.
conditions: Each Pi is spatially contained within the region occupied by W
The Pi collectively exhaust the spatial region of W
No Pi overlaps spatially with any other Pj
counterexample: Consider a flock of birds flying in formation across the sky. We may divide the spatial region occupied by the flock into non-overlapping sub-regions, each containing exactly one bird. Each bird is spatially contained within the region of the flock; the birds collectively exhaust that region; and no bird overlaps spatially with any other. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to say that the flock is a whole composed of these birds in the way that a table is composed of its legs and top, for the flock has no structural unity, no internal cohesion, and its boundaries are entirely arbitrary — a bird that drifts a few metres away neither clearly leaves nor remains part of the flock.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the parts be bound together by some form of structural, causal, or functional unity — that their arrangement constitute a genuine compositional relation rather than a merely spatial aggregation.
passage: A natural approach to the composition of wholes proceeds by way of spatial containment, exhaustion, and non-overlap: an object is a whole composed of certain parts just in case each part is spatially contained within the whole, the parts collectively fill the spatial region of the whole, and no two parts overlap. This analysis has the virtue of precision, and it captures many standard cases — a brick wall is composed of bricks that are contained within it, collectively exhaust its region, and do not overlap. Yet a case which tells against the sufficiency of this analysis may be fashioned from the behaviour of animals in collective motion. Consider a flock of birds flying in formation across an open sky. The spatial region occupied by the flock may be divided into non-overlapping sub-regions, each containing exactly one bird. Each bird is spatially contained within the region of the flock. The birds collectively exhaust that region. No bird overlaps spatially with any other. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet it would be peculiar to maintain that the flock is a whole composed of the birds in the robust mereological sense in which a table is composed of its legs and its top. The flock possesses no structural unity, no internal cohesion, no bonds holding the parts in their arrangement. Its boundaries are arbitrary and shifting — a bird that drifts a few metres from the formation neither clearly departs from nor remains within the flock. The definition, by attending solely to spatial relations, is unable to distinguish genuine composition from the mere spatial co-location of items that happen to fill a region without being unified into a single thing.

definition: An object O persists from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) O exists at t1, (ii) O exists at t2, and (iii) there is a continuous causal chain connecting O at t1 to O at t2.
conditions: O exists at t1
O exists at t2
There is a continuous causal chain connecting O at t1 to O at t2
counterexample: Suppose that a potter fashions a clay vase on Monday, and that on Tuesday the vase is accidentally shattered into fragments. The potter then collects every fragment and, on Wednesday, reassembles them into a vase that is qualitatively indistinguishable from the original. The reassembled vase exists on Monday (before destruction) and on Wednesday (after reassembly), and there is a continuous causal chain: the original vase caused the fragments, and the fragments caused the reassembled vase. All three conditions are met. Yet it is far from obvious that the reassembled vase is the same object that persisted through the interval of destruction — it passed through a period during which it did not exist as a vase at all.
