missing_condition: The definition fails to require that O exist continuously throughout the interval from t1 to t2 — that the causal chain not pass through a stage at which O ceases to exist as the kind of entity it is.
passage: One might propose that an object persists from one time to another just in case it exists at both times and there is a continuous causal chain linking its earlier and later stages. This analysis is attractive, for it grounds persistence in causation rather than in some mysterious non-causal relation, and it captures many paradigmatic cases — a stone that lies on a hillside for centuries persists because each temporal stage causally sustains the next. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the workshop of a potter. Suppose that on Monday a potter fashions a clay vase, and that on Tuesday the vase is accidentally shattered into a hundred fragments scattered across the floor. On Wednesday the potter painstakingly collects every fragment and reassembles them, producing a vase that is qualitatively indistinguishable from the original. The vase exists on Monday and on Wednesday. There is a continuous causal chain connecting the two: the original vase caused the fragments into which it shattered, and those fragments caused the reassembled vase into which they were reconstituted. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that it is far from clear that the vase has persisted through the interval of its destruction. During Tuesday, there was no vase — only a scattered collection of potsherds. The object passed through a stage at which it did not exist as a vase at all, and the causal chain, though continuous, traversed a gap in the object's existence. The definition, by requiring only that a causal chain connect the earlier and later stages without demanding that the object exist continuously throughout, permits cases in which an object is said to persist through a period of its own non-existence.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the negation of P is inconceivable, and (iii) P can be known independently of experience.
conditions: P is true
The negation of P is inconceivable
P can be known independently of experience
counterexample: Consider the proposition that every even number greater than two is the sum of two primes — Goldbach's conjecture. Suppose this proposition is in fact true. Its negation — that some even number greater than two is not the sum of two primes — appears to many mathematicians to be inconceivable, given centuries of failed attempts to find a counterinstance. And if the proposition is true, it is a truth of arithmetic and can presumably be known independently of experience. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet Goldbach's conjecture, even if true and a priori, may be a contingent truth of arithmetic rather than a necessary one, and its apparent inconceivability may reflect merely the limitations of our imagination rather than any genuine impossibility.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish genuine metaphysical necessity from subjective inconceivability — it does not require that the impossibility of the negation be grounded in the nature of things rather than in the contingent limitations of human powers of conception.
passage: It has sometimes been maintained that a proposition is necessarily true when it is true, its negation is inconceivable, and it can be known independently of sensory experience. This tripartite characterisation is designed to capture the class of truths that could not have been otherwise — the truths of logic, mathematics, and perhaps certain fundamental metaphysical principles. Yet a case which tells against the adequacy of this analysis may be constructed from the domain of unsolved mathematics. Consider Goldbach's conjecture, the proposition that every even number greater than two is the sum of two prime numbers. Suppose, for the sake of argument, that this proposition is in fact true. Its negation — the claim that some even number greater than two fails to be expressible as the sum of two primes — appears to be inconceivable to many working mathematicians, given that centuries of computation and theoretical effort have failed to produce even the suggestion of a counterinstance. And if the proposition is true, it is a truth of arithmetic that, like other arithmetical truths, can presumably be known without appeal to sensory experience. All three conditions of the proposed definition are thus satisfied. Yet it is by no means clear that Goldbach's conjecture, even if true and a priori, is a necessary truth in the robust metaphysical sense. Its apparent inconceivability may reflect nothing more than the limitations of our mathematical imagination, and there remains the open philosophical question whether truths of this sort hold of necessity or merely of fact. The definition, by grounding necessity in conceivability and a priori knowability, confuses the psychology of rational agents with the metaphysical structure of reality.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular entity, (ii) X persists through time as a numerically identical individual, and (iii) X is the bearer of properties without itself being a property of anything else.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular entity
X persists through time as a numerically identical individual
X is the bearer of properties without itself being a property of anything else
counterexample: Consider a case in which a particular wave pattern persists in the open ocean for several weeks, travelling thousands of miles while retaining its characteristic amplitude and frequency. The wave exists independently of any other particular entity, for it does not depend on any single parcel of water; the water molecules through which it passes are continually replaced. It persists through time as a numerically identical individual, being tracked and identified by oceanographers across its entire journey. And it is the bearer of properties — having a certain wavelength, a certain speed, a certain amplitude — without itself being a property of anything else. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this wave a substance, for it is a dynamic pattern of disturbance propagating through a medium, not an enduring thing in the sense that a stone or an organism is.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the entity possess its own intrinsic constitution, rather than consisting entirely in a relational pattern or disturbance sustained by other entities whose material identity is continually changing.
passage: One might propose that substance is to be understood as that which exists independently, persists through time as a numerically identical individual, and serves as the bearer of properties without being a property of anything else. This formulation captures much of what the philosophical tradition has associated with the notion of substantiality — self-sufficiency, temporal endurance, and the asymmetry between subject and predicate. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from natural phenomena. Suppose that a particular wave pattern arises in the open ocean and persists for several weeks, travelling thousands of miles while retaining its characteristic amplitude and frequency. The wave exists independently of any single parcel of water, for the molecules through which it passes are continually replaced; at no point does its existence depend upon any other particular entity. It persists as a numerically identical individual, being tracked by oceanographers across the whole of its journey. And it is the bearer of properties — possessing a determinate wavelength, speed, and amplitude — without itself being a property of anything else. All three conditions appear to be met. Nevertheless, one might observe that we should be reluctant to classify the wave as a substance. It is a dynamic pattern of disturbance propagating through a medium, and its apparent independence and persistence are artefacts of the continuous replacement of the material that sustains it. It appears that the definition, by requiring only independence, persistence, and property-bearing, fails to demand that the entity possess its own intrinsic constitution rather than consisting entirely in a relational configuration maintained by other entities.

definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) in the absence of C, E would not have occurred.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
In the absence of C, E would not have occurred
counterexample: Suppose that two assassins, acting independently, each fire a bullet at the same victim at precisely the same moment, and both bullets strike the victim simultaneously, either one of which would have been sufficient to cause death. Consider the first assassin's shot as C and the victim's death as E. C temporally precedes E, and C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous. Yet it is not the case that in the absence of C, E would not have occurred, for the second assassin's bullet would have killed the victim regardless. All three conditions are thus not jointly satisfied, and yet we should not deny that the first assassin's shot was among the causes of the death. The definition cannot accommodate cases of causal overdetermination.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for cases of symmetric overdetermination, in which multiple independently sufficient causes converge on the same effect, by requiring only counterfactual necessity rather than recognising that an event may be a cause even when the effect would have occurred without it.
passage: One might propose that causation consists in the temporal precedence of cause to effect, the spatiotemporal contiguity of the two, and the counterfactual dependence of the effect upon the cause. This analysis captures a large portion of our ordinary causal judgements and reflects the Humean tradition of grounding causation in observable relations supplemented by counterfactual reasoning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from a familiar sort of scenario. Suppose that two assassins, acting independently and without knowledge of each other, each fire a bullet at the same victim at precisely the same moment. Both bullets strike the victim simultaneously, and either would have been sufficient to cause death. Let us take the first assassin's shot as the candidate cause and the death as the effect. The shot temporally precedes the death and is spatiotemporally contiguous with it. However, it is by no means certain that in the absence of the first shot, the death would not have occurred, for the second bullet would have killed the victim in any case. The counterfactual condition is not satisfied, and yet one might observe that we cannot reasonably deny that the first assassin's shot was a cause of the death. Both shots are, intuitively, among the causes of the fatal outcome. It appears that the proposed definition, by requiring counterfactual necessity as its third condition, is unable to accommodate cases of symmetric causal overdetermination — cases in which two or more independently sufficient causes produce the same effect and neither can be singled out as necessary for the outcome.

definition: A moment T is present if and only if (i) T is the moment at which conscious experience is occurring, (ii) T is the unique temporal boundary between past and future, and (iii) T is directly accessible to awareness without the mediation of memory or anticipation.
conditions: T is the moment at which conscious experience is occurring
T is the unique temporal boundary between past and future
T is directly accessible to awareness without the mediation of memory or anticipation
counterexample: Suppose a person living with a neurological condition experiences a vivid, phenomenologically immediate auditory sensation — hearing a bell toll — that is in fact a delayed neural replay of a sound perceived three seconds earlier. The experience is phenomenologically indistinguishable from a present perception: the person is conscious of it, takes it to be occurring now, and accesses it without any sense of memory or anticipation. The moment of the experience appears to satisfy all three conditions. Yet the event that produces the experience lies in the past; the sensation is a delayed echo, not a perception of a presently occurring sound. The moment of the experience is not the unique boundary between past and future with respect to the event experienced.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the content of the experience at T correspond to events occurring at T, rather than merely being phenomenologically immediate; it conflates the subjective character of presentness with the objective temporal location of the experienced event.
passage: One might characterise the present moment as that at which conscious experience is occurring, as the unique boundary between past and future, and as the moment directly accessible to awareness without the mediation of memory or anticipation. This threefold formulation attempts to capture both the objective temporal structure of the present and its distinctive phenomenological character. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from neurological observation. Suppose a patient living with a certain condition experiences a vivid, phenomenologically immediate auditory sensation — the tolling of a bell — that is in fact a delayed neural replay of a sound perceived three seconds earlier. The experience is phenomenologically indistinguishable from present perception: the patient is conscious of the sound, takes it to be occurring now, and accesses it without any sense of recollection or expectation. It appears that the moment of the experience satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition. The patient is having a conscious experience; the experience presents itself as occurring at the boundary between past and future; and it is directly accessible without the mediation of memory. Nevertheless, one might observe that the event producing the sensation lies in the past, and the experience is a delayed echo rather than a perception of a presently occurring sound. The moment of the experience is not the genuine temporal boundary with respect to the event experienced. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the phenomenological features of conscious awareness, fails to require that the content of the experience correspond to events actually occurring at the moment in question. The subjective impression of presentness is thus conflated with the objective temporal location of what is experienced.

definition: An object A at time t1 is numerically identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B share the same spatiotemporal trajectory between t1 and t2, (ii) A and B are composed of the same material constituents, and (iii) A and B fall under the same sortal concept.
conditions: A and B share the same spatiotemporal trajectory between t1 and t2
A and B are composed of the same material constituents
A and B fall under the same sortal concept
counterexample: Let us imagine a case in which a craftsman possesses a wooden ship and, over the course of a year, gradually replaces every plank and nail with a new piece of identical material, each replacement occurring at the same location in the dry dock so that the spatial trajectory of the ship is continuous. A collector gathers every original plank and nail and, at the end of the year, reassembles them into a ship of the same design in a neighbouring dock. The ship in the original dock shares the same spatiotemporal trajectory and falls under the same sortal concept as the original, but is composed of entirely different material constituents. The reassembled ship is composed of the very same material constituents and falls under the same sortal concept, but occupies a different spatiotemporal trajectory. Neither candidate satisfies all three conditions, yet we are strongly inclined to say that at least one of them is identical with the original.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify which of its three conditions should take priority when they conflict, and more fundamentally it fails to recognise that numerical identity through time may depend upon the continuity of an organising principle or functional structure rather than upon the simultaneous satisfaction of material, spatial, and sortal requirements.
passage: One might propose that numerical identity through time requires shared spatiotemporal trajectory, sameness of material composition, and falling under the same sortal concept. This analysis appears to capture the intuition that a persisting object must trace a continuous path through space and time, retain its material constitution, and remain the same kind of thing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal may be constructed from the well-known situation of gradual replacement. Suppose a craftsman possesses a wooden ship and, over the course of a year, replaces every plank and nail with a new piece of identical material, each substitution occurring at the same location in the dry dock so that the spatiotemporal trajectory of the ship is continuous. Meanwhile, a collector gathers every discarded original piece and, at the year's end, reassembles them into a ship of the same design in a neighbouring dock. We now have two ships, each with a prima facie claim to identity with the original. The ship in the original dock shares the same spatiotemporal trajectory and falls under the same sortal concept, but is composed of entirely different material. The reassembled ship is composed of the very same original constituents and falls under the same sortal concept, but occupies a different trajectory. Neither candidate satisfies all three conditions simultaneously, and yet one might observe that we are strongly inclined to say that at least one of them is the original ship. It appears that the definition, by treating all three conditions as jointly necessary, cannot accommodate cases in which the conditions diverge, and that it overlooks the possibility that identity through time may rest upon the continuity of an organising principle or functional structure rather than upon the simultaneous fulfilment of material, spatial, and sortal criteria.

definition: A property F is a universal if and only if (i) F is instantiated by more than one particular, (ii) F is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) F is predicable of its instances in the same sense in each case.
conditions: F is instantiated by more than one particular
F is wholly present in each of its instances
F is predicable of its instances in the same sense in each case
counterexample: Consider the property of being the tallest mountain on Earth. At any given time, this property is instantiated by exactly one particular — namely, the mountain that happens to be tallest. It is wholly present in that instance and predicable of it in a determinate sense. But it is instantiated by only one particular, and so the first condition is not satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to deny that being the tallest mountain on Earth is a genuine property. Conversely, consider the property of resembling Napoleon. This property is instantiated by many particulars — every entity that bears some resemblance to Napoleon — and one might say it is wholly present in each and predicable in the same sense. Yet the resemblance is a matter of degree, and the property so described is arguably not a genuine universal but a relational and vague predicate that picks out no single repeatable nature.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property possess a determinate nature that is independent of the particular identity of any of its instances, thereby conflating genuinely repeatable universals with relational or indexical properties whose apparent universality is parasitic upon reference to a specific particular.
passage: One might define a universal as a property that is instantiated by more than one particular, wholly present in each instance, and predicable of each in the same sense. This characterisation captures the traditional idea that universals are repeatable entities, fully present wherever they are instantiated and uniform in their mode of predication. Yet cases which reveal the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from reflection on certain properties. Consider, on one hand, the property of being the tallest mountain on Earth. At any given time this property is instantiated by exactly one particular, and so the first condition is not met. Yet one might observe that there is no obvious reason to deny that it is a genuine property simply because contingent facts about geography restrict its instantiation. The condition of multiple instantiation appears to be too strong if it is meant to serve as a necessary mark of universality. On the other hand, consider the property of resembling Napoleon. This property is instantiated by a great many particulars, is in some sense wholly present in each, and appears to be predicable of each in the same sense. All three conditions seem to be satisfied. Yet one might hesitate to regard this as a genuine universal, for the resemblance is a matter of degree, and the property is parasitic upon reference to a specific particular — Napoleon himself. It picks out no single, determinate, repeatable nature but rather a relational and vague similarity to an individual. It appears that the definition, by requiring only multiple instantiation, whole presence, and uniform predication, cannot distinguish genuinely repeatable universals from relational properties whose apparent generality depends upon a tacit reference to some particular entity.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) the description of S contains no logical contradiction, (ii) S is consistent with the fundamental laws governing the actual world, and (iii) there exists a conceivable set of initial conditions from which S could arise.
conditions: The description of S contains no logical contradiction
S is consistent with the fundamental laws governing the actual world
S is consistent with some conceivable set of initial conditions from which S could arise
counterexample: Suppose one considers the state of affairs in which a particular radium atom decays at a specific instant rather than at the slightly later instant at which it actually decays. The description contains no logical contradiction; it is consistent with the laws of quantum mechanics, which assign a non-zero probability to decay at any moment within the atom's half-life; and there exists a conceivable configuration of conditions under which decay at that instant might have occurred. All three conditions are met. Yet the laws of quantum mechanics are indeterministic in a way that raises the question of whether the state of affairs is possible in some deeper metaphysical sense or merely not excluded by the formalism. The definition treats nomological and logical consistency as sufficient for possibility, but it cannot distinguish between states of affairs that are genuinely open alternatives and those that, while not formally excluded, may depend upon no sufficient reason or ground for their actualisation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that a possible state of affairs be grounded in some positive feature of reality — a capacity, a power, or a tendency — that could bring it about, rather than merely being logically and nomologically unexcluded.
passage: One might characterise possibility as the absence of logical contradiction, consistency with fundamental laws, and derivability from some conceivable set of initial conditions. This definition has the merit of uniting the formal, nomological, and physical dimensions of what we ordinarily mean by calling a state of affairs possible. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of indeterministic physical processes. Suppose one considers the state of affairs in which a particular radium atom decays at a specific instant rather than at the slightly later instant at which it in fact decays. The description of such a state of affairs contains no logical contradiction. It is consistent with the laws of quantum mechanics, which assign a determinate probability to decay at any instant within the atom's half-life and do not exclude decay at the moment in question. And there exists a conceivable set of initial conditions — indeed, the very conditions that actually obtained — from which decay at that instant might have arisen. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that there is a philosophical question whether such a state of affairs is possible in a genuinely metaphysical sense or merely not excluded by a formalism that assigns probabilities without identifying sufficient grounds for particular outcomes. The definition, by requiring only the absence of contradiction, nomological consistency, and the conceivability of suitable initial conditions, treats the mere non-exclusion of a state of affairs as equivalent to its genuine possibility. It appears that what is needed is a further requirement: that the possible state of affairs be grounded in some positive feature of reality — a power, a capacity, or a tendency — that could bring it about, rather than being merely unexcluded by law and logic.

definition: An entity X undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) X possesses a property F at time t1, (ii) X lacks property F at a later time t2, and (iii) X is numerically the same entity at both t1 and t2.
conditions: X possesses a property F at time t1
X lacks property F at a later time t2
X is numerically the same entity at both t1 and t2
counterexample: Consider a case in which a region of space is occupied by a red billiard ball at time t1, and the ball is then annihilated and instantaneously replaced by a qualitatively identical billiard ball that is blue. The region possesses (in a derivative sense) the property of containing a red ball at t1 and lacks it at t2. If we treat the region of space as the persisting entity, all three conditions are met: the region had one property, now lacks it, and is numerically the same region. Yet nothing has genuinely changed; one object was destroyed and another created. The region of space is not the kind of entity that undergoes change in the philosophically robust sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the entity X be a genuine continuant whose property alteration is the result of an intrinsic process of modification rather than the mere replacement of one occupant by another at the same location.
passage: One might define genuine change as the possession of a property by an entity at one time and its lack at a later time, with the entity remaining numerically the same throughout. This formulation captures the Aristotelian intuition that change requires a persisting subject that gains or loses a determination. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Suppose a red billiard ball occupies a certain region of space at time t1. At a later instant, the ball is instantaneously annihilated and replaced by a qualitatively identical billiard ball that is blue. If we identify the persisting entity as the region of space, then the region possessed the property of containing a red ball at t1 and lacks that property at t2. The region is numerically the same throughout. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that nothing has genuinely changed in the philosophically robust sense. No single object has been modified; rather, one object has been destroyed and another created. The region of space is a mere location, not the kind of continuant that undergoes change. It appears that the definition, by requiring only property variation in a persisting entity, fails to distinguish genuine change — the intrinsic modification of a continuant — from the mere replacement of one occupant by another at the same spatial address. A further condition is needed: that the persisting entity be a genuine continuant whose property alteration results from an intrinsic process of modification rather than from substitution.

definition: An object O persists from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) O exists at t1 and at t2, (ii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting O's location at t1 with O's location at t2, and (iii) O retains its essential properties throughout the interval from t1 to t2.
conditions: O exists at t1 and at t2
There is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting O's location at t1 with O's location at t2
O retains its essential properties throughout the interval from t1 to t2
counterexample: Suppose a particular amoeba exists at time t1 and then undergoes binary fission, producing two daughter amoebae at time t2. One of the daughter amoebae occupies a location connected by a continuous spatiotemporal path with the original amoeba's location, and it retains all the essential properties of an amoeba — it is a single-celled organism capable of reproduction, locomotion, and nutrition. All three conditions of the definition appear to be met for this daughter organism. Yet we should hesitate to say that the original amoeba persists as the daughter, for the very same argument could be made with equal force for the other daughter. The definition provides no principled basis for identifying one rather than the other as the continuant of the original, and to say that both are identical with the original would violate the transitivity of identity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the spatiotemporal path be non-branching — that at no point during the interval does the path split into two or more paths each of which satisfies the stated conditions — thereby leaving the analysis unable to handle cases of fission.
passage: One might analyse persistence as the existence of an object at two times connected by a continuous spatiotemporal path along which the object retains its essential properties. This proposal has the virtue of combining temporal existence, spatial continuity, and essential-property preservation into a single account. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from biological processes. Suppose a particular amoeba exists at time t1 and then undergoes binary fission, yielding two daughter organisms at time t2. One daughter occupies a location connected by a continuous spatiotemporal path with the original amoeba's location at t1. It retains all the essential properties of an amoeba: it is a single-celled organism capable of locomotion, nutrition, and reproduction. All three conditions are satisfied for this daughter. Nevertheless, one might observe that the very same argument applies with equal force to the other daughter, which also traces a continuous spatiotemporal path from the point of fission and retains all essential amoeba-properties. The definition provides no principled basis for identifying one daughter rather than the other as the continuant of the original. To say that both are identical with the original would violate the logical principle that identity is transitive, for the two daughters are plainly not identical with each other. It appears that the definition, by requiring only existence at two times, a continuous path, and essential-property retention, fails to demand that the spatiotemporal path be non-branching. Without such a requirement, the analysis is unable to accommodate cases of fission, in which a single entity gives rise to two or more successors each satisfying all the stated conditions.

definition: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ... Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is a proper part of W, (ii) no Pi overlaps any other Pj, and (iii) the sum of the Pi exhausts W without remainder.
conditions: Each Pi is a proper part of W
No Pi overlaps any other Pj
The sum of the Pi exhausts W without remainder
counterexample: Consider a particular living organism — say, a cat. One might decompose the cat into its cells: each cell is a proper part of the cat, no cell overlaps any other cell (each occupies a distinct spatial region), and the cells collectively exhaust the cat without remainder. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose we alternatively decompose the cat into its organ systems: the skeletal system, the circulatory system, the nervous system, and so forth. Each system is a proper part of the cat, the systems collectively exhaust the cat, and yet the systems do overlap — the blood vessels of the circulatory system penetrate every organ of every other system. This second decomposition violates condition (ii), and so on the proposed definition the organ systems do not compose the cat. Yet it seems plainly correct that the cat is composed of its organ systems.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the fact that a whole may admit of multiple legitimate decompositions, some of which involve overlapping parts, and that the non-overlap condition is too restrictive for entities whose structural organisation permits cross-cutting partitions.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a whole composed of certain parts just in case each part is a proper part of the whole, no two parts overlap, and the parts collectively exhaust the whole without remainder. This formulation captures the mereological intuition of a neat partition: the whole is divided into mutually exclusive, jointly exhaustive pieces. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the domain of biological organisation. Consider a particular cat. One may decompose the cat into its cells: each cell is a proper part of the cat, no cell overlaps any other, and the cells collectively exhaust the organism. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied by this cellular decomposition. But suppose one decomposes the cat instead into its organ systems — the skeletal, circulatory, nervous, muscular, and digestive systems, among others. Each system is a proper part of the cat, and the systems collectively exhaust the organism. Yet the systems overlap extensively: the blood vessels of the circulatory system penetrate every organ of every other system; the nerves of the nervous system ramify through the muscular system and beyond. The second condition is violated, and so, on the proposed definition, the organ systems do not compose the cat. Yet one might observe that it seems plainly correct that the cat is composed of its organ systems in a perfectly legitimate sense. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon non-overlap, is too restrictive for entities whose structural organisation permits multiple cross-cutting decompositions, each of which represents a genuine way in which the whole is constituted by its parts.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in the actual world, (ii) the negation of P is inconceivable, and (iii) no possible set of circumstances could render P false.
conditions: P is true in the actual world
The negation of P is inconceivable
No possible set of circumstances could render P false
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H2O. This proposition is true in the actual world, and many philosophers would hold that no possible set of circumstances could render it false. Yet the negation of this proposition — that water is not H2O — is perfectly conceivable, at least in the sense that a competent thinker who does not know the chemical composition of water can coherently entertain the thought that water might turn out to be some other substance. Prior to the chemical analysis of water, the proposition that water is not H2O was widely entertained without any sense of incoherence. Condition (ii) is thus not satisfied, yet the proposition may still be necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition conflates conceivability with metaphysical possibility, failing to recognise that what is conceivable may outstrip what is genuinely possible, and that a proposition may be necessary despite the conceivability of its negation, particularly in cases involving a posteriori necessities.
passage: One might define a necessarily true proposition as one that is true in the actual world, whose negation is inconceivable, and which no possible set of circumstances could render false. This characterisation attempts to unite actuality, the limits of conceivability, and modal invariance into a single account of necessary truth. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the philosophy of natural kinds. Consider the proposition that water is H2O. This proposition is true in the actual world, and there are strong reasons to hold that no possible set of circumstances could render it false: the identity of water with H2O is, one might argue, a matter not of contingent correlation but of what water is. Nevertheless, the negation of the proposition — that water is not H2O — appears to be perfectly conceivable, at least in the minimal sense that a competent thinker unacquainted with chemistry can coherently entertain the thought that water might turn out to be composed of some other substance. Prior to the chemical analysis of water, the proposition that water is not H2O was widely entertained without any sense of conceptual incoherence. It appears that condition (ii) of the proposed definition is not satisfied, and yet the proposition may well be necessarily true. One might observe that the definition conflates conceivability with metaphysical possibility. There exist propositions whose negations are conceivable and yet whose truth holds with metaphysical necessity — propositions whose necessity is discovered empirically rather than grasped a priori. The definition, by requiring inconceivability of the negation as a necessary condition of necessary truth, cannot accommodate such cases.

definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular entity, (ii) X is the bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything, and (iii) X persists through changes in its non-essential properties.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular entity
X is the bearer of properties but is not itself a property of anything
X persists through changes in its non-essential properties
counterexample: Consider a case in which a wave travels across the surface of the ocean. The wave bears properties — it has a determinate height, velocity, and wavelength — and it is not itself a property of any other entity. Moreover, the wave persists through changes in certain of its non-essential features: it may grow taller or slower without ceasing to be that very wave. Yet the wave does not exist independently of the water through which it propagates; it is a disturbance in a medium, not a self-subsistent particular. One might suppose that condition (i) excludes it, but the wave does not depend upon any single particular body of water — it may pass from one region to another — and so in a certain reading it satisfies even this requirement. The analysis thus appears insufficient to exclude entities that are manifestly not substances in the traditional sense.
missing_condition: X must not be constitutively dependent upon a medium or substratum of which it is a modification or pattern
passage: It has long been a central tenet of metaphysical enquiry that substances are to be distinguished from other categories of being by their self-subsistence, their role as bearers of properties, and their capacity to endure through alteration. One supposes, in offering such an analysis, that these three conditions jointly suffice to mark off substances from accidents, relations, events, and other non-substantial entities. Yet reflection upon certain natural phenomena ought to give us pause. Let us consider the case of an ocean wave — not the water itself, but the pattern of displacement that travels across the surface. Such a wave possesses determinate properties: it has a measurable height, a velocity, and a wavelength. It is not a property of any other entity in the straightforward sense; one does not predicate the wave of the ocean as one predicates blueness of the sky. Furthermore, the wave persists through changes in its non-essential features — it may increase in height as it approaches the shore, or diminish in speed as it encounters an obstacle, without our wishing to say that it has been replaced by a numerically distinct wave. It might appear, then, that the wave satisfies all three of the proposed conditions. And yet it is by no means evident that we should wish to count a wave as a substance. It is, after all, a pattern of disturbance in a medium; it depends for its existence upon the water through which it propagates, even if it does not depend upon any particular parcel of water. The case reveals that the proposed analysis lacks a condition addressing constitutive dependence upon a medium, and that without such a condition, mere patterns and modifications may be admitted to the category of substance on terms that would have struck the classical metaphysician as quite unacceptable.

definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
Events of type C are regularly followed by events of type E
counterexample: Consider a case in which a factory whistle sounds every day at noon, and every day at noon the workers in an adjacent factory cease their labour and go to lunch. The sounding of the whistle temporally precedes the cessation of work; the two events are spatiotemporally contiguous, the workers being within earshot; and events of the first type are invariably followed by events of the second type. Yet the whistle in the first factory does not cause the workers in the second factory to stop, for they are responding to their own factory's bell, which happens to ring at the same moment. The regular succession and the contiguity are genuine, but the causal connection is absent.
missing_condition: There must be a genuine productive or counterfactual connection between C and E, not merely regular succession
passage: The regularity theory of causation, in its most familiar formulation, holds that for one event to cause another it is necessary and sufficient that the first temporally precede the second, that the two be spatiotemporally contiguous, and that events of the relevant types be regularly conjoined. This analysis has the considerable virtue of avoiding reference to mysterious powers or necessary connexions in nature, relying instead upon observable features of the world. Yet it is by no means difficult to construct cases which satisfy all three conditions and yet in which we should be most reluctant to affirm that a causal relation obtains. Suppose that a factory whistle sounds each day precisely at noon, and that in a neighbouring factory the workers invariably stop their machines and proceed to the canteen at the same hour. The sounding of the whistle in the first factory precedes, by a few seconds, the cessation of work in the second. The events are spatiotemporally contiguous — the workers can hear the whistle plainly. And the regularity is perfect: every day without exception, the whistle is followed by the stoppage. All three conditions are met, and yet the whistle does not cause the workers to stop. They stop because their own factory bell rings at noon; the coincidence of timing is just that — a coincidence. One is therefore led to suspect that the regularity account, however attractive in its parsimony, omits something essential. What appears to be wanting is a condition requiring that C and E be connected not merely by regularity and contiguity but by some genuine productive or counterfactual link — that, had C not occurred, E would not have occurred either, or that C contributes to the bringing about of E in some manner more intimate than mere habitual conjunction.

definition: An instant T is the present if and only if (i) T is the instant at which conscious experience is occurring, (ii) T divides the temporal series into past and future, and (iii) T is uniquely privileged in being the only instant that is directly accessible to perception.
conditions: T is the instant at which conscious experience is occurring
T divides the temporal series into past and future
T is uniquely privileged in being the only instant that is directly accessible to perception
counterexample: Suppose that a woman is listening to a melody. Her experience at any given instant encompasses not merely the note sounding at that instant but also a lingering awareness of the notes just past and an anticipation of those about to come. Her conscious experience thus extends over a temporal interval rather than being confined to a single durationless point. If the present is identified with the instant of conscious experience, then one must say either that several instants are simultaneously present — which contradicts condition (ii) — or that the present has duration, in which case it does not divide the temporal series into two neat halves at a single point.
missing_condition: The definition must accommodate the extended or specious character of lived temporal experience rather than treating the present as a durationless point
passage: The notion of the present has seemed to many philosophers both indispensable and yet resistant to precise analysis. A natural attempt proceeds as follows: the present is that unique instant at which conscious experience is occurring, which divides the whole of time into what has been and what is yet to come, and which alone is directly accessible to perception — the past being available only to memory, and the future only to anticipation. This definition has a certain intuitive appeal, for it appears to capture the sense in which the present moment is, as it were, the locus of our engagement with reality. Yet one may construct a case which tells against this analysis in a manner that is both simple and philosophically instructive. Consider a woman who is listening to a melody. Her experience at any given instant is not confined to the single note that is sounding at that mathematical point; rather, she retains a fading awareness of the notes just past and entertains a forward-directed anticipation of the notes to come. Her conscious experience, in other words, has a temporal breadth — what some have called a specious present — that extends over an interval of appreciable duration. If we insist that the present is the instant at which experience occurs, we appear to be committed to saying that several instants are simultaneously present, for the woman's experience encompasses a stretch of time rather than a single point. But this would deprive condition (ii) of its force, for a present that spans an interval cannot divide the temporal series into two exhaustive halves at a single boundary. One is thus led to suspect that the analysis is insufficiently attentive to the phenomenological character of temporal experience, and that it must be revised to accommodate the extended nature of the experienced present.

definition: Object A is numerically identical with object B if and only if (i) A and B share all their properties, (ii) A and B occupy the same spatiotemporal region, and (iii) any predicate true of A is true of B and vice versa.
conditions: A and B share all their properties
A and B occupy the same spatiotemporal region
Any predicate true of A is true of B and vice versa
counterexample: Consider a case in which a bronze statue and the lump of bronze of which it is composed occupy precisely the same spatiotemporal region at every moment of their co-existence. They share all their physical properties — weight, colour, temperature, chemical composition. Yet certain predicates appear to apply to one but not the other: the lump of bronze can survive being melted down and recast, but the statue cannot. The lump existed before the sculptor gave it form, but the statue did not. If the predicate 'could survive being melted down' is true of the lump and false of the statue, then condition (iii) fails, and yet conditions (i) and (ii) appear to hold.
missing_condition: The analysis must account for modal and historical properties, which may distinguish co-located entities that share all their categorical properties
passage: It is a familiar principle that if two things are truly identical — if they are, in the strictest sense, one and the same thing — then whatever is true of the one must be true of the other, and conversely. To this principle one may add the further conditions that genuinely identical objects must share all their properties and must coincide in their spatiotemporal location. These requirements, taken jointly, seem to provide a complete and satisfactory analysis of numerical identity. Yet a well-known puzzle in the metaphysics of material objects suggests otherwise. Let us consider a sculptor who fashions a statue from a lump of bronze. The statue and the lump of bronze occupy exactly the same region of space at every moment of their concurrent existence; they weigh the same, possess the same colour and chemical composition, and are, in every observable respect, physically indistinguishable. Conditions (i) and (ii) are thus satisfied in full. But now suppose we ask whether the lump of bronze could survive being melted down. It seems natural to answer in the affirmative — the bronze persists through such a transformation, being merely reshaped. The statue, however, could not survive melting; it would be destroyed utterly. If this is correct, then the predicate 'could survive being melted' is true of the lump and false of the statue, which means that condition (iii) fails. We have two apparently distinct entities that share all their actual, categorical properties and occupy the same region, yet differ with respect to their modal properties — what could happen to them. The analysis, one is inclined to conclude, is insufficient as it stands, for it takes no account of modal and historical properties, which may serve to distinguish entities that are, in every other respect, indistinguishable.

definition: A universal U exists if and only if (i) U is instantiated by at least one particular, (ii) U is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) U is the ground of genuine resemblance between its instances.
conditions: U is instantiated by at least one particular
U is wholly present in each of its instances
U is the ground of genuine resemblance between its instances
counterexample: Consider the case of the property of being the only member of a species — a property instantiated, let us suppose, by a single surviving dodo in the final days before extinction. This property is instantiated by at least one particular, namely the last dodo. It is wholly present in that instance, since there is only one. And it would seem to ground a genuine resemblance if there were another instance — but there cannot be another, for if a second dodo existed, neither would instantiate the property of being the sole surviving member. The property is thus necessarily singly instantiated, and the third condition, which concerns resemblance between instances, becomes vacuous.
missing_condition: The definition must require that U be capable in principle of multiple instantiation, so that the resemblance condition is non-trivially satisfiable
passage: The realist about universals maintains that properties such as redness or squareness are entities in their own right, existing wherever they are instantiated and grounding the resemblances that we observe among particulars. A natural formulation of this view holds that a universal exists provided it has at least one instance, is wholly present in each instance, and serves as the ground of genuine resemblance between the things that share it. This analysis is meant to capture what is distinctive about universals as opposed to mere sets or predicates: they are in the world, repeated in their instances, and they explain why those instances are alike. Yet reflection upon a certain class of properties should make us hesitate. Suppose that a species is reduced to a single surviving member — the last of its kind. This individual instantiates the property of being the sole remaining member of its species. That property satisfies condition (i), for it has at least one instance. It satisfies condition (ii), for it is wholly present in the one creature that possesses it. But condition (iii) is more troublesome. The property is supposed to ground resemblance between instances, yet it is a property that, by its very nature, cannot be shared by two things at once. If a second creature of the same species were discovered, neither would any longer possess the property in question. The resemblance condition thus becomes vacuous — not merely unsatisfied but in principle unsatisfiable. One might contend that a property of this sort is not a genuine universal, but the proposed definition gives us no grounds for excluding it, since it meets every stated requirement. The analysis, it appears, ought to include a condition stipulating that a universal must be capable, at least in principle, of multiple instantiation, if the appeal to resemblance is to do any genuine philosophical work.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S does not entail a logical contradiction, (ii) S is compatible with the laws of nature, and (iii) there exists some conceivable set of initial conditions from which S could be realised.
conditions: S does not entail a logical contradiction
S is compatible with the laws of nature
There exists some conceivable set of initial conditions from which S could be realised
counterexample: Suppose that a certain radioactive atom has a half-life of one billion years, and consider the state of affairs in which this atom decays within the next five minutes. This does not entail any logical contradiction, it is fully compatible with the laws of quantum mechanics, and there exists no barrier in principle to its occurring — the initial conditions of the atom's quantum state permit such a decay. All three conditions are satisfied, and the decay is indeed possible. But now consider the state of affairs in which every radioactive atom in the universe decays within the next five minutes. This too entails no logical contradiction, is compatible with the laws governing each individual atom, and for each atom there exists a conceivable initial condition permitting its decay. Yet the probability of this collective state of affairs is so astronomically minute as to be, for all practical purposes, impossible — raising the question of whether the proposed definition captures a notion of possibility that is too permissive.
missing_condition: The definition must distinguish between bare logical or nomological possibility and a more robust notion that takes account of aggregate probability and physical feasibility
passage: When we enquire whether a given state of affairs is possible, we are asking, at a minimum, whether it involves no contradiction, whether it comports with the natural order as we understand it, and whether there exist some initial conditions under which it might come about. These three requirements seem individually necessary and, when taken together, jointly sufficient. Yet one may observe that they admit cases which strain the ordinary sense of the word 'possible' beyond what seems warranted. Consider the behaviour of radioactive atoms, each of which has a determinate probability of decaying within any given interval. For a single atom with a half-life of one billion years, it is entirely possible — logically, physically, and in terms of conceivable initial conditions — that it should decay within the next five minutes. The probability is vanishingly small, but it is not zero, and the laws of nature permit it without qualification. Let us now extend the case. Suppose we ask whether it is possible that every radioactive atom in the universe should decay simultaneously within the next five minutes. For each atom individually, the three conditions are satisfied. The collective state of affairs entails no logical contradiction, is compatible with the laws governing radioactive decay, and for each constituent event there exist initial conditions that would permit it. Yet the combined probability of all these independent events occurring together is so unfathomably small that one hesitates to describe the result as genuinely possible in any useful sense. The case suggests that our proposed analysis is too permissive — that it captures a notion of bare logical or nomological possibility that does not adequately distinguish between the practically accessible and the merely non-contradictory. A more satisfactory account would need to incorporate some sensitivity to aggregate probability or physical feasibility.

definition: An object O undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) O possesses a property P at time t1, (ii) O lacks property P at a later time t2, and (iii) O persists as numerically the same object from t1 to t2.
conditions: O possesses a property P at time t1
O lacks property P at a later time t2
O persists as numerically the same object from t1 to t2
counterexample: Consider a case in which a poker is cold at noon and hot at one o'clock, but let us add that at half past twelve the poker is removed from the room and replaced, without anyone's noticing, by a qualitatively identical poker that is then heated. The second poker is hot at one o'clock and the first was cold at noon, so the conjunction of conditions (i) and (ii) is satisfied if we carelessly identify the two pokers as one. Condition (iii) is designed to prevent this, yet it reveals a deeper difficulty: for in order to verify that the same object persists, we must already have a criterion of identity over time, and this criterion will itself appeal to some notion of continuity that is not specified in the definition. The definition is thus either circular or incomplete.
missing_condition: The definition must include or presuppose an independent criterion of numerical identity over time, such as spatiotemporal continuity of material constitution
passage: The concept of change would seem to be among the most straightforward in the whole of metaphysics: an object changes when it possesses a property at one time and lacks it at another, provided it remains the same object throughout. This simple analysis has the merit of distinguishing genuine change from mere replacement — the substitution of one object for another is not a change in anything, but rather the cessation of one thing and the arrival of another. Yet the very condition that is meant to secure this distinction — the requirement that the object persist as numerically the same entity — introduces a difficulty that is by no means easy to resolve. Suppose a poker is cold at noon. At half past twelve it is surreptitiously removed and replaced by a qualitatively identical poker, which is then placed in the fire and becomes hot by one o'clock. An observer who was not privy to the substitution would naturally say that the poker has changed from cold to hot. The first two conditions of our definition are, on a careless reading, satisfied: there is coldness at noon and hotness at one o'clock. It is condition (iii) that must do the work of excluding this case, for the poker at one o'clock is not numerically the same as the poker at noon. But how are we to determine this? To verify that an object has persisted, we require a criterion of numerical identity over time, and any such criterion will itself invoke notions — spatiotemporal continuity, continuity of material constitution, or the like — that go well beyond what the definition provides. The analysis, it appears, presupposes precisely what it ought to explain. Without an independent and non-circular account of persistence, the third condition remains an empty placeholder rather than a genuine constraint, and the definition of change is accordingly incomplete.

definition: An object O persists from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) O exists at t1 and at t2, (ii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting O at t1 with O at t2, and (iii) O retains its essential properties throughout the interval from t1 to t2.
conditions: O exists at t1 and at t2
There is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting O at t1 with O at t2
O retains its essential properties throughout the interval from t1 to t2
counterexample: Consider a case in which a wooden ship is gradually repaired over the course of many years, each plank being replaced as it decays, until no original timber remains. At each stage the ship exists, the spatiotemporal path is continuous, and the essential properties of the ship — its form, its function, its capacity to sail — are retained. All three conditions are met, and yet a reasonable man might hesitate to affirm that the ship at the end is the same object as the ship at the beginning, for every material part has been exchanged. The difficulty deepens if the old planks are reassembled into a second ship, which also satisfies conditions (i) through (iii) with respect to the original.
missing_condition: The definition must address material continuity or specify how essential properties are to be individuated when form and matter come apart
passage: The question of what it is for an object to persist through time has received a deceptively simple answer: the object must exist at the relevant times, there must be a continuous spatiotemporal path linking its earlier and later stages, and it must retain whatever properties are essential to its being the kind of thing it is. This analysis appears to handle the ordinary cases without difficulty — a table that stands in the same room for fifty years, suffering only superficial scratches, plainly satisfies all three conditions. Yet one may reflect upon a case which has troubled philosophers since antiquity. Imagine a wooden ship that is repaired, plank by plank, over the course of many years. Each decayed timber is removed and replaced by a sound one of the same dimensions. At no point is the ship out of existence; at no point is there a discontinuity in its spatiotemporal path; and at every stage the ship retains its essential properties — its form, its seaworthiness, its function as a vessel. All three conditions are satisfied throughout the process. Yet when the process is complete and every original plank has been replaced, it is by no means obvious that the resulting ship is numerically identical with the original. The difficulty becomes more acute if the discarded planks are collected and reassembled into a ship of the same design. This second ship also exists, is spatiotemporally continuous with the original material, and possesses the essential properties of a ship. We now have two candidates for identity with the original vessel, each satisfying all three conditions, which is plainly absurd if numerical identity is at issue. The case reveals that the proposed definition is silent on the question of material continuity, and that without some account of how essential properties are to be individuated when form and matter diverge, the analysis admits of outcomes that no philosopher should willingly accept.

definition: An entity X is a proper part of an entity Y if and only if (i) X is spatially contained within Y, (ii) the removal of X would diminish Y, and (iii) X and Y are not identical.
conditions: X is spatially contained within Y
The removal of X would diminish Y
X and Y are not identical
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man swallows a marble. The marble is spatially contained within the man's body; the removal of the marble would diminish the man in the sense that his total mass and spatial extent would be reduced; and the marble is plainly not identical with the man. Yet we should not ordinarily say that the marble is a proper part of the man in the way that his heart or his liver is. It is merely lodged within him, not integrated into his organic constitution. The three conditions are satisfied, but the intuitive verdict is that the marble is not genuinely a part of the man.
missing_condition: X must be functionally or constitutively integrated into Y, not merely spatially enclosed by it
passage: The notion of parthood is fundamental to our understanding of composite objects, and it is tempting to analyse it in straightforward spatial and mereological terms. One says that X is a proper part of Y provided that X is contained within the spatial boundaries of Y, that Y would be diminished by the removal of X, and that X and Y are distinct entities. This analysis is economical and appears to capture what we mean when we say that the engine is part of the motor-car, or that the heart is part of the body. Yet one may construct a case which reveals its insufficiency. Suppose a man inadvertently swallows a glass marble. The marble now resides within the spatial boundaries of his body; it is spatially contained within him in the clearest possible sense. Were the marble to be removed — by surgical means, let us say — the man's total mass and volume would be reduced; he would, in a perfectly literal sense, be diminished. And the marble is assuredly not identical with the man. All three conditions are thus fulfilled. Yet it would be most peculiar to maintain that the marble is a proper part of the man in the same way that his heart or his femur is. The marble bears no functional relation to the man's organic constitution; it plays no role in his biological processes; it is merely lodged within him as a foreign body. One is therefore led to conclude that spatial containment and the possibility of diminishment do not exhaust what is meant by parthood, at least when the composite entity in question is an organised whole. Some further condition — perhaps requiring that the part be functionally or constitutively integrated into the whole — appears to be needed if the analysis is to accord with our considered judgements about which things are genuinely parts of which.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in the actual world, (ii) the denial of P entails a contradiction, and (iii) no conceivable state of affairs obtains in which P is false.
conditions: P is true in the actual world
The denial of P entails a contradiction
No conceivable state of affairs obtains in which P is false
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. This proposition is true in the actual world; many philosophers would hold that its denial, once the nature of water is properly understood, entails something like a contradiction — for water just is H2O. And it may seem that no conceivable state of affairs obtains in which water is not so composed. Yet a chemist of the eighteenth century could conceive quite coherently of water being an element rather than a compound, and many have thought that this conceivability constitutes evidence of a state of affairs in which P is false. If conceivability is taken as the test of condition (iii), then the proposition fails; if it is not, then the condition is not genuinely operative.
missing_condition: The definition must specify what is meant by conceivability and distinguish between genuine metaphysical possibility and mere cognitive or imaginative possibility
passage: The analysis of necessity has long been bound up with the notions of truth, contradiction, and conceivability. One says that a proposition is necessarily true if it holds in fact, if its negation is contradictory, and if no conceivable state of affairs falsifies it. These three conditions are meant to work in concert: the first anchors the proposition in actuality, the second connects it to the logical structure of thought, and the third extends its reach to all that might be the case. And for the propositions of logic and mathematics, this analysis seems entirely satisfactory — no one can coherently conceive of a state of affairs in which two and two do not make four. But consider the proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. This is true in the actual world, and there is a strong philosophical tradition according to which its truth is necessary — water just is that substance, and anything not so composed would not be water. The denial of this proposition, on such a view, is in some sense contradictory, though not in the manner of a formal logical contradiction. But now examine condition (iii). A natural philosopher of the eighteenth century, ignorant of the chemical composition of water, could conceive without the slightest difficulty of water being an elemental substance rather than a compound. Her conception was coherent, detailed, and free of any evident contradiction. If conceivability is to serve as the test of condition (iii), then the proposition about water's composition fails, for there is a conceivable state of affairs in which it is false. If conceivability is not the test, then condition (iii) stands in need of an alternative interpretation that the definition does not supply. One suspects that the root of the difficulty lies in the failure to distinguish between genuine metaphysical possibility and the merely cognitive or imaginative variety, and that until this distinction is properly drawn, the analysis of necessity will remain unsatisfactorily ambiguous.

definition: A substance is an entity that (i) exists independently of any other particular entity, (ii) serves as the ultimate subject of predication, and (iii) persists through changes in its accidental properties.
conditions: The entity exists independently of any other particular entity
The entity serves as the ultimate subject of predication
The entity persists through changes in its accidental properties
counterexample: Consider a case in which a magnetic field exists independently of any other particular entity in a region of empty space, serves as the ultimate subject of predication (we say of it that it has a certain intensity and orientation), and persists through changes in its accidental properties (its intensity fluctuates while it remains numerically the same field). Yet one might hesitate to call such a field a substance, for it appears to be constitutively dependent upon the spatial region it occupies and upon the physical laws that sustain it, even though it satisfies the three stated conditions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the entity possess intrinsic causal powers or an internal principle of unity, which would exclude entities like fields that are ontologically parasitic upon broader physical structures.
passage: It has long been supposed that the concept of substance may be analysed in terms of independent existence, subjecthood, and persistence through accidental change. One might observe that these three marks have served philosophers from Aristotle onward as the distinguishing features of what is most fundamental in the ontological order. Yet we should hesitate to accept this tripartite analysis without reservation. Suppose that in a region of otherwise empty space there obtains a magnetic field of considerable intensity. This field exists independently of any other particular entity in the relevant sense: it is not a mode or modification of some further thing. It serves as the subject of genuine predications, for we ascribe to it direction, magnitude, and temporal variation. Moreover, it persists through changes in these accidental features, maintaining its numerical identity even as its intensity waxes and wanes. Nevertheless, it appears doubtful that such a field constitutes a substance in any robust philosophical sense. The field seems constitutively dependent upon the spatial region and the physical laws that sustain it; it lacks an internal principle of unity or intrinsic causal power of the sort we ordinarily attribute to substances. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that independent existence, subjecthood, and accidental persistence do not jointly suffice to capture what we mean by substance.

definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C and E both occur, (ii) C temporally precedes E, and (iii) had C not occurred, E would not have occurred.
conditions: C and E both occur
C temporally precedes E
Had C not occurred, E would not have occurred
counterexample: Suppose that two assassins, acting entirely independently, each fire a shot at the very same moment, and both bullets strike the victim simultaneously, either one alone being sufficient to bring about death. Both shots occur and temporally precede the death. Had the first assassin not fired, the victim would still have died from the second bullet, so the counterfactual condition fails for the first shot, and symmetrically for the second. Yet we are strongly inclined to say that each shot caused the death.
missing_condition: The definition neglects cases of symmetric overdetermination, where multiple independently sufficient causes operate simultaneously. It lacks a condition addressing cases in which the effect is multiply determined by concurrent sufficient causes.
passage: The counterfactual analysis of causation has enjoyed considerable favour among philosophers who wish to provide a reductive account of the causal relation. According to this view, an event C causes an event E just in case both events occur, C precedes E in time, and it is true that had C not occurred, E would not have occurred. One might observe that this formulation captures a great many ordinary causal judgements with admirable economy. Yet we should hesitate to regard it as wholly adequate. Let us imagine a case in which two assassins, operating with complete independence of one another, each discharge a weapon at precisely the same instant, and both projectiles strike the intended victim simultaneously, either shot being sufficient on its own to produce death. Here both events occur and the temporal precedence condition is satisfied. However, the counterfactual condition fails for each shot individually, since the removal of either shot leaves the other to produce the very same effect. We are nevertheless strongly inclined to affirm that each shot caused the death. This case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis, for symmetric overdetermination generates circumstances in which genuine causes fail to satisfy the counterfactual requirement. What appears to be missing is a provision for cases in which the effect is multiply determined by concurrent and independently sufficient causes.

definition: A temporal instant is a moment if and only if (i) it has no temporal extension, (ii) it is the boundary between a past interval and a future interval, and (iii) every event occurring at that instant is simultaneous with every other event occurring at that instant.
conditions: The instant has no temporal extension
The instant is the boundary between a past interval and a future interval
Every event occurring at the instant is simultaneous with every other event occurring at it
counterexample: Consider a case in which, according to the special theory of relativity, two spatially separated observers each experience a durationless instant that lies at the boundary of their respective past and future intervals. For each observer, every event at that instant is simultaneous with every other event at that instant, relative to that observer's frame. Yet the two instants, though each satisfying all three conditions within its own frame, need not be the same moment, and events deemed simultaneous at one instant may fail to be simultaneous at the other. The definition thus identifies as a single well-defined moment what is in fact frame-dependent and observer-relative.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the frame of reference or to include a condition of frame-invariance, without which the notion of a moment and the simultaneity of events at that moment become relative rather than absolute.
passage: The analysis of temporal instants has occupied a central place in the philosophy of time, and it is natural to propose that a moment is simply a durationless point serving as the boundary between what has been and what is yet to come, at which all co-occurring events are simultaneous. One might observe that this characterisation has a pleasing simplicity and accords with our naive temporal intuitions. Yet we should hesitate to accept it without further qualification. Suppose that two observers, widely separated in space but each at rest in a different inertial frame, each experience a durationless instant that serves as the boundary between their respective past and future intervals. For each observer, every event at that instant is indeed simultaneous with every other event occurring then, relative to that observer's own frame. All three conditions of the proposed analysis are satisfied within each frame. Nevertheless, the two instants need not coincide, and events judged simultaneous at one observer's instant may be judged non-simultaneous at the other's. The definition thus yields what purports to be a single well-defined moment, yet the simultaneity upon which it relies is frame-dependent. This reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis: it fails to include a condition of frame-invariance, without which the concept of a moment dissolves into observer-relative fragments rather than designating an objective feature of temporal reality.

definition: Object A is numerically identical to object B if and only if (i) A and B share all the same properties at every time at which they exist, (ii) A and B occupy the same spatial region at every such time, and (iii) any change undergone by A is undergone by B and vice versa.
conditions: A and B share all the same properties at every time at which they exist
A and B occupy the same spatial region at every such time
Any change undergone by A is undergone by B and vice versa
counterexample: Suppose that a bronze statue and the lump of bronze composing it share all the same properties at every time, occupy the same spatial region at every time, and undergo all the same changes. Yet the statue could be destroyed by being flattened while the lump of bronze would persist, and the lump could be gradually replaced molecule by molecule while the statue endures. They thus differ in their modal properties, though they satisfy all three conditions of the definition at every actual time.
missing_condition: The definition omits modal properties, failing to require that A and B share the same dispositional and modal profiles, including what changes they could or could not survive.
