passage: One of the most venerable questions in metaphysics concerns the conditions under which two apparent objects are in fact numerically one and the same. It is tempting to propose that numerical identity obtains whenever two objects share all their properties at every time, co-occupy the same spatial region throughout their existence, and undergo precisely the same changes. One might observe that this formulation captures much of what we ordinarily mean when we say that A and B are really one thing rather than two. Yet we should hesitate to regard this analysis as complete. Let us imagine a bronze statue standing in a public square and the lump of bronze that constitutes it. At every moment of their coexistence, the statue and the lump share all the same intrinsic and relational properties, occupy the very same region of space, and undergo the very same alterations. All three conditions are satisfied without remainder. Nevertheless, the statue could be destroyed by a sharp blow that flattens it into a featureless disc, while the lump of bronze would persist through such a transformation. Conversely, if the bronze were gradually replaced molecule by molecule with a different alloy, the statue might endure while the original lump of bronze would cease to exist. The statue and the lump thus differ in what they could survive, even though they coincide in every actual respect. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it neglects the modal dimension of identity.

definition: A universal is an entity that (i) is predicable of many particular things, (ii) is wholly present in each of its instances, and (iii) exists only if it has at least one instance.
conditions: The entity is predicable of many particular things
The entity is wholly present in each of its instances
The entity exists only if it has at least one instance
counterexample: Consider a case in which the property of being a dinosaur is predicable of many particular things, was wholly present in each dinosaur when dinosaurs existed, and currently has no instances. According to the definition, this property does not now exist, since it has no present instances. Yet it appears that we can still meaningfully predicate it, reason about it, and distinguish it from other properties; the property seems to retain its identity and character despite being uninstantiated. The definition thus entails that a universal can blink in and out of existence as instances come and go, which sits uneasily with our understanding of universals as stable, recurring features of reality.
missing_condition: The definition lacks a condition permitting the existence of uninstantiated universals or distinguishing between a universal's being instantiated and its existing as an abstract entity available for predication.
passage: The question of what universals are and under what conditions they exist has been a source of persistent disagreement among metaphysicians. A natural proposal holds that a universal is an entity predicable of many particulars, wholly present in each instance, and existent only in so far as it is instantiated. One might observe that this view has the virtue of tying universals firmly to the concrete world, avoiding the extravagance of a realm of uninstantiated abstracta. Yet we should hesitate before embracing this analysis without reservation. Suppose we consider the property of being a dinosaur. This property was predicable of many particular organisms during the Mesozoic era and was wholly present in each individual dinosaur. At present, however, it has no instances. The definition therefore entails that this property does not now exist. Yet it appears that we can still meaningfully predicate it of past creatures, reason about its features, and distinguish it from the property of being a mammal. The property seems to retain its identity and its distinctive character despite its current lack of instances. The analysis thus implies that universals may blink in and out of existence as their instances arise and perish, a consequence that sits uneasily with the supposition that universals are stable, recurring features of reality. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis.

definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S does not involve a logical contradiction, (ii) S is consistent with the laws of nature, and (iii) there exists some conceivable set of initial conditions from which S could arise.
conditions: S does not involve a logical contradiction
S is consistent with the laws of nature
There exists some conceivable set of initial conditions from which S could arise
counterexample: Suppose that S is the state of affairs in which a fair coin, tossed at the beginning of the universe, lands heads on every one of an infinite sequence of tosses. This state of affairs involves no logical contradiction, is consistent with the laws of nature (each individual toss landing heads is physically permissible), and there exist conceivable initial conditions from which such a sequence could commence. Yet the probability of this outcome is zero in the measure-theoretic sense, and one might reasonably deny that a probability-zero event is genuinely possible in any substantive sense, even though it satisfies all three conditions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a condition requiring that S have a non-zero probability or non-negligible measure, thereby admitting as possible states of affairs that, though logically and physically consistent, have vanishing likelihood.
passage: It is common to analyse possibility in terms of logical consistency, conformity with natural law, and the conceivability of appropriate initial conditions. One might observe that this analysis has the merit of providing clear and individually plausible criteria. A state of affairs that contradicts no logical truth, violates no law of nature, and could in principle arise from some set of antecedent circumstances would seem, on this account, to be genuinely possible. Yet we should hesitate to accept this analysis as fully adequate. Let us imagine the state of affairs in which a fair coin, tossed at the dawn of the universe, lands heads on every one of an infinite succession of tosses. No logical contradiction is involved, for each outcome taken individually is consistent with the rules of logic. The sequence is consistent with the laws of nature, since nothing in physics forbids a coin from landing heads on any given toss. And there exist conceivable initial conditions from which such a sequence might commence. All three conditions of the definition are thus satisfied. Nevertheless, the probability of this outcome is zero in the measure-theoretic sense, and it seems reasonable to deny that an event of probability zero is possible in any robust or substantive sense. The proposed analysis is therefore insufficient: it admits as possible states of affairs that, though logically and nomologically consistent, have a vanishing likelihood of obtaining.

definition: An object O persists through time if and only if (i) O exists at more than one moment, (ii) O maintains a continuous spatiotemporal trajectory between those moments, and (iii) O retains at least some of its essential properties throughout.
conditions: O exists at more than one moment
O maintains a continuous spatiotemporal trajectory between those moments
O retains at least some of its essential properties throughout
counterexample: Consider a case in which a watch is carefully disassembled, its parts shipped to a distant city in separate containers over the course of several weeks, and then reassembled from exactly those parts into the very same configuration. The watch exists at the time of disassembly and at the time of reassembly, it retains all its essential properties (its parts, structure, and function), yet its spatiotemporal trajectory is discontinuous: for a period, there is no single connected object traversing a path through space and time. We are nevertheless strongly inclined to say that the watch persists through the interval of disassembly.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for persistence through temporal gaps in spatiotemporal continuity, neglecting cases in which an object's identity is preserved by continuity of composition and structure rather than by an unbroken trajectory.
passage: The question of what it is for an object to persist through time has exercised metaphysicians considerably, and a familiar proposal holds that persistence requires existence at more than one moment, a continuous spatiotemporal trajectory, and the retention of essential properties. One might observe that these conditions capture a wide range of ordinary cases: a stone on a hillside, a river flowing through a valley, or a ship sailing across the sea. Yet we should hesitate to regard this analysis as fully satisfactory. Suppose that a fine watch is carefully disassembled by a skilled horologist, its individual components placed in separate containers and shipped by different routes to a distant city. Over the course of several weeks, there is no single connected object tracing a continuous path through space and time; the watch, as a unified entity, has no spatiotemporal trajectory during this interval. Upon arrival, the parts are reassembled into precisely the same configuration, yielding a watch that retains all of its essential properties: the same components, the same structure, the same function. The watch exists at the earlier moment and at the later moment and retains its essential properties throughout, yet the condition of spatiotemporal continuity is violated. We are nevertheless inclined to affirm that the reassembled watch is the very same object. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it demonstrates that persistence may be grounded in continuity of composition and structure rather than in an unbroken spatiotemporal path.

definition: An object X is a proper part of an object Y if and only if (i) every region occupied by X is also occupied by Y, (ii) there exists some region occupied by Y that is not occupied by X, and (iii) X contributes to the causal powers of Y.
conditions: Every region occupied by X is also occupied by Y
There exists some region occupied by Y that is not occupied by X
X contributes to the causal powers of Y
counterexample: Suppose that a vestigial organ, such as the human appendix on a certain understanding of it, occupies a region entirely within the body, the body occupies regions the organ does not, and yet the organ contributes nothing to the causal powers of the organism: it performs no physiological function, has no effect on behaviour, and its removal leaves the organism's causal profile unchanged. The organ satisfies the first two conditions but fails the third. Yet we are strongly inclined to say that the appendix is a proper part of the body.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires a causal contribution, when proper parthood is a purely mereological relation that depends only on spatial inclusion and non-identity, not on functional or causal relevance.
passage: The concept of proper parthood is central to mereological inquiry, and it is tempting to supplement the purely spatial conditions with a requirement of causal contribution. On such an analysis, X is a proper part of Y just in case X is spatially included in Y, Y extends beyond X, and X contributes to the causal powers of Y. One might observe that this formulation has the advantage of distinguishing genuine parts from mere spatial inclusions that bear no functional relation to the whole. Yet we should hesitate to accept the causal condition as necessary. Let us imagine a vestigial organ residing within a human body, occupying a region entirely contained within the body's boundaries. The body, in turn, occupies many regions the organ does not, so the first two conditions are plainly satisfied. However, suppose the organ performs no physiological function whatsoever: it makes no contribution to the organism's behaviour, health, or causal powers, and its surgical removal leaves the organism's causal profile entirely unchanged. The third condition is therefore not met. Yet it seems manifestly clear that this vestigial organ is a proper part of the body in which it resides. We do not ordinarily deny parthood to an organ simply because it has lost its function. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis: proper parthood is a mereological relation grounded in spatial inclusion and distinctness, not in causal or functional relevance.

definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in the actual world, (ii) the negation of P is inconceivable, and (iii) P can be known to be true a priori.
conditions: P is true in the actual world
The negation of P is inconceivable
P can be known to be true a priori
counterexample: Suppose that the proposition 'water is H2O' is true in the actual world and is necessarily true (being true in all possible worlds, if one accepts Kripkean necessity). However, its negation is perfectly conceivable: one can coherently imagine a substance that looks, tastes, and behaves like water but has a different chemical composition. Moreover, the proposition cannot be known a priori, since it required empirical chemical investigation to establish. The proposition thus fails conditions (ii) and (iii) while being necessarily true, showing that the conditions are not necessary for necessary truth.
missing_condition: The definition conflates metaphysical necessity with conceivability and aprioricity, failing to recognise that there are necessary a posteriori truths whose negations are conceivable.
passage: Among the most important questions in modal metaphysics is the question of what it is for a proposition to be necessarily true. A natural proposal holds that a proposition is necessarily true just in case it is actually true, its negation is inconceivable, and it is knowable a priori. One might observe that this analysis captures a familiar class of necessary truths: the truths of logic and mathematics, whose negations do indeed seem inconceivable and whose truth is discoverable by reason alone. Yet we should hesitate to regard this analysis as exhaustive. Consider the proposition that water is composed of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. This proposition is true in the actual world, and there are strong grounds for holding it to be necessarily true, since the essential nature of a substance fixes what it is across all possible circumstances. Nevertheless, the negation of this proposition is perfectly conceivable: one can coherently imagine a liquid that exhibits all the superficial characteristics of water yet possesses a quite different molecular structure. Furthermore, the proposition was established not by a priori reflection but by empirical chemical investigation. The proposition therefore fails both the inconceivability condition and the aprioricity condition, even though it is, by hypothesis, necessarily true. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that necessity cannot be reduced to conceivability and aprioricity without excluding an important class of necessary truths discovered through experience.

definition: An object O undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) O possesses property P at time t1, (ii) O lacks property P at a later time t2, and (iii) O is numerically the same object at t1 and t2.
conditions: O possesses property P at time t1
O lacks property P at a later time t2
O is numerically the same object at t1 and t2
counterexample: Consider a case in which Socrates possesses the property of being taller than Theaetetus at time t1. At a later time t2, Theaetetus has grown, and Socrates, without undergoing any intrinsic alteration whatsoever, now lacks the property of being taller than Theaetetus. Socrates is numerically the same individual at both times. All three conditions are satisfied, yet Socrates has not undergone any genuine change; it is Theaetetus who has changed, and Socrates has merely come to stand in a different relation to him through no alteration of his own.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the property P be intrinsic to O rather than merely relational, thus admitting mere Cambridge changes as instances of genuine change.
passage: The analysis of change has been a central preoccupation of metaphysics since antiquity. A straightforward proposal holds that an object undergoes genuine change whenever it possesses some property at one time, lacks that property at a later time, and remains numerically the same entity throughout. One might observe that this captures the obvious cases: a leaf changing colour, a pond freezing, or a person ageing. Yet we should hesitate to accept this analysis without qualification. Suppose that at an earlier time Socrates is taller than the young Theaetetus. As the years pass, Theaetetus grows considerably, and at a later time Socrates is no longer taller than him. Socrates is numerically the same individual throughout; he possessed the property of being taller than Theaetetus and subsequently lacked it. All three conditions of the analysis are met. Nevertheless, it appears quite wrong to say that Socrates has undergone genuine change in this respect. Socrates has suffered no intrinsic alteration; his height, his constitution, and his internal states have remained as they were. It is Theaetetus who has changed, and Socrates has merely come to stand in a different comparative relation through no doing of his own. The proposed analysis thus admits mere Cambridge changes, as they are sometimes called, as instances of genuine change, revealing its insufficiency. What is wanted is a further condition requiring that the property in question be intrinsic to the object.

definition: A feature F is a genuine property of an object O if and only if (i) F is predicable of O, (ii) F contributes to the causal powers of O, and (iii) F is intrinsic to O rather than dependent on O's relations to other things.
conditions: F is predicable of O
F contributes to the causal powers of O
F is intrinsic to O rather than dependent on O's relations to other things
counterexample: Suppose that the property of having a certain weight is considered. Weight is predicable of a physical object and contributes to its causal powers — a heavier object exerts a greater gravitational pull and resists acceleration more strongly. Yet weight depends on the gravitational field in which the object is situated: the same object weighs less on the Moon than on the Earth. Weight is therefore not intrinsic to the object but relational, dependent on the object's position relative to a gravitational source. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, weight is a genuine property of the object; we measure it, predicate it, and rely upon it in causal explanations.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously excludes relational properties from the class of genuine properties, when many genuine and causally efficacious properties — such as weight, velocity, and temperature — are constitutively relational.
passage: The question of what constitutes a genuine property of an object has been debated with considerable vigour. One proposal holds that a genuine property must be predicable of the object, must contribute to its causal powers, and must be intrinsic rather than relational. One might observe that this analysis captures many central cases: the mass of a body, the charge of a particle, and the chemical composition of a substance are all predicable, causally efficacious, and intrinsic. Yet we should hesitate to accept the intrinsicality condition as necessary for genuine propertyhood. Consider the property of weight. Weight is predicable of any physical object and contributes manifestly to its causal powers: a heavier object resists acceleration more effectively, exerts greater force upon a scale, and behaves differently in a variety of physical interactions. Yet weight is not intrinsic to the object; it depends constitutively upon the gravitational field in which the object is situated. The same object possesses a different weight on the Moon than on the Earth, not because of any intrinsic change but because of its altered relation to a gravitational source. The third condition is thus unsatisfied. Yet weight is indisputably a genuine property: we measure it with precision, predicate it without hesitation, and invoke it in causal explanations of the most fundamental kind. This case reveals the insufficiency of the proposed analysis, for it shows that genuine properties may be constitutively relational.
